dax: Total Sales = SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue by summing every value in the SalesAmount column of the Sales table. It responds fully to whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, or report-level filters will all slice the result automatically, with no explicit filters defined inside the measure itself. Because it's a simple column aggregation, it's fast and implicit context propagation handles most use cases cleanly. The key watch-out is that any blank or NULL values in SalesAmount are silently ignored by SUM, which is usually fine, but if blanks represent missing data rather than true zeros, your totals could be understated without any visible warning.

dax: Order Count = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderNumber] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique sales orders in the current filter context - for example, orders placed in a selected region, time period, or product category. It does this by scanning the OrderNumber column in the Sales table and returning the count of distinct values after all active filters have been applied. One important watch-out: if OrderNumber contains blanks, DISTINCTCOUNT will count that blank as one additional distinct value, which can silently inflate your order count - use DISTINCTCOUNTNOBLANK if that's a concern in your data.

dax: Total Quantity = SUM ( Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure sums every value in the Quantity column of the Sales table to produce total units sold. It respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, or other filters - so it automatically scopes to the relevant product, region, date, or any other dimension in view. The calculation is straightforward with no iteration or time intelligence involved. The one watch-out is that if Quantity contains blanks or nulls, SUM silently ignores them, which is usually fine but can mask data quality issues where a missing value should arguably be zero or trigger an alert.

dax: Active Customers = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique customers who appear in the Sales table - in plain business terms, it tells you how many distinct customers made at least one purchase in whatever context is being evaluated. It works by scanning the CustomerKey column in Sales after all active filters (from slicers, rows, columns, or other measures) have been applied, returning a count of non-duplicate, non-blank values. The key watch-out is that it only reflects customers present in the Sales table, so a customer who exists in a Customer dimension but has never purchased will never be counted - and if CustomerKey contains blanks, DISTINCTCOUNT silently excludes them, which can mask data quality issues.

dax: Average Unit Price = AVERAGE ( Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates the simple arithmetic mean of the Unit Price column across all rows in the Sales table that are visible in the current filter context - for example, by product, region, or time period as selected in a report. It works by summing every UnitPrice value in the filtered Sales table and dividing by the count of those rows, which is exactly what AVERAGE does natively in DAX. One critical watch-out is that this is a row-count-weighted average, not a revenue-weighted average - if a product was sold in many small transactions at a low price and a few large transactions at a high price, the result will skew toward the low-price transactions and may not reflect true average realized price; use a measure like [Total Revenue] / [Total Quantity] if volume-weighted pricing is the business intent.

dax: Sales YTD = TOTALYTD ( [Total Sales], 'Date'[Date] )
explanation: This measure gives you a year-to-date (YTD) total of sales - a running tally that restarts each January and adds up every day's sales from January 1st up to the latest date you're looking at. The TOTALYTD function does the heavy lifting: it quietly rewrites the date filter so that, no matter which single month or day a visual is showing, the calculation reaches back to the start of the year and sums everything in between. For this to work, your model needs a proper Date table - marked as a date table, with one row per day and no gaps - because TOTALYTD walks along that continuous calendar to find where the year begins. The key watch-out is that the year is assumed to end on December 31st by default, so if your business runs on a fiscal year that ends on, say, June 30th, you must pass the optional year-end argument ("6/30"); forget it, and the total resets on the wrong day without ever throwing an error.

dax: Sales PY = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR ( 'Date'[Date] ) )
explanation: This measure pulls up last year's sales for whatever period you're currently viewing, giving an instant prior-year comparison. CALCULATE is used to swap the current dates for the matching dates one year earlier, and SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR is the function that builds that shifted-back range - if you're looking at March 2024, it quietly hands back March 2023 and totals sales over those dates instead. Like most time-intelligence, it depends on a clean, continuous Date table that's been marked as a date table in the model. The key watch-out is how it fails: if the Date table has missing days or its link to the sales data is broken, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR returns blank rather than an error - and a blank looks exactly like a period that genuinely had no sales last year, so the problem can hide in plain sight.

dax: Sales YoY % = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales] - [Sales PY], [Sales PY] )
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year percentage change in sales, expressing how much current-period revenue has grown or declined relative to the same period last year. It subtracts the prior-year sales figure from the current-year figure, then divides by the prior year using DIVIDE - which safely returns BLANK instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The prior-year value ([Sales PY]) almost certainly relies on SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or a similar time-intelligence function, meaning the measure depends on a properly marked, contiguous date table connected to your fact data. The key watch-out is that if [Sales PY] returns BLANK rather than zero for periods with no prior-year data (such as the first year in your dataset), DIVIDE will return BLANK too, which looks clean in visuals but can silently distort totals or aggregations if downstream measures treat BLANK as zero.

dax: Sales MTD = TOTALMTD ( [Total Sales], 'Date'[Date] )
explanation: This measure calculates the running cumulative sales total from the first day of the current month up to and including the last visible date in the current filter context - essentially a month-to-date sales figure. Under the hood, TOTALMTD is syntactic sugar for CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESMTD('Date'[Date])), which overrides the date filter to expand it from the month's start to the current date, then evaluates [Total Sales] within that modified context. The key watch-out is that your Date table must be marked as an official date table (or at least be contiguous with no gaps), because if the table isn't marked, Power BI won't correctly identify the month boundary, and if you have missing dates, the "first of month" anchor can behave unpredictably. Also note that in a report with no date filter applied at all, the measure will aggregate across the entire month of the latest date in your Date table, which can surprise users expecting a current-period default.

dax: Rolling 3M Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], DATESINPERIOD ( 'Date'[Date], MAX ( 'Date'[Date] ), -3, MONTH ) )
explanation: This measure adds up sales over the most recent three months - a rolling 3-month total that's handy for smoothing out the month-to-month bumpiness in a trend line. The work is done by DATESINPERIOD, which builds a date range starting from the latest date you're looking at (MAX('Date'[Date])) and stretching exactly three months backwards; CALCULATE then swaps that three-month window in place of the normal date filter before summing Total Sales. Because the window is anchored to the latest date currently in view, it slides along automatically as you move through time. The key watch-out is that the anchor is the latest date in context, not the latest date that actually has sales - so if your calendar table runs into the future (common, since date tables are often built years ahead), the window quietly drifts forward and scoops up empty months, making the total look smaller than it should; the fix is to cap the calendar at the last date with real data, or anchor on the last date in the Sales table instead.

dax: Gross Margin % = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales] - [Total Cost], [Total Sales] )
explanation: This measure calculates the gross margin percentage - the share of revenue remaining after deducting direct costs, expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.35 for 35%). It simply subtracts the [Total Cost] measure from [Total Sales], divides by [Total Sales], and inherits whatever filter context is active at evaluation time (slice by product, region, date, etc.), so it dynamically reflects the margin for whatever subset of data is in scope. DIVIDE is used instead of the / operator specifically to return BLANK rather than an error when [Total Sales] is zero, which keeps visuals clean. The key watch-out is that this measure is only as reliable as its two dependencies - if [Total Sales] or [Total Cost] have mismatched filter contexts (for example, one ignores a slicer the other respects), the resulting percentage will be silently wrong, so always validate both base measures behave consistently under the same filters.

dax: Sales per Customer = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales], [Active Customers] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per active customer, giving a sense of customer productivity or monetisation efficiency. It simply divides the Total Sales measure by the Active Customers measure, both of which evaluate under whatever filter context is currently applied - by region, time period, product, etc. - so the ratio always reflects the slice in view. The key watch-out is that the result is only as meaningful as the definition of "Active Customers": if that measure uses a loose or inconsistent activity threshold (e.g., anyone who ever purchased vs. purchased in the last 90 days), the per-customer figure can be misleading, and any mismatch in the date ranges or filters used by the two underlying measures will silently produce a nonsensical ratio.

dax: % of Total Sales = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales], CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALL ( Product ) ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates each product's (or product slice's) share of the overall total sales, expressed as a proportion. It does this by dividing the current context's Total Sales by a version of Total Sales where ALL filters on the Product table have been explicitly removed using CALCULATE and ALL, leaving any other active filters - such as date, region, or customer - intact. The DIVIDE function safely handles division by zero, returning blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero or empty.

The key watch-out is that ALL only strips filters from the Product table, so if your report has filters applied on other tables that happen to relate back to Product (e.g., a product category slicer sitting on a separate Category table), those filters will not be removed and the denominator won't represent a true grand total - leaving you with percentages that don't sum to 100%.

dax: Return Rate = DIVIDE ( [Returned Quantity], [Total Quantity] )
explanation: This measure calculates the proportion of sold units that were returned, expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.05 = 5% return rate), giving a quick read on product or channel quality. It simply divides two existing measures - returned quantity over total quantity - inheriting whatever filter context is active, so it will automatically slice correctly by product, date, region, or any other dimension in your report. DIVIDE is used instead of the raw / operator, which means a zero denominator returns BLANK rather than an error, keeping visuals clean. The key watch-out is that the result is only as meaningful as how [Returned Quantity] and [Total Quantity] are defined - if they draw from different fact tables or use mismatched date logic (e.g., one filters on order date and the other on return date), the ratio will be misleading or silently wrong.

dax: Average Order Value = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales], [Order Count] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per order - a standard efficiency metric for understanding customer spend. It works by dividing two existing measures: Total Sales (typically a sum of revenue) and Order Count (typically a distinct count of orders), with DIVIDE handling any zero-denominator scenario by returning BLANK rather than an error, and both measures inherit whatever filter context is active (date range, product, region, etc.). The key watch-out is that the result is entirely dependent on how Order Count is defined - if it counts order lines rather than distinct order IDs, the "average" will be diluted and misleading, so you need to confirm that measure counts at the correct granularity before trusting the output.

dax: Sales (Line Level) = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates revenue the careful way - line by line - by multiplying each sale's quantity by its unit price and then adding all those line totals together. It uses SUMX, which is an 'iterator' function: that just means it walks through the Sales table one row at a time, works out Quantity * UnitPrice for that single row, and only adds the results up at the end. The row-by-row approach matters because multiplying the grand-total quantity by the grand-total price gives the wrong answer - in plain terms, adding up each line's price is not the same as multiplying the totals together. The key watch-out is about where the price lives: if UnitPrice isn't a column sitting directly on the Sales table but instead comes from a separate Products table, you have to fetch it inside the loop with RELATED(Product[UnitPrice]), otherwise the formula errors or quietly returns blank.

dax: Weighted Avg Price = DIVIDE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure works out the average selling price the honest way - weighting by how many units sold at each price rather than just averaging the price tags. Under the hood it divides total revenue by total units: SUMX goes row by row to add up Quantity * UnitPrice (the revenue), SUM adds up the quantities, and DIVIDE protects against an awkward divide-by-zero by returning blank. Because it's quantity-weighted, a product that sells in huge volumes pulls the average toward its price, which is usually exactly what you want. The key watch-out is that the revenue on top and the quantity on the bottom both read from the same filtered Sales table, so if the model accidentally duplicates rows (for example through a many-to-many relationship), both numbers inflate together - the average still looks believable, which neatly hides the underlying data problem.

dax: Total Margin = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) )
explanation: Total Margin calculates the gross profit in dollars by multiplying each row's quantity by its per-unit margin (price minus cost) and summing the results across the Sales table. SUMX iterates row by row, so the subtraction and multiplication happen at the transaction level before aggregation - this is important because it produces an accurate result even when unit price and unit cost vary across individual rows, unlike a simpler approach using pre-aggregated column totals. The measure respects whatever filter context is active (date slicer, product selection, etc.), so it slices correctly without any extra CALCULATE needed. The key watch-out is that if UnitCost is missing or blank for certain rows, those rows contribute their full revenue as margin rather than zero, silently overstating profitability - always validate cost coverage before trusting the number.

dax: Largest Line Total = MAXX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest line-level revenue amount across all sales transactions visible in the current filter context. It does this by iterating row-by-row over the Sales table, computing Quantity × UnitPrice for each row, and returning the maximum of those calculated values - making it a row-context operation driven by MAXX rather than a simple column aggregation. The key watch-out is that because the multiplication happens at row level during iteration, it correctly handles cases where neither column alone would give you the right answer - but if Sales[Quantity] or Sales[UnitPrice] can be blank or negative (e.g. returns, adjustments), the result may be misleading, and you should confirm whether those rows should be excluded before relying on this figure.

dax: High-Value Customers = COUNTROWS ( FILTER ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), [Total Sales] > 10000 ) )
explanation: This measure counts how many customers are 'high value' - defined here as anyone whose sales come to more than $10,000 - within whatever slice of the report you're currently viewing. It works in two steps: VALUES(Customer[CustomerKey]) gets the list of customers currently in view, then FILTER walks down that list and keeps only the ones whose Total Sales clears the $10,000 bar, and finally COUNTROWS counts the survivors. A subtle but important detail is that Total Sales is recalculated for each customer as the loop checks them, using that one customer as the context. The key watch-out is that the $10,000 test is applied to each customer's sales within the current view, not their lifetime spend - so a customer worth $12,000 overall but only $8,000 in the region you've filtered to won't be counted; if you want eligibility based on lifetime value regardless of the slice, you'd measure them under ALL(Customer) or use a pre-built customer-segment column.

dax: Product Rank by Sales = RANKX ( ALL ( Product[ProductName] ), [Total Sales], , DESC )
explanation: This measure ranks your products by sales, handing the best-selling product a 1 and counting upward toward the worst. It relies on RANKX: first ALL(Product[ProductName]) temporarily ignores any filter on the product name so the ranking can see the full list of products, then RANKX works out each product's Total Sales and sorts them highest to lowest to find where the current product lands. The important thing is what ALL does and doesn't do here - it only clears the filter on product name, nothing else. The key watch-out follows from that: any other filters still in play - a region slicer, a date range, a category - are left untouched, so the rank really means 'best seller given what's currently on screen,' not an all-time leaderboard; for a fixed global ranking you'd clear those filters too.

dax: Top 5 Products Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], TOPN ( 5, ALL ( Product[ProductName] ), [Total Sales], DESC ) )
explanation: This measure adds up the sales of just your five best-selling products, ignoring whatever product filters happen to be on the report - useful as a benchmark for 'how much do our top performers bring in.' TOPN does the selecting: ALL(Product[ProductName]) lets it look across the full product list rather than only the filtered ones, it ranks them by Total Sales from high to low, and keeps the top five as a little virtual table that CALCULATE then uses to limit the sum. The result is dynamic rather than hard-coded - the five products are chosen on the fly. The key watch-out is that 'top 5' is recalculated under whatever non-product filters are active, like a region or a date range, so the five products can change as you slice the report; if you need a fixed, global top-five list that never shifts, you'd also strip those outer filters with ALL.

dax: Customer Sales Rank = RANKX ( ALLSELECTED ( Customer[CustomerName] ), [Total Sales], , DESC, DENSE )
explanation: This measure ranks each customer by their sales, but only against the other customers the user has actually chosen to look at. The clever part is ALLSELECTED(Customer[CustomerName]): it brings back the set of customers as defined by the slicers and page filters, while ignoring the row-by-row filtering that a table visual would otherwise impose - so each row gets ranked against its visible peers rather than just against itself. RANKX then scores every customer by Total Sales and assigns a 'dense' rank, meaning tied customers share a number and there are no gaps afterward. The key watch-out is that because ALLSELECTED honors the user's slicer and cross-filter choices, the pool of customers - and therefore the rank numbers - shifts every time someone changes a selection; if stakeholders expect a fixed, global rank that never moves, use ALL(Customer[CustomerName]) instead.

dax: Is Top 10 Product = IF ( [Product Rank by Sales] <= 10, 1, 0 )
explanation: This measure is a simple yes/no flag - it returns 1 when the product in view is among the top 10 by sales, and 0 when it isn't. It does that by calling another measure, [Product Rank by Sales] (which itself uses RANKX), and checking whether that rank is 10 or better; because the rank is recalculated inside whatever filters are active, the flag always reflects the products currently on screen. You can then use this 1/0 as a slicer, a visual filter, or drop it inside CALCULATE to isolate top-10 performance. The key watch-out is that 'top 10' isn't fixed - if the report is already filtered to one category or region, the ranking restarts among only those products, so the very same product can flip from a 1 to a 0 as the user changes slicers, which may surprise stakeholders expecting a stable list.

dax: Region Rank = RANKX ( ALL ( 'Geography'[Region] ), [Total Sales], , DESC )
explanation: This measure ranks each region by its Total Sales figure from highest (1) to lowest across all regions. It works by first stripping any region filter from the Geography table using ALL, so every region is always included in the ranking pool regardless of what's selected in a visual or slicer, then iterating over that full list and evaluating Total Sales for each region to determine relative position. The DESC argument means the highest-selling region gets rank 1, which is the default behavior anyway, so omitting it would produce the same result. The key watch-out is tie handling: by default RANKX uses SKIP, meaning tied regions share the same rank and the next rank is skipped (e.g., two regions tied at rank 2 means no rank 3 appears), so if your business requires dense ranking you'll need to explicitly pass DENSE as the fifth argument.

dax: Sales (All Products) = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALL ( Product ) )
explanation: This measure always shows the sales total for every product, even when the report has been filtered down to a single product or a handful. It does that with CALCULATE and ALL(Product): ALL(Product) strips away any filter coming from the Product table - slicers, table rows, chart axes - before Total Sales is calculated, while filters from other tables (like a date range or a store) stay switched on. A classic use is the denominator in a '% of all products' calculation, where you want the all-products figure on the bottom but still want it to honor the date the user picked. The key watch-out is that ALL(Product) only frees the Product table itself - if you have a separate Category or Brand table joined in that's also being filtered, that filter still applies, and your 'all products' number can come out lower than you expect.

dax: Online Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Channel] = "Online" )
explanation: This measure isolates revenue (or whatever [Total Sales] aggregates) to only the Online sales channel, giving analysts a clean slice of digital revenue without manually filtering every visual. It works by injecting a hard filter on the Channel column directly inside CALCULATE, which overwrites any conflicting filter on that column already in the filter context - so even if a slicer or row context says "All Channels," this measure forces Channel to "Online" only. The key watch-out is that CALCULATE's filter argument here uses a Boolean expression, which under the hood becomes FILTER(ALL(Sales[Channel]), Sales[Channel] = "Online") - meaning it replaces, not intersects with, existing Channel filters, so if a user selects "Retail" in a slicer, the measure still returns Online figures rather than going blank, which can surprise end users expecting cross-filter behavior.

dax: Sales Excl Returns = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[OrderStatus] <> "Returned" )
explanation:
This measure calculates net revenue by excluding any transactions marked as returned, giving a cleaner view of genuine sales performance. It works by injecting an additional filter into the current filter context via CALCULATE, overriding or appending to whatever filters already exist so that only rows where OrderStatus is not "Returned" contribute to the [Total Sales] base measure. The mechanics are straightforward - no iteration or time intelligence involved, just a simple column filter applied before [Total Sales] evaluates.

The key watch-out is that this filter *adds to* the existing context rather than replacing it, so if a report-level or slicer filter already restricts OrderStatus to a subset that happens to include "Returned," CALCULATE will intersect those filters - but if nothing else is filtering OrderStatus, the measure works as expected. More critically, this approach assumes returns are captured as a distinct status value rather than as negative quantity rows or a separate returns table; if your data model handles reversals differently, this measure will silently miss them.

dax: Sales (Keep High Value) = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( Sales[SalesAmount] > 1000 ) )
explanation: This measure totals sales but counts only the individual transactions worth more than $1,000, while still respecting the filters already on the report. The interesting piece is KEEPFILTERS: normally a condition inside CALCULATE replaces any existing filter on that column, but KEEPFILTERS makes the new '> $1,000' rule layer on top of - intersect with - whatever filter is already there, so both have to be true. It's also worth knowing this test runs against the raw transaction rows in the Sales table, one row at a time, not against an already-summed figure. The key watch-out flows from those two facts: it's a row-level filter on raw data (so it behaves differently from a threshold on aggregated numbers), and without KEEPFILTERS the condition would have wiped out any incoming SalesAmount filter instead of narrowing it down.

dax: Category Share = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales], CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Product, Product[Category] ) ) )
explanation: This measure tells you what slice of its category's sales a given product (or selection) is responsible for - a within-category share. The denominator is where the trick is: CALCULATE recomputes Total Sales with ALLEXCEPT(Product, Product[Category]), which clears every filter on the Product table except the one on Category, so the bottom of the fraction always represents the whole category's sales no matter how the products are sliced; DIVIDE then guards against dividing by zero by returning blank. The top of the fraction is just the normal, fully-filtered Total Sales. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only lifts filters that sit on the Product table itself - if Category is being filtered from a separate dimension table, or from an outside slicer, that filter survives in the denominator and the share collapses to a meaningless 100%.

dax: Total Cost = SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] )
explanation: This measure sums every value in the TotalCost column of the Sales table to produce an aggregate cost figure for whatever slice of data is currently in view. It relies entirely on the surrounding filter context - slicers, row/column headers, and report-level filters all silently narrow which rows get summed, with no explicit filtering logic inside the measure itself. Because it's a simple column sum, it will return a grand total when no filters are applied, which can be misleadingly large if the Sales table contains returns, adjustments, or other offsetting rows that inflate the raw sum. Watch out for blank or null values in TotalCost - DAX treats blanks as zero in SUM, so missing cost data will be silently ignored rather than flagged, potentially understating true cost exposure.

dax: Total Profit = SUM ( Sales[Profit] )
explanation: This measure sums every value in the Profit column of the Sales table to give total profit in dollars (or whatever currency the column holds). It respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, or other filters - so it will automatically return the right subtotal for any combination of product, region, date, or other dimension you drop into your report. There is no explicit iterator or time intelligence at work; it is a straightforward aggregation that relies entirely on the relationship model to scope its results. The key watch-out is that if the Profit column is pre-calculated at the source (e.g., Revenue minus Cost baked in during ETL), any inconsistencies or nulls in that column will silently distort the total, so validate the source logic before trusting this number in executive reporting.

dax: Total Discount Amount = SUM ( Sales[DiscountAmount] )
explanation: This measure returns the total monetary value of discounts applied across all sales transactions in the current filter context. It works by simply summing every row in the DiscountAmount column of the Sales table, with Power BI's filter context automatically narrowing that sum based on any slicers, visual filters, or row/column headers in play. There are no time shifts, iterations, or complex context transitions involved - it's a straightforward aggregation. The key watch-out is that if DiscountAmount is stored as a positive number, it will report as a positive figure, which may conflict with how discounts are treated elsewhere in your P&L logic (e.g., as a deduction from revenue); always confirm the sign convention before using it in margin or net revenue calculations.

dax: Total Freight = SUM ( Sales[FreightCost] )
explanation: This measure sums every freight cost value in the Sales table's FreightCost column, giving you the total shipping expense for whatever slice of data is currently in view. It relies entirely on the surrounding filter context - slicers, rows, columns, and report-level filters all automatically narrow which rows get summed, so the number changes dynamically as users interact with the report. There are no explicit filters or iterations inside the measure itself, making it straightforward but fully dependent on the model's relationships to propagate filters correctly from related tables. The key watch-out is that any blank or null values in FreightCost are silently ignored by SUM, which is usually fine, but if blanks represent missing data rather than zero freight, your totals could be understated without any visible warning.

dax: Total Tax Amount = SUM ( Sales[TaxAmount] )
explanation: This measure totals the tax collected across all sales transactions in the current filter context. It works by simply aggregating the TaxAmount column in the Sales table, respecting whatever slicers, row labels, or report filters are active at the time of evaluation. Because it relies on a physical column rather than a calculated expression, it reflects pre-computed values stored in the data source, not a live recalculation of tax logic. The key watch-out is that if upstream ETL processes ever load incorrect or NULL tax amounts into Sales[TaxAmount], this measure will silently surface those bad values with no indication of an issue - always validate source data quality before trusting totals at any grain.

dax: Total Line Items = COUNTROWS ( Sales )
explanation: This measure counts the total number of transaction line items recorded in the Sales table, giving analysts a raw volume metric independent of any monetary value. It simply counts every visible row in Sales after whatever filters are currently active in the report - slicers, page filters, visual context - all apply automatically through standard filter context propagation. One watch-out: if your Sales table has duplicate or erroneous rows (e.g., from a flawed ETL load), COUNTROWS will count them faithfully without complaint, so a spike in this measure can indicate a data quality issue rather than genuine sales activity.

dax: Total Products = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[ProductKey] )
explanation: Counts the number of unique products that exist in the current filter context - for example, how many distinct products were sold in a selected category, region, or time period, depending on what slicers or report filters are active. It works by scanning the ProductKey column of the Product table and returning the count of non-duplicate, non-blank values after all active filters have been applied. The key watch-out is that this measure counts products visible in the filter context, so if ProductKey appears in a fact table column rather than the dimension, blank or missing keys from unmatched rows can silently inflate or deflate the count - always anchor DISTINCTCOUNT to the clean dimension table column, not a foreign key in a fact table.

dax: Total Customers = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Customer[CustomerKey] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique customers present in the current filter context - for example, by region, product category, or time period, depending on what slicers or visual filters are active. It works by scanning the CustomerKey column in the Customer table and returning a count of distinct values, automatically respecting whatever filters the report context applies. One key watch-out: if your data model has a many-to-many or ambiguous relationship between the Customer table and your fact tables, the measure may count customers in the Customer dimension who have no actual transactions, inflating the number beyond what your business defines as an "active" customer.

dax: Total Cities = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Geography[City] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique cities present in the Geography table, giving you a single figure representing how many distinct city values exist under whatever filter context is currently active - for example, a region slicer would narrow the count to only cities within that region. It uses DISTINCTCOUNT, which internally iterates the City column and tallies non-duplicate values, respecting any row-level or report-level filters automatically. One important watch-out: DISTINCTCOUNT includes a blank as a distinct value, so if your City column contains any nulls or empty strings, the count will be inflated by one - clean your data or add an explicit blank filter if that's a concern.

dax: Total Countries = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Geography[Country] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique countries present in the Geography table, giving you the distinct cardinality of the Country column under whatever filter context is active - for example, slicing by region or product category will reduce the count to only the countries relevant to that selection. It works by scanning the Country column after all filters have been applied and returning the number of non-duplicate values, so it naturally adapts to report slicers, row context in a matrix, or filters passed by other measures. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT includes blank as a countable value, so if your Country column contains any nulls or empty strings from incomplete data, the result will be inflated by one, potentially making you think you have more countries than you actually do.

dax: Total States = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Geography[StateProvince] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique states or provinces visible in the current filter context - useful for understanding geographic coverage or spread across whatever slice the user has selected (e.g., a specific country, sales region, or time period). It works by scanning the StateProvince column in the Geography table and returning the count of distinct, non-blank values after all active filters have been applied. One key watch-out: if your Geography table contains rows with a blank or null StateProvince - common with incomplete data or rows representing unassigned geography - DISTINCTCOUNT silently excludes that blank, so your count may appear lower than expected; use COUNTROWS(VALUES(Geography[StateProvince])) if you need to include or explicitly handle blanks.

dax: Total Categories = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[Category] )
explanation: This measure counts how many unique product categories exist in the current filter context - for example, how many distinct categories appear in a selected region, time period, or product segment. It uses DISTINCTCOUNT to scan the Category column and return the number of non-duplicate, non-blank values after all active slicers and filters have been applied. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT automatically ignores blank values, so if any products have a missing or null Category, those rows are silently excluded from the count - which can cause the result to appear lower than expected and mask data quality issues in your product dimension.

dax: Total Subcategories = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[Subcategory] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique product subcategories visible in the current filter context. It works by scanning the Subcategory column in the Product table and returning the count of distinct values after all active slicers, filters, and visual context have been applied. No iteration or time intelligence is involved - it's a straightforward aggregation that respects whatever filters are in play. The key watch-out is that if Subcategory contains blanks, DISTINCTCOUNT will include the blank as one distinct value, which can inflate your count by one and mislead users who assume every counted subcategory is a named, legitimate category.

dax: Average Sales Amount = AVERAGE ( Sales[SalesAmount] )
explanation: This measure calculates the mean transaction value across all sales records in scope, giving analysts a sense of the typical deal or order size. It works by summing every value in the SalesAmount column that survives the current filter context - slicers, row context from a visual, or any outer CALCULATE filters - then dividing by the count of those rows. One key watch-out: AVERAGE ignores blank values but includes zeros, so if your data uses zero as a placeholder for missing or cancelled sales, your average will be silently understated compared to a true average of legitimate transactions.

dax: Average Order Quantity = AVERAGE ( Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure calculates the mean number of units per sales order line, giving analysts a sense of typical transaction size across whatever slice of the data is in context. It works by summing all values in the Quantity column within the current filter context and dividing by the count of non-blank rows in that column - standard columnar aggregation with no iteration or time intelligence involved. The key watch-out is that AVERAGE operates at the row granularity of the Sales table, so if each row represents an order line rather than a complete order, the result reflects average line quantity, not average total order quantity - a distinction that matters when orders contain multiple line items and you actually want order-level averages.

dax: Average Discount Percent = AVERAGE ( Sales[DiscountPercent] )
explanation: This measure calculates the simple arithmetic mean of the discount percentage applied across individual sales line items, giving analysts a quick read on how aggressively discounts are being offered. It works by summing all values in the Sales[DiscountPercent] column visible in the current filter context and dividing by the count of non-blank rows - standard column aggregation with no explicit iteration or time intelligence involved. The key watch-out is that this is a row-level average, not a revenue-weighted one: a transaction worth $1 and one worth $1,000,000 contribute equally to the result, which can be deeply misleading when deal sizes vary significantly. If weighted average discount is what the business actually needs, you'll want to divide total discount amount by total undiscounted revenue instead.

dax: Average Freight Cost = AVERAGE ( Sales[FreightCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the mean freight cost per sales transaction by summing all FreightCost values in the visible rows and dividing by the count of those rows - a straightforward operational cost metric. It responds fully to whatever filter context is active (date slicers, region filters, product selections, etc.), so the average automatically recalculates to reflect only the rows that survive those filters. The key watch-out is that AVERAGE ignores blank values but includes zeros, so if missing freight costs are stored as 0 rather than blank, those rows will pull the average down and distort the result - always confirm how nulls are handled in the source data before trusting this figure.

dax: Average Profit = AVERAGE ( Sales[Profit] )
explanation: This measure calculates the mean profit per row across the Sales table, giving analysts a single figure representing typical transaction-level profitability. It works by summing all Profit values visible in the current filter context - whatever slicers, row/column filters, or report-level filters are active - and dividing by the count of those rows. The calculation is straightforward, but the key watch-out is that AVERAGE is sensitive to granularity: if the Sales table stores data at order-line level, the result is average line-item profit, not average order or average customer profit, so the business meaning shifts entirely depending on how the table is structured.

dax: Average Cost = AVERAGE ( Sales[TotalCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average cost per sales transaction row by summing all values in the TotalCost column and dividing by the number of rows. It responds fully to whatever filter context is active - slicers, visual filters, or row context from a matrix - so the result automatically narrows to the relevant subset of Sales rows. The key watch-out is that this averages at the row granularity of the Sales table, not at any higher level like product or customer; if rows represent line items rather than orders, the average reflects line-item cost, which can mislead if stakeholders expect an order-level or customer-level average.

dax: Max Sales Amount = MAX ( Sales[SalesAmount] )
explanation: This measure returns the single highest individual sales transaction amount recorded in the Sales table. It evaluates MAX over the SalesAmount column, respecting whatever filter context is active - so slicing by region, product, or date will automatically narrow the column values before finding the peak. There's no explicit iteration or row context involved; it's a straightforward column aggregation handed off to the storage engine. The watch-out is that MAX ignores blanks but is sensitive to data quality - one erroneous outlier row (e.g., a test entry with an inflated value) will silently dominate the result, so it's worth validating the source data before trusting this metric in reports.

dax: Min Sales Amount = MIN ( Sales[SalesAmount] )
explanation: This measure returns the single smallest individual transaction amount recorded in the Sales table. It scans every row of SalesAmount visible in the current filter context - sliced by whatever dimensions (date, product, region, etc.) are on the report - and surfaces the minimum value. Because MIN is a simple aggregator, it respects all active filters automatically, so the result changes correctly as users drill or cross-filter. The key watch-out is that MIN ignores blank rows but will return 0 if any transaction was recorded as zero, which can be misleading if zeros represent missing or erroneous data rather than genuine zero-value sales.

dax: Max Unit Price = MAX ( Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure returns the single highest unit price found across all rows of the Sales table that are visible in the current filter context - for example, the priciest product in a selected category or time period. It works by having the MAX function scan every value in the UnitPrice column and surface the largest one, with no iteration or row-by-row logic required since MAX operates natively over a column. If all rows are filtered out - say by a slicer combination that returns no sales - the measure returns BLANK rather than zero, which can silently distort visuals or calculations that depend on it downstream. Also worth noting: MAX reflects the highest price at which something *appeared in a sales transaction*, not necessarily the current or list price, so if your pricing data lives in a separate dimension, this measure may not tell the full story.

dax: Min Unit Price = MIN ( Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure returns the lowest unit price recorded across all rows in the Sales table that are visible in the current filter context - for example, the cheapest price for a selected product, region, or time period. It works by scanning the UnitPrice column and returning its minimum value, fully respecting whatever slicers, filters, or row/column context are active in the report. Because it operates directly on a stored column rather than a calculated one, it's fast and straightforward. The key watch-out is that if UnitPrice contains blanks, MIN silently ignores them, so the result reflects only rows with an actual value - which could be misleading if missing prices are meaningful in your data (e.g., complimentary or unpriced items that should arguably anchor the minimum at zero).

dax: Max Order Quantity = MAX ( Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure returns the single highest order quantity recorded across all rows in the Sales table that are visible in the current filter context - useful for spotting peak demand, outlier orders, or setting dynamic axis scales. It works by scanning the Quantity column and returning its maximum value, with whatever slicers, visual filters, or row/column selections are active automatically narrowing the rows considered. The aggregation respects all filter context naturally, so the result will shift correctly when a user filters by product, region, or time period. The key watch-out is that if Quantity contains blanks, MAX silently ignores them - which is usually fine, but if *all* visible rows are blank (e.g., a product with no sales in a selected period), the measure returns blank rather than zero, which can cause unexpected gaps in visuals or break downstream calculations that assume a numeric result.

dax: Min Order Quantity = MIN ( Sales[Quantity] )
explanation:
This measure returns the smallest single-line-item order quantity recorded in the Sales table, giving analysts a quick read on the minimum units ever sold in a single transaction row. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active - slicing by product, region, date, or any other dimension will narrow the rows MIN scans, so the result always reflects the minimum within the current visual or slicer selection. Under the hood, MIN simply walks the Quantity column in the filtered Sales table and returns the lowest non-blank numeric value.

The key watch-out is that MIN ignores blank values entirely, so if Quantity is blank (rather than zero) for certain rows - perhaps due to returns, cancellations, or ETL gaps - those rows are silently excluded and the true floor of your data may be misrepresented; always validate that blanks and zeros are handled intentionally in your data model before trusting this measure for operational decisions.

dax: Unique Order Dates = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderDate] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of distinct calendar dates on which at least one order was placed, giving a sense of trading-day activity rather than order volume. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active - slicers, row context in a matrix, or report-level filters - so the result narrows automatically when you filter by product, region, or time period. Mechanically, it scans the OrderDate column in the Sales table and returns the count of unique values after duplicates are removed. The key watch-out is that if OrderDate stores a datetime with a time component rather than a pure date, two orders on the same calendar day at different times will be counted as two distinct values, inflating the result - so confirm the column is stored as a date type or truncated to date granularity before trusting the number.

dax: Unique Ship Dates = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[ShipDate] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of distinct calendar dates on which at least one order was shipped, giving a quick sense of shipping activity density across a time period. It operates directly on the Sales table's ShipDate column, and any filters already in the evaluation context - from slicers, rows, or columns in your report - automatically narrow which rows are considered before the distinct count is applied. One important watch-out: if ShipDate contains blank values (e.g., unshipped or pending orders), DISTINCTCOUNT will include that blank as one additional "distinct value," potentially inflating your count by one - use DISTINCTCOUNTNOBLANK if you want to exclude nulls from the tally.

dax: Total Sales Territories = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Geography[SalesTerritory] )
explanation: This measure counts how many unique sales territories exist in the Geography table, giving you the distinct number of territory groupings active in the current filter context. It works by scanning the SalesTerritory column and returning the count of non-duplicate, non-blank values - so if a report is filtered to a specific region or country, only the territories visible under that filter are counted. One key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT silently ignores blank values, so if any rows in Geography[SalesTerritory] are null or empty, those territories won't be reflected in the count - which can make your territory coverage look cleaner than the underlying data actually is.

dax: Total Store Count = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[StoreKey] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique stores that appear in the Sales fact table, giving you the distinct store footprint reflected in actual sales transactions rather than a master list of all stores. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active - slicing by region, date, or product will narrow the count to only stores with at least one matching sale row in that filtered result set. The mechanics are straightforward: DISTINCTCOUNT scans the StoreKey column and returns the number of unique non-blank values it finds. The key watch-out is that stores with zero sales in a given period are invisible to this measure - if you need to count all stores including those that didn't sell (e.g., newly opened or temporarily closed locations), you'd need to drive the count from a Store dimension table instead.

dax: Total Employee Count = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[EmployeeKey] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique employees who appear in the Sales table - in other words, how many distinct salespeople generated at least one transaction within whatever filter context is active (date range, region, product category, etc.). It works by scanning the EmployeeKey column in the filtered Sales table and returning the count of unique values, so it naturally respects all slicers and report filters without any additional manipulation. The key watch-out is that it counts employees who made sales, not your total headcount - if an employee exists in an HR or Employee dimension table but has no rows in Sales, they will be silently excluded from this count, which can make the number misleading if you're trying to measure workforce size or identify zero-sales reps.

dax: Total Resellers = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[ResellerKey] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique resellers that appear in the Sales table, giving you a distinct headcount of active selling partners rather than a raw row count. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, report-level filters - so it naturally scopes down to, say, a specific region or time period without any extra logic needed. The key watch-out is that it counts resellers who appear in the Sales table, not those defined in a Reseller dimension; if a reseller exists in your dimension but has made no sales in the current filter context, they won't be counted, which can cause discrepancies if stakeholders expect a "total registered resellers" figure instead.

dax: Total Promotions Used = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[PromotionKey] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique promotions that appear in the Sales table, giving you a sense of promotional variety or reach - for example, how many distinct promotions were applied across a set of transactions. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active (a date range, product category, store, etc.), so the result naturally scopes down as slicers and filters are applied. Under the hood, DISTINCTCOUNT is essentially COUNTROWS over a deduplicated list of PromotionKey values in the current context. The key watch-out is that if your data includes a "no promotion" sentinel value - a common warehouse pattern where PromotionKey = 0 or a blank-equivalent key represents transactions with no promotion applied - that placeholder will be counted as a distinct promotion, inflating the figure; you may need to filter it out explicitly.

dax: Total Currency Types = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CurrencyKey] )
explanation: This measure counts how many distinct currencies appear in the Sales table for whatever filter context is active - for example, a single region, time period, or product category selected in a report. It works by scanning the CurrencyKey column in the (potentially filtered) Sales table and returning the number of unique values found, with no iteration or time intelligence involved. One key watch-out: it counts distinct keys present in actual Sales transactions, not all currencies defined in a Currency dimension table, so currencies that exist in your model but have never been used in a sale will be silently excluded from the count.

dax: Total Product Colors = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[Color] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique color values that exist in the Product table's Color column, giving you a simple cardinality figure - for example, how many distinct product colors are available in your catalogue. It evaluates DISTINCTCOUNT against whatever filter context is active, so slicing by category, brand, or any related dimension will reduce the count to only the colors associated with filtered products. The one watch-out is that blank values in the Color column are counted as a distinct value by DISTINCTCOUNT, so if some products have no color assigned you may see a higher number than expected - consider cleaning nulls upstream or adding an explicit filter to exclude blanks if that inflates your business metric.

dax: Total Product Sizes = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[Size] )
explanation: This measure counts how many unique size values exist in the Product table's Size column - useful for understanding the breadth of the product catalogue by size. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active, so slicing by category or brand will narrow the count to only the distinct sizes available for those filtered products. The mechanics are straightforward: DISTINCTCOUNT scans the filtered column and returns the number of non-duplicate values. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT silently ignores blanks, so if some products have no size assigned, those rows won't contribute to the count - which can make the result misleadingly low if incomplete data is a known issue in your Product table.

dax: Total Product Styles = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[Style] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique product styles present in your product catalog, giving analysts a quick read on style-level assortment breadth. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, or report-level filters - so the number will naturally narrow as filters are applied, showing only distinct styles for the visible subset of products. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT includes blank as a distinct value, so if your Style column has any missing or unpopulated entries, the count will be inflated by one - clean your data or swap in CALCULATE(DISTINCTCOUNT(...), NOT ISBLANK(Product[Style])) if that's a concern.

dax: Total Customer Segments = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Customer[CustomerSegment] )
explanation: This measure counts how many unique customer segment values exist in the Customer table under the current filter context. It uses DISTINCTCOUNT to scan the CustomerSegment column and return the number of distinct non-blank values, so slicing by region, date, or product will naturally reduce the count to only those segments associated with filtered customers. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT ignores blanks, so if any customers have a null or missing segment value, those records are silently excluded from the count - which can make the result misleadingly low if segment data quality is poor.

dax: Average Customer Age = AVERAGE ( Customer[Age] )
explanation:
This measure calculates the mean age of customers by summing all values in the Customer Age column and dividing by the count of non-blank rows. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active - slicers, report filters, or visual-level filters - so the average will automatically narrow to the filtered subset of customers. The mechanics are straightforward: AVERAGE iterates the column directly without explicit row-by-row logic.

The key watch-out is that AVERAGE silently ignores blank values, so if Age is unpopulated for a significant portion of customers, the result will reflect only those with a recorded age and may overstate or skew the true population average without any visible warning.

dax: Total Return Amount = SUM ( Sales[ReturnAmount] )
explanation: This measure sums every value in the ReturnAmount column of the Sales table to produce the total monetary value of customer returns within whatever filter context is active - slice it by date, product, or region and it automatically narrows to the relevant rows. It uses a straightforward SUM aggregation with no explicit iteration or time intelligence, so it relies entirely on the report's filter context (visuals, slicers, and relationships) to scope the result. The key watch-out is that this measure will silently return a result even if ReturnAmount contains nulls or negative values mixed with positives - if returns are stored as negative numbers in the same column as sales amounts rather than as standalone positive figures, this measure may net off against sales and misrepresent the true gross return value.

dax: Total Return Quantity = SUM ( Sales[ReturnQuantity] )
explanation: This measure sums every value in the ReturnQuantity column across all rows of the Sales table that survive the current filter context - slicers, row/column headers, or report-level filters all narrow which rows are included before the SUM executes. It produces a single aggregated count of returned units, making it useful as a standalone KPI or as a building block inside more complex measures like net quantity sold. The key watch-out is that if ReturnQuantity stores returns as negative integers (a common convention), this measure will return a negative total, which can cause double-negation errors when you subtract it from gross quantity - confirm the sign convention in the source data before using it in downstream calculations.

dax: Total Net Revenue = SUM ( Sales[NetRevenue] )
explanation: This measure returns the total net revenue by summing every value in the NetRevenue column of the Sales table. It respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, or other CALCULATE filters - so the result automatically scopes to the selected period, product, region, or any other dimension in play. The mechanics are straightforward: no iteration, no time intelligence, just a direct column aggregation handed off to the storage engine for maximum performance. The key watch-out is that this measure is only as clean as the data in NetRevenue itself - if returns, discounts, or adjustments aren't already baked into that column, the figure will overstate revenue, and that assumption should be documented clearly for downstream consumers.

dax: Max Profit = MAX ( Sales[Profit] )
explanation: This measure returns the single highest profit value recorded across any individual sales transaction row. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active - a selected product, region, time period, or any slicer combination - and simply scans the Profit column to surface the maximum value meeting those conditions. The key watch-out is that this operates at the row level of the Sales table, not as an aggregated total; if your Profit column stores pre-calculated line-level figures rather than a true computed margin, dirty source data or negative margins on individual rows could mislead users into thinking a product or period is more profitable than it actually is in aggregate.

dax: Min Profit = MIN ( Sales[Profit] )
explanation: This measure returns the single lowest profit value found across all rows of the Sales table that are visible in the current filter context - for example, the worst-performing transaction for a selected product, region, or time period. It works by scanning the Profit column and returning the minimum scalar value, inheriting whatever filters are already applied by slicers, rows, or columns in the report. One key watch-out: if Profit contains negative values (losses), the measure will correctly surface the largest loss as the minimum, which is often the intended behaviour - but if nulls or blanks exist in the column, MIN silently ignores them, so a result of zero doesn't necessarily mean no losses occurred; it means the lowest non-blank value is zero.

dax: Total Sales Transactions = COUNTROWS ( Sales )
explanation:
This measure counts the total number of rows in the Sales table, where each row is assumed to represent a single sales transaction. It works by simply counting all rows that survive the current filter context - so slicing by date, region, or product will automatically narrow the count to matching transactions. The mechanics are straightforward: no iteration or aggregation beyond the row count itself.

The key watch-out is that this measure equals the grain of your Sales table, so if your table contains line items rather than order headers, it counts line items - not distinct orders - which can mislead stakeholders who expect an "order count." Always confirm what one row actually represents in your model before using this as a transaction KPI.

dax: Total Revenue = SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] )
explanation: This measure sums every value in the SalesAmount column to produce total revenue in dollars (or whatever currency the column holds). It respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, report-level filters - so the result automatically scopes to the selected product, date range, region, or any other dimension without any explicit CALCULATE or FILTER needed. The simplicity is its strength, but also its main watch-out: if SalesAmount contains returns, discounts, or cancelled orders that haven't been excluded upstream in the data model or ETL, this measure will silently include them, overstating true revenue. Always confirm what the source column actually represents before publishing this as a headline KPI.

dax: Total Quantity Sold = SUM ( Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure sums every value in the Quantity column of the Sales table to produce total units sold across whatever context is active - a product, a region, a time period, or the full dataset. It relies entirely on the implicit filter context imposed by report visuals, slicers, and row/column headers, with no explicit filters of its own. Because it's a simple aggregation with no CALCULATE wrapper, any filter context placed on the model flows through naturally and predictably. The one watch-out is that if the Sales table contains returns or cancellations as negative quantities rather than in a separate table, those will silently net down the total - which may be intentional but can surprise stakeholders expecting gross units sold.

dax: Total Transactions = COUNTROWS ( Sales )
explanation:
This measure counts the total number of rows in the Sales table, where each row is assumed to represent a single transaction. It respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, report filters, or visual headers - so the result automatically adjusts to show only the transactions relevant to the current selection. There is no explicit iteration or time intelligence involved; it simply hands the filtered table to COUNTROWS and returns the row count.

The key watch-out is that the measure treats every row as exactly one transaction, so if the Sales table contains line-item-level granularity (e.g., multiple rows per order), the result reflects line items rather than distinct orders - make sure the table's grain matches your intended business definition of "transaction."

dax: Average Sale Amount = AVERAGE ( Sales[SalesAmount] )
explanation: This measure calculates the mean transaction value across all sales records in scope, giving analysts a sense of the typical deal or order size. It works by summing every value in the SalesAmount column visible under the current filter context and dividing by the count of non-blank rows in that same filtered set - standard AVERAGE behaviour. One watch-out: AVERAGE silently ignores blank cells but includes zeros, so if missing or cancelled sales are stored as zero rather than blank, they will drag the average down and distort the true picture of typical sale size.

dax: Unique Customers = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of distinct customers who appear in the Sales table - in other words, how many unique customers made at least one purchase within whatever context is active (a date range, a region, a product category, etc.). It works by scanning the CustomerKey column in Sales and returning the count of unique values after the current filter context has been applied, so slicing by month will give you unique customers for that month, not across all time. The one watch-out is that it counts distinct keys present in the Sales table only, so a customer who exists in a Customers dimension but has never transacted will be invisible to this measure - if you need to count registered customers regardless of purchases, you'd need to run DISTINCTCOUNT against the dimension table instead.

dax: Unique Products Sold = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[ProductKey] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of distinct products that appear in the Sales table - in other words, how many unique products were actually sold in any given context. It works by scanning the ProductKey column in Sales after all active filters have been applied (by the report slicers, row/column headers, or other measures), returning a count of unique values within that filtered result set. No iteration or time intelligence is involved; it's a straightforward aggregation over the current filter context. The key watch-out is that it counts distinct keys present in the Sales table, not in the Products dimension - so if a product exists in your product catalogue but has never generated a transaction, it won't be counted, and if ProductKey contains NULLs, DISTINCTCOUNT will count NULL as one additional distinct value, which can silently inflate your numbers.

dax: Total Orders = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderNumber] )
explanation: Counts the number of unique sales orders in the current filter context, giving you a clean order-volume metric regardless of how many line items each order contains. It works by scanning the OrderNumber column in the Sales table and returning the count of distinct non-blank values, automatically respecting whatever slicers, page filters, or row/column headers are active. One watch-out: DISTINCTCOUNT ignores blank values, so if OrderNumber is ever null or empty due to data quality issues or an incomplete load, those records will be silently excluded from the count and you'll understate true order volume without any error to alert you.

dax: Max Sale Amount = MAX ( Sales[SalesAmount] )
explanation: This measure returns the single highest individual sale amount recorded in the Sales table, giving analysts a quick read on the largest transaction within whatever slice is currently selected. It works by iterating over every row of Sales[SalesAmount] visible in the current filter context - slicers, report filters, and row/column selections all narrow the set of rows MAX evaluates. Because it operates on the physical column rather than a calculated expression, it is fast and straightforward with no time intelligence or row-by-row calculation involved. The key watch-out is that if SalesAmount contains nulls or blanks, MAX silently ignores them, which is usually fine - but if *all* rows in context are blank, the measure returns blank rather than zero, which can cause unexpected behaviour in visuals or downstream measures that assume a numeric result.

dax: Min Sale Amount = MIN ( Sales[SalesAmount] )
explanation: This measure returns the single lowest individual sales transaction amount recorded in the Sales table. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active - a slicer, row in a visual, or report-level filter - so the minimum automatically scopes to the visible subset of data. Internally, DAX iterates the filtered Sales[SalesAmount] column and returns the smallest non-blank numeric value it finds. The key watch-out is that MIN silently ignores blank cells, so if a transaction has a missing or null SalesAmount it won't surface as zero or flag an anomaly - you could be understating your true data quality issues without realising it.

dax: Total Bonus Amount = SUM ( Sales[BonusAmount] )
explanation: This measure sums every value in the BonusAmount column of the Sales table, giving the total bonus dollars earned across whatever scope is currently in view - a region, a time period, a product, etc. It relies entirely on the surrounding filter context (slicers, rows, columns, report filters) to slice the result; there is no explicit filtering or iteration inside the measure itself. One watch-out: if BonusAmount contains blanks or nulls, SUM silently skips them, which is usually fine - but if upstream data processes sometimes write a zero instead of a blank (or vice versa), your totals can be inconsistently under- or over-stated depending on source data quality.

dax: Average Unit Cost = AVERAGE ( Sales[UnitCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the simple arithmetic mean of the unit cost across all rows in the Sales table that survive the current filter context - for example, sliced by product, region, or date. It delegates directly to the AVERAGE function, which sums all non-blank UnitCost values and divides by their count, with no explicit iteration or time intelligence involved. Because it averages row-level costs rather than computing a weighted average, a product with thousands of low-volume transactions will pull the result differently than one with a few high-volume lines, which can be misleading when transaction counts vary significantly across segments. Ensure that UnitCost is stored at the right granularity in the Sales table; if rows represent order lines with varying quantities and the cost is not already per-unit, the figure will be wrong without a quantity-weighted adjustment.

dax: Total Shipping Cost = SUM ( Sales[ShippingCost] )
explanation: This measure totals the shipping cost across all sales transactions visible in the current filter context - essentially answering "how much did we spend on shipping for this slice of data?" It works by simply summing every value in the ShippingCost column that survives whatever filters are applied by slicers, rows, columns, or other measures at the time of evaluation. Because it's a straightforward SUM with no explicit CALCULATE or filter manipulation, it responds directly and fully to all external filter context with no override. The key watch-out is that if ShippingCost contains blanks or nulls - common when shipping isn't applicable to certain order types - those rows are silently ignored by SUM, which may cause the total to appear lower than expected and mislead comparisons or margin calculations.

dax: Unique Countries = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Geography[Country] )
explanation: This measure counts how many distinct countries appear in the Geography table's Country column under whatever filter context is active - for example, dropping it into a visual filtered to a region will return only the countries within that region. It uses DISTINCTCOUNT, which is a single-pass aggregation requiring no iteration or time intelligence, making it straightforward and performant. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT includes blank as a countable value, so if your Country column contains any blank rows - common when fact rows fail to match a dimension key - your count will be inflated by one, silently misrepresenting the true number of named countries.

dax: Unique States = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Geography[StateProvince] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of distinct state/province values present in the Geography table, filtered down to whatever slice the report consumer has selected - for example, a particular country, region, or time period if those filters cross-filter the Geography table. It uses DISTINCTCOUNT, which internally iterates the visible rows of the StateProvince column and returns the count of unique non-blank values within the current filter context. The one watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT ignores blanks, so if your Geography table contains rows with a null or empty StateProvince - common when geocoding is incomplete or a country-level record has no state equivalent - those rows will be silently excluded from the count, potentially understating coverage.

dax: Unique Postal Codes = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Geography[PostalCode] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of distinct postal codes present in the Geography table, giving you a feel for geographic spread or coverage - for example, how many unique service areas, delivery zones, or customer locations exist. It uses DISTINCTCOUNT, which internally groups the PostalCode column and counts the unique values, respecting whatever filter context is active (slicers, report filters, row context in a matrix), so the result will change dynamically as users drill down or filter by region, date, or other dimensions. One key watch-out: DISTINCTCOUNT includes a blank as a distinct value, so if PostalCode contains any nulls or empty strings in the source data, the count will be inflated by one - worth cleaning upstream or explicitly filtering blanks if data quality is inconsistent.

dax: Total Product Count = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[ProductKey] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique products that have actually appeared in at least one sales transaction, making it a "sold products" count rather than a full product catalogue count. It works by scanning the ProductKey column in the Sales table under whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, or other measures - and returning the number of distinct values found there. One key watch-out: because it operates on the Sales table rather than a Products dimension table, any product that exists in your catalogue but has never generated a sale will be silently excluded from the count, which can cause the total to appear lower than expected and makes it unsuitable for counting your full product range.

dax: Total Male Customers = COUNTROWS ( FILTER ( Customer, Customer[Gender] = "Male" ) )
explanation: This measure counts the number of male customers in the Customer table, giving a simple headcount of the male segment of your customer base. It works by iterating over the Customer table with FILTER to isolate rows where Gender equals "Male," then counting those rows with COUNTROWS - and because no explicit CALCULATE wrapper is used, the result still respects any external filter context applied by slicers, report filters, or visual interactions. The key watch-out is that this measure is case-sensitive to the exact string stored in the Gender column, so inconsistent data entry like "male," "MALE," or leading/trailing spaces will cause those rows to be silently excluded from the count, making data quality in the source a critical dependency.

dax: Total Female Customers = COUNTROWS ( FILTER ( Customer, Customer[Gender] = "Female" ) )
explanation: This measure counts the total number of female customers in the Customer table, giving you a simple headcount of your female customer base regardless of any transaction activity. It works by iterating over the Customer table with FILTER to return only rows where Gender equals "Female," then COUNTROWS tallies those rows - and because it uses the full table rather than a column, it will respond to any external filter context applied (e.g., region, segment slicers). The key watch-out is that this counts customer *records*, not distinct customers, so if your Customer table has duplicate rows for the same individual - common when Customer is built from a fact table rather than a true dimension - the count will be inflated; always verify the table has one row per customer.

dax: Unique Brands = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[Brand] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of distinct brand values in the Product table's Brand column - in plain terms, it tells you how many unique brands exist in whatever context is currently in view. It uses DISTINCTCOUNT, which applies the current filter context, so slicing by category, region, or date will automatically narrow the count to only the brands visible after those filters are applied. The one watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT includes a blank as a distinct value if any rows in Product[Brand] are empty, which can inflate your count by one and mask data quality issues in your product master.

dax: Unique Product Colors = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[Color] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of distinct color values that exist in the Product table's Color column, giving you a simple cardinality figure - for example, how many unique colors appear in your product catalogue. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active, so slicing by Category, for instance, will automatically narrow the count to only the colors present in that category.
