Under the hood, TOTALYTD overrides the existing date filter context and replaces it with a range spanning January 1st (or a custom fiscal year-end date if specified) to the latest date in the current filter, then evaluates [Total Returns] over that expanded window. The key watch-out is that TOTALYTD defaults to a December 31st year-end - if your business runs a non-calendar fiscal year, you must pass the optional year_end_date argument (e.g., "6/30") or the accumulated totals will silently reset on the wrong date. Also ensure your Date table is marked as a Date Table and is contiguous, otherwise time intelligence functions like this one can produce unexpected gaps or miscalculations.

dax: Returns PY = CALCULATE ( [Total Returns], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR ( 'Date'[Date] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the total product returns for the same period in the prior year, giving analysts a like-for-like prior-year baseline to compare against current performance. It works by wrapping the [Total Returns] base measure inside CALCULATE, which replaces the current date filter context with an equivalent date range shifted back exactly 12 months using SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR. The function relies on a contiguous, complete Date table with no gaps, where the Date column is properly marked as a date table or at minimum covers the full calendar years in your data. The key watch-out is that if your current filter context contains a partial period - say, only part of a month or year - SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR shifts that exact partial range back 12 months, so you're always comparing apples to apples in terms of days; however, if the Date table doesn't extend back far enough to cover the prior-year equivalent dates, the measure will return blank rather than an error, which can silently mislead users into thinking there were zero returns.

dax: Net Sales YTD = TOTALYTD ( [Net Sales], 'Date'[Date] )
explanation: This measure accumulates Net Sales from the first day of the current year through the last date in the current filter context, giving you a running year-to-date total. Under the hood, TOTALYTD overrides the date filter context to include all dates from January 1st up to the latest visible date in your 'Date' table, then evaluates the base [Net Sales] measure over that expanded window. The critical watch-out is that your 'Date' table must be marked as a Date Table (or have a continuous, gap-free date range with a properly configured relationship), and by default the year boundary resets on December 31st - if your fiscal year ends on a different date, you must supply the optional year-end-date argument to TOTALYTD, otherwise the accumulation will reset at the wrong time.

dax: Net Sales MTD = TOTALMTD ( [Net Sales], 'Date'[Date] )
explanation: This measure accumulates Net Sales from the first day of the current month through the last visible date in the filter context, giving you a running month-to-date total. Under the hood, TOTALMTD is syntactic sugar for CALCULATE([Net Sales], DATESMTD('Date'[Date])), which overrides the date filter with a contiguous range from the month start to the current date in context - no explicit iteration needed. The key watch-out is that your Date table must be marked as a Date Table (or at least have contiguous, gap-free dates) and the filter context must contain exactly one period; if your report has no date selected or spans multiple months, TOTALMTD will use the *last* date in the current filter range as its anchor, which can produce unexpectedly large or misleading accumulations.

dax: Sales QTD = TOTALQTD ( [Total Sales], 'Date'[Date] )
explanation: This measure calculates the running cumulative sales total from the first day of the current quarter up to the last visible date in the current filter context - essentially a quarter-to-date revenue figure. It works by having TOTALQTD automatically override whatever date filter is active on the Date table, replacing it with a range that spans from the quarter's start date through the last date currently selected, then evaluating [Total Sales] within that expanded context. Under the hood it's equivalent to wrapping CALCULATE around [Total Sales] with a DATESQTD filter, so it relies entirely on a properly marked Date table with contiguous, gap-free dates to function correctly. The key watch-out is that if your Date table isn't marked as an official Date Table in the model (or contains date gaps), TOTALQTD can silently return incorrect results - always verify the Date table is fully contiguous and flagged accordingly.

dax: Gross Profit YTD = TOTALYTD ( [Gross Profit], 'Date'[Date] )
explanation: This measure accumulates Gross Profit from the first day of the current fiscal/calendar year through the last date in the current filter context, giving you a running year-to-date gross profit figure. Under the hood, TOTALYTD overrides whatever date filter is active in the report and replaces it with a range from January 1st (or your defined fiscal year-end date) up to the latest visible date, then evaluates the base [Gross Profit] measure within that expanded context. One critical watch-out: if your model's date table isn't marked as an official Date Table or has gaps in its calendar, TOTALYTD can produce incorrect or blank results - and if your fiscal year doesn't end on December 31st, you must pass the optional year-end-date argument (e.g., "6/30") or the YTD boundary will silently be wrong.

dax: Gross Profit QTD = TOTALQTD ( [Gross Profit], 'Date'[Date] )
explanation: This measure accumulates Gross Profit from the first day of the current quarter up to the last date in the current filter context, giving users a running quarter-to-date total on any report visual. Under the hood, TOTALQTD is syntactic sugar for CALCULATE([Gross Profit], DATESQTD('Date'[Date])), which overrides the existing date filter with a contiguous range starting at the quarter boundary and ending at the selected date. One critical watch-out: this measure requires your Date table to be properly marked as a Date Table in Power BI (or have its date column flagged accordingly), and it must have contiguous, gap-free dates covering your data range - missing dates will cause TOTALQTD to silently return incorrect or blank results.

dax: Total Sales PY = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR ( 'Date'[Date] ) )
explanation: This measure returns the total sales figure for the same calendar period one year prior to whatever date range is currently selected or filtered in the report. It works by wrapping the base [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which replaces the existing date filter context with an equivalent date range shifted exactly 12 months back, using SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR to perform that shift against a contiguous Date column. The mechanics rely on CALCULATE propagating all other filter context - slicers, page filters, row context from a matrix - unchanged, while only swapping out the date filter. The critical watch-out is that your Date table must be marked as an official date table and contain a complete, unbroken range of dates covering both the current and prior year periods; if any dates are missing or the table isn't properly marked, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR can silently return blank or incorrect results.

dax: Total Cost PY = CALCULATE ( [Total Cost], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR ( 'Date'[Date] ) )
explanation: This measure returns the total cost for the same period one year prior to whatever time period is currently in context - for example, if you're looking at March 2024, it shows March 2023's total cost. It works by wrapping the base [Total Cost] measure inside CALCULATE, using SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR to replace the current date filter context with the equivalent set of dates shifted back exactly 365 days (or one calendar year). The function returns a table of those prior-year dates, which CALCULATE uses to override the filter on the Date table before evaluating the measure. The critical watch-out is that your Date table must be marked as a Date Table (or at minimum contain a contiguous, gap-free set of dates), and your model must have an active relationship between the Date table and the fact table - without these, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR will either return blank or produce incorrect results silently.

dax: Sales YoY Change = [Total Sales] - [Total Sales PY]
explanation: This measure calculates the absolute year-over-year change in sales by subtracting the prior year's total sales from the current year's total sales, giving you a raw dollar (or unit) difference rather than a percentage. It relies entirely on whatever [Total Sales PY] does under the hood - typically a CALCULATE with SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or a DATEADD shift - meaning the date table and its relationship to the fact table must be correctly configured for the time intelligence to resolve. Because it's a simple subtraction of two measures, it inherits the filter context of each in full, so the result will be blank wherever [Total Sales PY] returns blank (e.g., the earliest year in your dataset), which can quietly suppress rows or visuals rather than showing the expected current-year value. Make sure your date table is marked as a Date Table and covers a full, contiguous calendar range, otherwise the prior-year shift may silently return blank or incorrect values.

dax: Units Sold YoY Change = [Total Units] - [Units Sold PY]
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year change in units sold by subtracting the prior-year units from the current-year units, giving analysts a simple absolute volume delta rather than a percentage shift. It relies entirely on the logic baked into [Total Units] and [Units Sold PY], where the latter almost certainly uses SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or a similar time-intelligence function to shift the date filter context back twelve months. The result inherits whatever filter context is active on the report - slicer selections, row/column dimensions, and date filters all flow through both base measures simultaneously. The key watch-out is that if the date table has gaps or the current period is incomplete (e.g., a partial month being compared to a full prior-year month), the delta will be misleadingly negative, so always validate that both periods have comparable date coverage before drawing conclusions.

dax: Gross Profit YoY Change = [Gross Profit] - [Gross Profit PY]
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year change in gross profit in absolute currency terms - the difference between the current period's gross profit and the same period one year prior. It works by simply subtracting the prior-year gross profit measure from the current-year measure, meaning all the heavy lifting (the time intelligence shift, typically via SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or DATEADD) lives inside [Gross Profit PY], and this measure inherits whatever filter context is active in the report - slicers, row context, page filters, and so on. If your date table has gaps, the current period is only partially complete, or [Gross Profit PY] returns BLANK for periods with no prior-year data, subtracting BLANK from a number returns the current value unchanged rather than signalling a missing comparison, which can silently make a partial or unmatched period look like a flat year-over-year result instead of an invalid one.

dax: Cost YoY Change = [Total Cost] - [Total Cost PY]
explanation: This measure calculates the absolute year-over-year change in total cost, telling you whether costs increased or decreased compared to the same period in the prior year. It works by subtracting a prior-year cost measure - which internally uses SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or a similar time-intelligence function to shift the date filter context back exactly 12 months - from the current period's total cost. The result is a simple dollar (or currency) delta, not a percentage, so a large number could look alarming in a low-base prior year or misleading in a high-base one. The critical watch-out is that if the prior-year period has no data - because your date table doesn't extend back far enough, or the earliest year in your model is the current one - [Total Cost PY] will return BLANK, causing this measure to also return BLANK rather than the current cost value, which can silently suppress visuals or totals.

dax: Transactions YoY Change = [Total Transactions] - [Transactions PY]
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year absolute change in transaction count - the raw difference between the current period's transactions and the same period one year prior. It works by subtracting a prior-year measure (which internally uses SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or a similar time-intelligence function to shift the date filter back 12 months) from the current [Total Transactions], both evaluated within whatever filter context exists on the report. The result is a simple delta: positive means growth, negative means decline. The key watch-out is that if [Transactions PY] returns BLANK for periods where no prior-year data exists - such as the first year in your date table or for dates beyond the last actual transaction - the subtraction will silently treat BLANK as zero, which can produce misleading results; always validate that your date table fully covers the comparison window and consider wrapping the measure in an ISBLANK check if partial periods are a concern.

dax: Revenue Prior Year = CALCULATE ( [Total Revenue], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, YEAR ) )
explanation: This measure returns the total revenue for the same period one year ago, letting analysts compare current performance against the prior year. It works by wrapping the existing [Total Revenue] measure inside CALCULATE, which replaces the current date filter context with an equivalent date range shifted back exactly 365 days - or more precisely, one calendar year - using DATEADD on the Date table's Date column. The key watch-out is that your Date table must be marked as an official date table (or at minimum be contiguous with no gaps) and must contain dates for the prior-year period; if your Date table only covers the current fiscal year, DATEADD will find no matching dates and the measure will return blank rather than an error, which can silently distort year-over-year comparisons.

dax: Gross Profit Prior Month = CALCULATE ( [Gross Profit], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, MONTH ) )
explanation: This measure returns the Gross Profit figure for the calendar month immediately preceding whatever month is currently in filter context - useful for period-over-period comparisons or MoM variance calculations. It works by wrapping the [Gross Profit] measure inside CALCULATE, using DATEADD to shift the entire set of dates in the current filter context back by one month, so the underlying sales and cost data evaluated comes from that prior period. The Date table must be contiguous (no gaps) and the filter context must resolve to a recognisable date range - if your report has no date filter applied at all, DATEADD will shift the full Date table back a month and likely return an unexpectedly large number rather than blank. Make sure your Date table extends far enough into the past to cover the shifted period, otherwise the measure will return blank for the earliest month in your dataset.

dax: Gross Profit Prior Quarter = CALCULATE ( [Gross Profit], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, QUARTER ) )
explanation: This measure returns the Gross Profit figure for the quarter that immediately precedes whatever quarter is currently in the filter context, making it useful for quarter-over-quarter variance analysis. It works by using DATEADD to shift the entire set of dates in the current filter context back by one quarter, then recalculating the Gross Profit measure over that shifted date range within CALCULATE. The key watch-out is that this depends on having a fully contiguous, gap-free Date table that is properly marked as a date table and covers at least one full quarter before the earliest date in your data - if the Date table is incomplete or the filter context spans partial quarters, DATEADD can return unexpected or blank results.

dax: Units Sold Prior Month = CALCULATE ( [Total Units], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, MONTH ) )
explanation: This measure returns the total units sold during the same period one month prior to whatever time period is currently in context - for example, if you're looking at March 2024, it returns February 2024 units. It works by wrapping the base [Total Units] measure inside CALCULATE, using DATEADD to shift the entire set of dates in the current filter context back by one calendar month, so the measure evaluates [Total Units] against that shifted date range rather than the original one. The key watch-out is that DATEADD requires a contiguous, complete set of dates in your Date table - gaps, missing dates, or a Date table that doesn't extend far enough into the past will silently return blank or incorrect results, so always verify your Date table is fully populated and marked as a Date table in the model.

dax: Units Sold Prior Quarter = CALCULATE ( [Total Units], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, QUARTER ) )
explanation: This measure returns the total units sold during the same period one quarter ago, giving analysts a like-for-like prior-period comparison. It works by wrapping the base [Total Units] measure inside CALCULATE, where DATEADD shifts the current filter context's date range back by exactly one quarter - so if the visual is filtered to Q2, the measure evaluates [Total Units] over Q1 dates instead. The date shift is applied to whatever dates are currently in filter context, meaning it works correctly for partial quarters too (e.g., if only the first two months of Q2 are selected, it returns only the corresponding two months of Q1). The critical watch-out is that your Date table must be marked as a Date Table and contain a contiguous, gap-free calendar - if any dates are missing or the table isn't properly marked, DATEADD can silently return blank or incorrect results.

dax: Cost Prior Month = CALCULATE ( [Total Cost], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, MONTH ) )
explanation: This measure returns the total cost for the calendar month that is exactly one month prior to whatever month is currently in context - useful for period-over-period cost comparisons. It works by using DATEADD to shift every date in the current filter context back by one month, then passing that shifted date set to CALCULATE, which replaces the existing date filter so [Total Cost] evaluates over the prior month's rows. The key watch-out is that DATEADD requires a contiguous, complete set of dates in your Date table - if your Date table has gaps, doesn't extend far enough back, or the current context spans a partial month, the shifted date range may return incomplete or blank results rather than a clean full-month figure.

dax: Cost Prior Quarter = CALCULATE ( [Total Cost], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, QUARTER ) )
explanation: This measure returns the total cost figure from exactly one quarter prior to whatever time period is currently in context - useful for period-over-period comparisons or calculating quarter-on-quarter variance. It works by wrapping [Total Cost] in CALCULATE and using DATEADD to shift the active date filter back by one quarter, so if your report is filtered to Q2 2024, the measure evaluates [Total Cost] over Q1 2024 instead. The key watch-out is that DATEADD requires a contiguous, complete set of dates in the filter context to work reliably - if your Date table has gaps, your slicer selection covers a partial quarter, or the Date table isn't properly marked as a Date table, the time shift can return blanks or silently mis-calculate.

dax: Rolling 12M Revenue = CALCULATE ( [Total Revenue], DATESINPERIOD ( 'Date'[Date], MAX ( 'Date'[Date] ), -12, MONTH ) )
explanation: This measure computes the trailing 12-month revenue total as of the latest date visible in the current filter context - essentially a rolling annual revenue figure useful for trend analysis that smooths out seasonality. It works by using DATESINPERIOD to generate a set of dates spanning the 12 months ending on the MAX date in context, then passing that date set to CALCULATE to override the existing date filter and sum revenue across that window. The critical watch-out is that MAX('Date'[Date]) anchors to the last date *in the current filter context*, not necessarily today - so in a matrix sliced by month, each cell correctly rolls back 12 months from that month-end, but if your Date table is filtered to a partial period or doesn't extend to the current date, the anchor point will be earlier than expected, silently understating the result.

dax: Rolling 6M Units = CALCULATE ( [Total Units], DATESINPERIOD ( 'Date'[Date], MAX ( 'Date'[Date] ), -6, MONTH ) )
explanation: This measure computes the rolling six-month total of units sold ending on the latest visible date in the current filter context, giving analysts a smoothed demand view that absorbs short-term volatility. It works by using DATESINPERIOD to generate a dynamic date range spanning the six months prior to (and including) the MAX date in context, then passes that range to CALCULATE to override the existing date filter and sum units across that window. The MAX date shifts automatically as you slice by month, year, or any date attribute, so the window always trails the last date in scope rather than a fixed calendar anchor. Watch out when the Date table is filtered to a partial period - for example, if your slicer or row context only exposes a few days in a month, MAX('Date'[Date]) will anchor the window to that partial month-end, potentially shrinking the true six-month lookback and understating the rolling total.

dax: Rolling 3M Gross Profit = CALCULATE ( [Gross Profit], DATESINPERIOD ( 'Date'[Date], MAX ( 'Date'[Date] ), -3, MONTH ) )
explanation: This measure returns the cumulative Gross Profit over the three calendar months ending on the latest visible date in the current filter context, giving a rolling 3-month window that moves as users slice by time. It works by using DATESINPERIOD to build a dynamic date range - anchoring its end point at the MAX date in context and stepping back exactly 3 months - then passing that range to CALCULATE to override the existing date filter. The key watch-out is that DATESINPERIOD counts back 3 × 30 days worth of calendar months, so the window is anchored to MAX('Date'[Date]) rather than to clean month-end boundaries; if your Date table has gaps or the current context lands mid-month, you may get a partial boundary month, producing a window that doesn't align neatly with full calendar months as users often expect.

dax: Rolling 12M Gross Profit = CALCULATE ( [Gross Profit], DATESINPERIOD ( 'Date'[Date], MAX ( 'Date'[Date] ), -12, MONTH ) )
explanation: This measure computes the cumulative gross profit over the trailing 12 months ending on the latest visible date in the current filter context - essentially a rolling annual gross profit figure useful for smoothing seasonality. It works by using DATESINPERIOD to build a dynamic date range that stretches back 12 months from MAX('Date'[Date]), then passes that set of dates into CALCULATE to override the existing date filter and sum gross profit across that window. The MAX date shifts automatically as users slice by month or drag across a chart, so the window always rolls forward with the selection. The key watch-out is that MAX('Date'[Date]) responds to whatever dates are currently filtered, meaning if your Date table contains future dates with no transactions, or if a slicer cuts the table mid-period, the 12-month window anchors to an unexpected endpoint - always ensure your Date table is filtered or marked so MAX lands on the last date with actual data.

dax: Revenue MoM Change = [Total Revenue] - [Revenue Prior Month]
explanation: This measure calculates the absolute month-over-month change in revenue by subtracting the prior month's revenue from the current month's revenue, giving analysts a simple pound/dollar delta to track growth or decline. It relies entirely on whatever filter context is active - typically a month selection on a date table - and delegates the time-shift logic to the [Revenue Prior Month] measure, which almost certainly uses DATEADD or PREVIOUSMONTH to shift the context back one calendar month. The key watch-out is that if the date table has gaps, the current period is the very first month in your data, or [Revenue Prior Month] returns BLANK, the result will equal the full current-month revenue rather than BLANK or zero, which can silently distort period-over-period trend visuals and percentage calculations built on top of this measure.

dax: Revenue YoY % = DIVIDE ( [Total Revenue] - [Revenue PY], [Revenue PY] )
explanation: This measure shows year-over-year growth as a percentage - how much bigger or smaller this period's revenue is compared with the same stretch a year ago. It subtracts last year's figure ([Revenue PY]) from this year's and divides the gap by last year's number to turn it into a percentage; the [Revenue PY] piece is itself a time-intelligence measure that shifts the dates back twelve months (usually with SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR), and DIVIDE keeps things tidy by returning blank if last year's value is zero. So a result of 0.15 means '15% up on last year.' The key watch-out is that the calculation inherits whatever dates are on the visual, so if the current period isn't finished - say you're partway through this month - you'll be comparing a half-finished period against a complete one a year ago, which makes growth look far worse than it really is.

dax: Net Sales QTD = TOTALQTD ( [Net Sales], 'Date'[Date] )
explanation: This measure gives a quarter-to-date running total of Net Sales - everything from the first day of the current quarter up to the latest date in view, which is handy for tracking progress partway through a quarter. TOTALQTD does it by rewriting the date filter to cover the quarter-start-through-today range and then evaluating Net Sales over that span; it's really just a friendly shorthand for CALCULATE([Net Sales], DATESQTD('Date'[Date])). For that to work it needs a continuous calendar to find the quarter's starting point. The key watch-out is the same as with most time-intelligence: your Date table must be contiguous with no missing days (and ideally marked as the official Date table), or TOTALQTD can hand back wrong running totals without any error to warn you.

dax: Net Sales PY = CALCULATE ( [Net Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR ( 'Date'[Date] ) )
explanation: This measure pulls last year's Net Sales for whatever period you're currently looking at, giving you a like-for-like prior-year benchmark. CALCULATE swaps the current dates for the matching window twelve months earlier - built by SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR - and then evaluates Net Sales over those shifted dates. So viewing Q2 this year quietly compares against Q2 last year. The key watch-out has two parts: it needs a continuous Date table covering both years, and because SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR shifts by exact calendar dates, oddities like February 29th or a non-standard fiscal calendar can behave differently from DATEADD('Date'[Date], -1, YEAR) - so pick the function that matches how your business defines 'a year ago.'

dax: Net Sales YoY % = DIVIDE ( [Net Sales] - [Net Sales PY], [Net Sales PY] )
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year percentage change in net sales, expressing how much sales have grown or declined relative to the same period in the prior year. It works by subtracting the prior-year net sales figure from the current-year figure to get the absolute change, then dividing by the prior year as the base - both underlying measures carry their own filter context, so whatever date period is selected in the report (month, quarter, YTD, etc.) is automatically respected, assuming Net Sales PY uses a time-intelligence function like SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or DATEADD to shift that context back 12 months. The watch-out is that DIVIDE returns BLANK rather than an error when Net Sales PY is zero or blank, which is safe behaviour but can cause the measure to silently disappear in visuals for periods with no prior-year data - such as the first year in your date table - potentially misleading users into thinking growth is zero when in fact there is no valid comparison period at all.

dax: Gross Profit MTD = TOTALMTD ( [Gross Profit], 'Date'[Date] )
explanation: This measure accumulates Gross Profit from the first day of the current month through the last date present in the current filter context, giving a month-to-date running total. Under the hood, TOTALMTD is syntactic sugar for CALCULATE([Gross Profit], DATESMTD('Date'[Date])), which overrides the date filter context with all dates from the month-start up to and including the latest visible date. The result resets to zero at the start of each new month and grows daily, making it ideal for period-progress tracking on visuals sliced by date. The key watch-out is that your Date table must be marked as a Date Table (or have a contiguous range with no gaps) and the filter context must include a date selection - if a user filters to a non-date slicer alone or the Date table has missing dates, the "last visible date" logic can behave unexpectedly and return a larger-than-expected window or a blank.

dax: Units Sold YoY % = DIVIDE ( [Total Units] - [Units Sold PY], [Units Sold PY] )
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year percentage change in units sold - how much faster or slower sales are moving compared to the same period last year. It subtracts the prior-year units from the current-year units and divides by the prior-year baseline, relying on [Units Sold PY] to handle the time shift (typically via SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or DATEADD under the hood) and DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The result respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, row context in a matrix, page filters - so it will correctly scope to a selected product, region, or partial date range. The key watch-out is that if [Units Sold PY] returns blank rather than zero for a brand-new product with no prior-year history, DIVIDE will return blank too, which may silently hide new product launches in reports where you'd actually want to flag them as having no comparable baseline.

dax: Transactions YoY % = DIVIDE ( [Total Transactions] - [Transactions PY], [Transactions PY] )
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year percentage change in transaction volume, telling you whether the current period's transactions are growing or declining relative to the same period last year. It subtracts the prior-year transaction count from the current-year count and divides that difference by the prior-year count, relying on whatever [Transactions PY] uses internally - almost certainly a SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or DATEADD time-intelligence function - to shift the filter context back twelve months. DIVIDE handles the division safely, returning blank (by default) rather than an error when the prior-year value is zero or missing, which is cleaner than using the / operator. The key watch-out is that the result is only meaningful when the current period has complete data comparable to the prior period; if you're mid-month or filtering on a partial date range, you'll be comparing a full prior period against an incomplete current one, silently understating growth and misleading any trend analysis.

dax: Cost YoY % = DIVIDE ( [Total Cost] - [Cost PY], [Cost PY] )
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year percentage change in total cost, showing how much costs have grown or shrunk compared to the same period last year. It does so by subtracting the prior-year cost from the current-year cost and dividing by the prior-year cost - leaning on whatever [Cost PY] uses internally (typically SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or DATEADD) to shift the filter context back 12 months, and using DIVIDE to safely return BLANK instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The result is a decimal, so ensure the measure's format is set to percentage in your report, otherwise users will see 0.15 instead of 15%. The critical watch-out is that if [Cost PY] returns BLANK - common when there's no prior-year data in the model, or when the date table doesn't extend far enough back - the measure also returns BLANK rather than signalling an infinite or undefined change, which can silently hide the first year of data from trend visuals.

dax: Rolling 12M Transactions = CALCULATE ( [Total Transactions], DATESINPERIOD ( 'Date'[Date], MAX ( 'Date'[Date] ), -12, MONTH ) )
explanation: This measure computes the total number of transactions over the trailing 12 months ending on the latest visible date in the current filter context - essentially a rolling annual transaction count. It works by using DATESINPERIOD to generate a 12-month date range looking backward from MAX('Date'[Date]), then passing that set of dates to CALCULATE to override the existing date filter and sum transactions across that window. The result shifts automatically as you slice by month or year, making it useful for trend charts and period-over-period comparisons without manual date entry. The key watch-out is that MAX('Date'[Date]) anchors to the last date *in the current filter context*, not today - so if your report is filtered to, say, March, the window runs March back to April of the prior year, which is correct behavior, but if no date filter is applied the anchor becomes the last date in your entire Date table, which may surprise users expecting a "last 12 months from today" calculation.

dax: Revenue MoM Change = [Total Revenue] - CALCULATE ( [Total Revenue], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, MONTH ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the absolute change in revenue compared to the same period one month prior - in other words, how much revenue grew or shrank month-over-month in dollar terms. It works by subtracting a prior-month revenue value from the current period's revenue: CALCULATE wraps [Total Revenue] with DATEADD, which shifts the current filter context back by one month to pull the comparison figure, and the difference is returned. The entire calculation is sensitive to whatever filter context is already in play - slicers, report filters, and row context in visuals all shape both the current and prior-month values simultaneously. The key watch-out is that DATEADD requires a contiguous, complete set of dates in your Date table; if the date table has gaps, or if the current selection covers a partial month (e.g., month-to-date), the shifted period will also be partial, making the comparison misleading without additional handling.

dax: Revenue MoM % = DIVIDE ( [Total Revenue] - CALCULATE ( [Total Revenue], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, MONTH ) ), CALCULATE ( [Total Revenue], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, MONTH ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the month-over-month percentage change in revenue, showing how much revenue grew or declined compared to the prior month. It works by using DATEADD to shift the current filter context back by one month, recalculating Total Revenue over that shifted period, then dividing the difference between current and prior-month revenue by the prior-month revenue - DIVIDE handles the zero-denominator case gracefully by returning blank instead of an error. The key watch-out is that DATEADD requires a contiguous, complete set of dates in your Date table; if the current selection covers a partial month (e.g., month-to-date), the prior period will also shift by exactly one month of days rather than aligning to the equivalent partial prior month, which can produce misleading comparisons - so confirm whether partial-month reporting is in scope and whether PREVIOUSMONTH or a custom offset better fits your grain.

dax: Revenue QoQ Change = [Total Revenue] - CALCULATE ( [Total Revenue], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, QUARTER ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the absolute change in revenue compared to the same period one quarter ago - in other words, how much revenue has grown or shrunk quarter-over-quarter in dollar terms. It works by subtracting a prior-quarter revenue figure from the current period's revenue: CALCULATE wraps [Total Revenue] with DATEADD shifting the Date table back by one quarter, which overrides the existing date filter context to land on the equivalent date range three months earlier. The difference between the two gives you the raw revenue delta for whatever time window is currently selected. The key watch-out is that DATEADD requires a contiguous, complete set of dates in your Date table covering both the current and prior periods - if the prior quarter has any gaps or doesn't exist in the table at all (e.g., you're looking at the very first quarter in your data), the prior-period result will be blank and the measure will simply return the current revenue rather than a meaningful change figure, which can silently mislead users.

dax: Revenue QoQ % = DIVIDE ( [Total Revenue] - CALCULATE ( [Total Revenue], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, QUARTER ) ), CALCULATE ( [Total Revenue], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], -1, QUARTER ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the quarter-over-quarter percentage change in revenue, showing how much revenue has grown or declined relative to the same metric one quarter prior. It works by using DATEADD to shift the current filter context back by one quarter, computing prior-quarter revenue via CALCULATE, then dividing the absolute difference by that prior-quarter figure - DIVIDE handles any division-by-zero gracefully by returning BLANK rather than an error. The key watch-out is that DATEADD requires a contiguous, complete set of dates in your Date table for the current selection; if the current period is a partial quarter (e.g., a month-to-date filter mid-quarter), the prior-quarter window will be equally partial, which can produce a misleading comparison rather than a true full-quarter-vs-full-quarter result.

dax: Gross Profit YTD PY = CALCULATE ( [Gross Profit YTD], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR ( 'Date'[Date] ) )
explanation: This measure returns the year-to-date gross profit for the same period in the prior year, giving analysts a like-for-like YTD baseline for year-over-year comparison. It works by taking the existing Gross Profit YTD measure - which itself accumulates gross profit from the start of the fiscal or calendar year through the current date - and shifts the entire filter context back exactly 12 months using SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR, so if you're viewing March 2024 YTD, you see January-March 2023. The mechanics chain two layers of time intelligence: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR reshapes the date filter, and then the inner Gross Profit YTD measure applies its own YTD accumulation on top of that shifted range. The critical watch-out is that both measures depend on a properly marked, contiguous Date table with no gaps; if last year's dates are missing from the table or the Date table isn't connected to your fact table on every relevant date, the measure will return blank or silently undercount without any error to alert you.

dax: Revenue YTD PY = CALCULATE ( [Revenue YTD], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR ( 'Date'[Date] ) )
explanation: This measure returns the year-to-date revenue for the same period in the prior year, giving analysts a like-for-like baseline to assess growth against current YTD performance. It works by taking the existing [Revenue YTD] measure - which itself uses DATESYTD or a similar accumulation - and shifting the entire filter context on the Date table back exactly 12 months using SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR, so whatever date range is currently selected, the measure evaluates the YTD total as of the equivalent point one year earlier. The key watch-out is that [Revenue YTD] must already be a proper time-intelligence measure (not a simple SUM) for the year boundary reset to behave correctly; if your fiscal year end differs from December 31, you need to pass the fiscal year-end date argument to the underlying DATESYTD, otherwise the YTD accumulation will reset on the wrong date and silently return misleading prior-year figures.

dax: Revenue YTD YoY % = DIVIDE ( [Revenue YTD] - [Revenue YTD PY], [Revenue YTD PY] )
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year percentage growth in year-to-date revenue, answering the question "how much faster or slower is cumulative revenue growing compared to the same point last year?" It works by subtracting last year's YTD revenue from this year's, then dividing by last year's figure using DIVIDE, which safely returns BLANK instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The result inherits all the filter context from the two underlying YTD measures, so slicing by region, product, or any other dimension will correctly scope both the current and prior-year figures before the ratio is computed. The key watch-out is that if [Revenue YTD PY] returns BLANK rather than zero for periods with no prior-year data - common in the first year of trading or for newly added products - DIVIDE will also return BLANK, which can silently disappear from visuals and mislead users into thinking no comparison exists rather than signalling a true data gap.

dax: Returns YoY % = DIVIDE ( [Total Returns] - [Returns PY], [Returns PY] )
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year percentage change in product returns, expressing how much returns have grown or shrunk compared to the same period last year. It subtracts the prior-year returns figure from the current-year figure and divides by the prior year, relying entirely on whatever [Returns PY] does internally - typically a CALCULATE with SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or a DATEADD - to shift the filter context back twelve months. DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator so that a zero or blank denominator returns blank rather than an error, which is safe default behavior. The key watch-out is that this measure inherits all the date-intelligence assumptions baked into [Returns PY]: if your date table isn't marked as a date table, has gaps, or the current period is only partially complete, the comparison will be misleading - a partial current month divided against a full prior month will always look artificially negative.

dax: Revenue Next Year Forecast = CALCULATE ( [Total Revenue], DATEADD ( 'Date'[Date], 1, YEAR ) )
explanation: This measure projects what next year's revenue would look like by shifting the current filter context forward by one year - in other words, it shows the revenue that actually occurred in the period one year ahead of whatever date range is currently selected. It works by wrapping [Total Revenue] inside CALCULATE, where DATEADD replaces the active date filter with the equivalent set of dates shifted 12 months into the future, pulling real actuals from that future window rather than applying any statistical forecasting logic. The critical watch-out is that this measure returns blank for any period where no future data yet exists - so if your date table extends only through today, selections near or beyond the current date will silently return nothing, which can easily be mistaken for a zero or a model error rather than simply a gap in data coverage.

dax: Discount Rate = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[DiscountAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[GrossSales] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the percentage of gross revenue that was given away as discounts - essentially how aggressive discounting is relative to full-price sales in any given filter context. It works by summing all discount amounts and dividing by total gross sales using DIVIDE, which safely returns BLANK (rather than an error) when gross sales is zero. The result respects whatever filter context is active, so it will correctly recalculate across any slicer, row, or column - no special iteration or time intelligence is involved. Watch out for rows where GrossSales is zero but DiscountAmount is not - DIVIDE returns BLANK in that case, silently masking potentially bad data; also confirm that DiscountAmount is always a positive number in the source, since sign conventions vary and a negative discount would understate (or invert) the rate.

dax: Cost per Unit Sold = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average cost incurred for each unit sold - essentially a per-unit cost efficiency metric - by dividing total cost by total quantity within whatever filter context is active (e.g., a product, region, or time period selected in a report). It works straightforwardly: both SUM aggregations collapse all visible rows in the Sales table, and DIVIDE safely handles the case where quantity is zero by returning BLANK rather than a division error. One key watch-out is that this is a simple weighted average by row volume, not a true weighted average by cost structure - if your Sales table mixes products with wildly different cost profiles, the result at a higher level (e.g., total company) will be skewed by whichever product has the highest quantity, which may not reflect the cost story you intend to tell. Ensure your model doesn't contain duplicate or pre-aggregated rows in Sales, as those would silently inflate both numerator and denominator.

dax: Revenue per Order = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per unique sales order, giving a sense of typical order value across whatever slice of data is currently in view. It works by summing all SalesAmount values in the current filter context, then dividing by the count of distinct OrderIDs in that same context, using DIVIDE to return blank rather than an error if the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that if a single order spans multiple rows with different products or line items - which is common in transactional models - DISTINCTCOUNT correctly deduplicates the OrderID, but if OrderIDs are reused across different customers, dates, or regions (i.e., they aren't globally unique), the denominator will be understated and the average will be inflated.

dax: Profit Margin % = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[Profit] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the overall profit margin percentage - how many cents of profit are earned for every dollar of revenue - within whatever filter context is currently active (a product category, region, time period, etc.). It works by summing all rows in the Profit and SalesAmount columns that survive the current filter context, then safely dividing the two totals, returning BLANK rather than an error when SalesAmount is zero. The key watch-out is that because both columns are summed independently before dividing, this is a ratio of totals, not an average of row-level margins - so the result at a rolled-up level (e.g., a category total) will not equal a simple average of the individual product margins shown beneath it, which is actually the correct aggregation behavior but can surprise stakeholders expecting a straight average.

dax: Tax Rate % = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[TaxAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the effective tax rate as a percentage - the proportion of total tax collected relative to total sales revenue within whatever filter context is active (e.g., a product category, region, or time period selected in a report). It works simply by summing the TaxAmount and SalesAmount columns from the Sales table and dividing them using DIVIDE, which safely returns blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The result is a ratio, so you'll need to format the measure as a percentage in Power BI to display it correctly, as it won't automatically multiply by 100. The key watch-out is that this is a weighted blended rate across all transactions in context, not an average of per-row tax rates - if you mix product lines or jurisdictions with different tax rates, the result will shift with the sales mix, which can mask underlying rate changes.

dax: Shipping Cost Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[ShippingCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses shipping cost as a proportion of revenue - for every dollar of sales, how many cents are consumed by shipping. It sums both columns directly within whatever filter context is active (selected product, region, time period, etc.), then safely divides them using DIVIDE, which returns BLANK rather than an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that DIVIDE returning BLANK on zero sales can silently disappear from visuals or cause unexpected results in downstream measures that reference this ratio - if you need a zero instead, pass 0 as DIVIDE's third argument, and ensure your model has no rows where SalesAmount is zero but ShippingCost is non-zero, which would produce a misleading infinite-looking ratio otherwise handled only by that BLANK fallback.

dax: Sales per Product = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[ProductKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per distinct product sold, giving analysts a sense of how much each product contributes to total sales on average. It sums all SalesAmount values in the current filter context, then divides by the count of unique ProductKeys appearing in the same filtered Sales table - both aggregations respond to any slicers, row context, or report filters already in play. The division is wrapped in DIVIDE rather than a hard slash, so it safely returns blank instead of an error when no rows exist. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT counts only ProductKeys that actually appear in the Sales table within the current context, so products that exist in a Product dimension but have zero sales are silently excluded from the denominator - if your business definition of "products" includes non-selling items, this measure will overstate the per-product average.

dax: Units per Order = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average number of units sold per order, giving analysts a sense of order size or basket depth. It divides the total quantity of items sold - summed across all rows visible in the current filter context - by the count of distinct order IDs in that same context, using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that if a single order spans multiple rows in the Sales table (e.g., multiple line items), DISTINCTCOUNT correctly deduplicates it - but if OrderIDs are ever null or blank, those rows are excluded from the distinct count while their quantities still flow into the numerator, silently inflating the result.

dax: Revenue per Employee = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[EmployeeID] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the average revenue generated per employee by dividing total sales revenue by the number of distinct employees who appear in the Sales table. It evaluates entirely within the current filter context - whatever slicers, row labels, or report filters are active - so both the sum and the distinct count are scoped to the same filtered slice simultaneously. The division is handled safely by DIVIDE, which returns blank rather than an error when the denominator is zero.

The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT operates on Sales[EmployeeID], meaning it only counts employees who have at least one sales transaction in the filtered period - employees with no sales are invisible to this measure. If your business question is "revenue per headcount," you should be driving the employee count from a separate Employees dimension table instead, otherwise the metric will be inflated whenever staff have gaps in activity.

dax: Average Discount per Order = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[DiscountAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average discount applied per sales order - in other words, how much discount, on average, each order receives in dollar terms. It does this by summing all discount amounts within the current filter context, then dividing by the count of distinct order IDs in that same context, using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that if a single order spans multiple rows in the Sales table (e.g., one row per line item), DISTINCTCOUNT correctly handles that, but if any rows have a null or blank OrderID, those rows are excluded from the denominator while their discount amounts may still be included in the numerator - silently inflating the average.

dax: Sales per Transaction = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), COUNTROWS ( Sales ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per sales transaction - essentially a basket size or ticket-value KPI. It works by summing all SalesAmount values in the current filter context and dividing by the count of rows in that same filtered Sales table, with DIVIDE handling any zero-denominator scenario gracefully by returning BLANK rather than an error. The key watch-out is that COUNTROWS counts every row in the Sales table, so if your data is granular at the line-item level rather than the order level, you're actually computing revenue per line item, not revenue per order - to get true per-transaction averages you'd need to DISTINCTCOUNT on an order ID column instead.

dax: Cost Share of Revenue = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses total cost as a percentage of total revenue, telling you what proportion of every sales dollar is consumed by cost - effectively a cost ratio or inverse gross margin. It sums both columns directly within whatever filter context is active (selected product, region, date range, etc.), so the ratio automatically respects any slicers or report filters applied by the user. DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator to safely return BLANK rather than an error when SalesAmount sums to zero. The key watch-out is that both columns live in the same table, so if your model has row-level filters or RLS that exclude some cost rows but not their corresponding revenue rows (or vice versa), the ratio will be silently distorted - always verify that cost and revenue records are filtered symmetrically.

dax: Refund Rate = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[RefundAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses refunds as a proportion of gross sales - essentially, what percentage of revenue was returned or reversed. It evaluates within whatever filter context is active (report page filters, slicers, visual row/column headers), summing both columns from the Sales table and dividing them using DIVIDE, which safely returns blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The result is a simple ratio, so formatting it as a percentage in the report layer is essential to make it meaningful to business users. The key watch-out is that if RefundAmount and SalesAmount live in the same fact table row - meaning refunds are recorded as negative values within sales transactions rather than as separate rows - the SUM of SalesAmount may already be net of refunds, causing the measure to double-count the deduction and understate the true refund rate.

dax: Category Sales Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Product[Category] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates each product category's share of total sales as a percentage, answering "what fraction of all sales does this category represent?" It divides the category's sales - naturally filtered by whatever category is in context - by a denominator that uses ALL on the Category column to strip that filter and return the grand total across all categories, leaving any other active filters (date range, region, etc.) intact. The key watch-out is that ALL(Product[Category]) only removes the filter on that specific column, not the entire Product table, so if your report also filters on another Product column (like Subcategory or Brand), those filters still apply to the denominator - which may give you a share that sums to less than 100% and can mislead users into thinking the numbers are wrong.

dax: Customer Sales Contribution = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Customer ) ) )
explanation: This measure expresses each customer's sales as a percentage of total sales across all customers - essentially a customer-level share of wallet or contribution metric. It divides the sales amount in the current filter context (e.g., a specific customer or customer segment) by a denominator that strips away all customer filters using ALL(Customer), giving the grand total regardless of which customer is selected. The DIVIDE function handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning BLANK rather than an error. The key watch-out is that ALL(Customer) only removes filters on the Customer table - any other active filters (date range, product, region, etc.) still apply to the denominator, so the "total" you're dividing by is not a true company-wide grand total unless those other slicers are also cleared; this is intentional in many designs but surprises analysts who expect a pure 100%-of-everything figure.

dax: Region Sales Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Geography ) ) )
explanation: This measure expresses each region's sales as a percentage of total sales across all geographies - essentially a "share of total" metric. It works by dividing the sales sum in the current filter context (whatever region, country, or territory is selected) by a denominator that uses ALL(Geography) to strip out any filters on the Geography table, leaving all other active filters (like date or product) intact. The result is a ratio that will correctly re-calculate as you slice by other dimensions, since only Geography filters are removed, not the entire filter context. The key watch-out is that this assumes a clean, active relationship between the Geography table and the Sales table - if Geography isn't the filtering table (e.g., your visuals filter on a region column stored directly in Sales rather than in a related Geography table), ALL(Geography) won't remove those filters, and your denominator will equal your numerator, producing a share of 100% everywhere.

dax: Average Price per Unit = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the weighted average selling price per unit by dividing total revenue by total units sold across whatever filter context is active - a slice by product, region, or time period will naturally scope both the numerator and denominator together. It uses DIVIDE rather than the / operator, which means it safely returns BLANK instead of a division-by-zero error when Quantity sums to zero. The key watch-out is that this is a *weighted* average, not a simple average of unit prices - if you have a Price column and expect this to match an unweighted AVERAGE(Sales[Price]), it won't when sales volumes differ across products or transactions, which can catch analysts off-guard during reconciliation.

dax: Average Cost per Order = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average cost incurred per distinct sales order, giving analysts a sense of how much each order costs the business on average. It works by summing all values in the TotalCost column and dividing that total by the count of unique OrderIDs, both evaluated within whatever filter context is active - slicing by product, region, or date will naturally scope both the numerator and denominator accordingly. DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator to safely return BLANK rather than an error when there are no orders. The key watch-out is that if a single order spans multiple rows in the Sales table, DISTINCTCOUNT correctly handles deduplication - but if TotalCost is stored at the line-item level, the SUM will aggregate all line costs correctly only if each row represents a partial cost; if TotalCost is instead repeated at the order level across rows, you'll inflate the numerator and get a misleading average.

dax: Profit per Unit = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[Profit] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average profit earned for each unit sold - a margin efficiency metric that tells you how much profit the business extracts per item. It works by summing total profit and total quantity across whatever filter context is active (selected product, region, time period, etc.), then safely dividing them using DIVIDE, which returns blank rather than an error if quantity is zero. The one watch-out is that this is a ratio of sums, not an average of row-level ratios - so when aggregated across a mix of products with very different volumes, high-volume low-margin products will dominate the result, which may not reflect the "typical" per-unit experience across the product range.

dax: Orders per Customer = DIVIDE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average number of distinct orders placed per distinct customer, giving analysts a quick read on purchasing frequency within any given filter context. It works by independently counting unique order IDs and unique customer keys from the Sales table, then safely dividing the two - DIVIDE handles the zero-denominator case by returning BLANK rather than an error. Both counts respond fully to whatever slicers, page filters, or row context is active, so the ratio will shift as filters change. The key watch-out is that both counts draw only from rows that exist in the Sales table, meaning customers who have never placed an order are silently excluded from the denominator - if your model has a separate Customers dimension with rows for inactive customers, this measure will overstate frequency because those zero-order customers never appear in Sales.

dax: Freight % of Sales = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[FreightCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses freight cost as a proportion of revenue, telling you how many cents of every sales dollar is consumed by shipping. It sums both columns directly within whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, or report-level filters all shape which rows are included before the aggregation happens - and DIVIDE handles any zero-denominator scenario by returning blank rather than a hard error. The one watch-out is that both columns live in the same table, so the ratio is always computed over the same filtered set of rows; if your model ever routes freight and sales amounts through different tables or relationships, the implicit filter paths may diverge and produce a misleading blended ratio that doesn't reflect a true like-for-like comparison.

dax: Online Sales Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Sales[Channel] = "Online" ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the proportion of total sales revenue that came through the online channel, expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.35 for 35%). It works by using CALCULATE to override the channel filter in the current filter context, hard-coding it to "Online" to get the online subtotal, then dividing that by the unfiltered sum of all sales within whatever other filters are active (date, region, product, etc.). DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator to safely return BLANK rather than an error when the denominator is zero.

The key watch-out is that if a report user applies a slicer or filter that already restricts the Channel field - for example, selecting only "Store" - the numerator will return BLANK (no online sales pass through), making the measure show BLANK or 0% rather than signalling that the filter is conflicting; this can silently mislead users who don't notice their channel filter is active.

dax: Store Sales Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Sales[Channel] = "Store" ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates each store's (or any sliced dimension's) share of total sales that came through the physical "Store" channel, expressed as a proportion between 0 and 1. The numerator uses CALCULATE to layer a hard filter restricting SalesAmount to Store-channel rows only, while the denominator sums SalesAmount under whatever filter context already exists from the report (slicers, rows, columns, etc.) - so both parts naturally respond to any external filters like date, region, or product. DIVIDE safely handles division by zero, returning BLANK() rather than an error when the denominator is zero.

The key watch-out is that the denominator reflects the *current filter context*, not necessarily all-channel grand total sales - so if a user slices the report by Channel and selects "Online," the denominator drops to zero (or near zero), making the ratio either BLANK or misleading rather than showing Store as a share of the true total. If the intent is always "Store sales as a share of ALL channels regardless of any Channel slicer," wrap the denominator in CALCULATE(SUM(Sales[SalesAmount]), ALL(Sales[Channel])).

dax: New Customer Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Customer[CustomerType] = "New" ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the proportion of total revenue that comes from customers classified as "New," expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.23 for 23%). The numerator uses CALCULATE to override the current filter context and isolate sales where CustomerType equals "New," while the denominator simply sums all sales under whatever filters are already active - a date range, region, product, etc. - so the ratio naturally respects any slicers or report filters applied by the user. DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator to safely return BLANK rather than an error when total sales is zero.

The key watch-out is that this measure assumes CustomerType is a column on a Customer dimension table that has a many-to-one relationship with the Sales fact table; if CustomerType lives directly on the Sales table, the CALCULATE filter would still work but could behave unexpectedly if a single customer has rows with mixed types, potentially double-counting or misclassifying revenue - so confirm the relationship cardinality and where "New" is defined before trusting the output.

dax: Discount per Unit = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[DiscountAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average discount given per unit sold - essentially how much discount, in currency, is being applied for every unit across the current filter context (selected product, region, time period, etc.). It works by summing all discount amounts and all quantities within whatever filter context is active, then safely dividing the two totals using DIVIDE, which returns BLANK rather than an error if the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that this is a ratio of sums, not an average of per-row ratios - meaning it weights heavily toward high-volume rows, so if you have a mix of high-quantity/low-discount and low-quantity/high-discount transactions, the result can look very different from what stakeholders expect when they think "average discount per unit."

dax: Net Revenue Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[NetSales] ), SUM ( Sales[GrossSales] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses what proportion of gross sales is retained after deductions - discounts, returns, allowances - essentially a revenue quality or leakage indicator. It simply sums both columns within whatever filter context is active (product, region, period, etc.) and divides them using DIVIDE, which safely returns blank instead of an error when gross sales is zero. Because it operates on column-level sums rather than iterating row by row, the ratio reflects the aggregate relationship across all rows in context, not an average of individual transaction ratios - which is the right behaviour for a weighted metric like this. The key watch-out is that if GrossSales ever contains zeros or nulls for legitimate transactions (e.g., fully comped orders), DIVIDE will return blank for those slices, potentially hiding gaps in your data rather than surfacing them.

dax: Product Sales Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Product ) ) )
explanation: This measure expresses each product's (or product group's) sales as a percentage of total sales across all products - essentially a product-level contribution-to-total metric. It works by dividing the sales amount in the current filter context against a recalculated total where ALL(Product) strips out any filters on the Product table, leaving all other filters (date, region, customer, etc.) intact. That means the denominator is not necessarily grand-total revenue but rather the total for whatever non-product filters are active - so if a user filters to "Q1" and "North," the denominator is Q1 North revenue across all products, which is usually the desired behavior. The key watch-out is that ALL(Product) only removes filters originating from the Product table; if product attributes are stored directly on the Sales fact table rather than in a related Product dimension, those column filters won't be cleared and the denominator will be understated, silently breaking the percentage calculation.

dax: Sales per Geography = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[GeographyKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average sales amount per distinct geographic area - in other words, how much revenue each geography contributes on average within whatever filter context is active (e.g., a selected region, time period, or product category). It works by summing all SalesAmount values in the filtered Sales table, then dividing that total by the count of unique GeographyKey values present in the same filtered rows, with DIVIDE handling any division-by-zero gracefully by returning blank. The key watch-out is that GeographyKey is counted from the Sales fact table itself, not from a Geography dimension, so geographies that exist in your model but have zero sales transactions will be silently excluded from the denominator - this can inflate the per-geography average if you expect all geographies to be counted regardless of activity.

dax: Returned Orders Rate = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ), Sales[IsReturned] = TRUE () ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the proportion of distinct orders that were returned, expressed as a rate between 0 and 1. The denominator counts all unique order IDs under the current filter context, while the numerator uses CALCULATE to layer an additional filter keeping only rows where IsReturned is TRUE, then counts distinct order IDs within that subset. DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator to safely return BLANK (rather than an error) when no orders exist in context.

The key watch-out is that IsReturned must be a row-level flag on the Sales table itself - if an order spans multiple rows (e.g., line items) and only some rows are marked as returned, DISTINCTCOUNT in the numerator will count that OrderID as "returned" even if just one line was flagged, which can overstate the return rate depending on how partial returns are modelled.

dax: Warranty Cost Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[WarrantyCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses warranty costs as a proportion of revenue, telling you how many cents of every sales dollar are consumed by warranty obligations. It sums both columns directly from the Sales table within whatever filter context is active - slicers, row context in a matrix, report-level filters - and safely divides them using DIVIDE, which returns BLANK instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that both columns live in the same table, so any row-level filter (say, a product category slicer) will simultaneously filter both the numerator and denominator - which is usually correct, but if warranty costs are logged against different rows or a different granularity than sales transactions, the ratio will silently misalign and produce misleading results.

dax: Subcategory Sales Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Product[SubCategory] ) ) )
explanation: This measure shows what percentage of total sales each product subcategory contributes, expressed as a ratio between 0 and 1. It divides the subcategory's sales - naturally filtered by whatever subcategory is in context - by total sales recalculated with all subcategory filters removed via ALL, while preserving any other active filters such as date, region, or product category. DIVIDE handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning BLANK rather than an error when no sales exist. The key watch-out is that ALL removes only the SubCategory column filter, not the entire Product table, so if your report also filters on Category or another Product column, those filters remain in the denominator - meaning shares will sum to 100% within a category slice, not necessarily across the whole dataset, which may or may not match your intended business logic.

dax: Average Selling Price = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the revenue-weighted average selling price per unit - in other words, total sales revenue divided by total units sold for whatever slice of data is in context. It works by summing the SalesAmount and Quantity columns across all rows visible in the current filter context (by date, product, region, etc.) and then safely dividing the two totals using DIVIDE, which returns blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. Because it divides aggregated totals rather than averaging individual row-level prices, it produces a weighted average that reflects actual sales mix rather than a simple mean of unit prices. The key watch-out is that this weighting behaviour means the measure will shift when the product or channel mix changes - a period-over-period movement in ASP may reflect a mix shift rather than a genuine price change, which can mislead analysts who assume it tracks pure pricing.

dax: Revenue per Region = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Geography[Region] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue spread evenly across distinct regions - essentially a per-region revenue figure useful for benchmarking or normalising sales performance. It sums every SalesAmount row in the current filter context, then divides by the count of unique Region values visible in that same context, using DIVIDE to handle a zero-denominator gracefully. The critical watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT operates on the Geography table, so if your model has regions with no sales (or your relationships are configured with a cross-filter direction that leaves orphaned geography rows), the denominator may count regions that contributed zero revenue, silently deflating the metric in ways that are hard to spot without inspecting the data directly.

dax: Cost per Customer = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average total cost incurred per unique customer - useful for understanding cost efficiency or customer profitability. It sums every row's TotalCost in the Sales table and divides that by the count of distinct CustomerKey values, both evaluated within whatever filter context is active (a specific product, region, time period, etc.). DIVIDE is used instead of the native division operator, so the measure safely returns blank rather than an error when there are no customers in context. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT counts distinct CustomerKey values present in the Sales table, not in a customer dimension - so a customer who made no purchases is excluded from the denominator, which is usually correct but can distort the metric if your business definition of "customer" includes prospects or inactive accounts.

dax: Profit per Customer = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[Profit] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the average profit generated per unique customer, giving a sense of how much value each customer contributes on average. It sums all profit values in the current filter context, then divides by the count of distinct customer keys within that same context - so it naturally slices correctly by region, product, time period, or any other slicer on your report. The division is wrapped in DIVIDE rather than the / operator, which silently returns blank instead of a hard error when there are no customers.

The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT operates on CustomerKey values present in the Sales table, not in a separate Customers dimension - so any customer who exists in your customer master but has zero sales in the current filter window is excluded from the denominator, which will inflate the metric compared to a definition that counts all active or registered customers.

dax: Fulfillment Cost Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[FulfillmentCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses fulfillment cost as a percentage of revenue, answering the business question "how many cents of fulfillment cost does it take to generate each dollar of sales." It works by summing both columns across whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, or report-level filters - then safely dividing the two totals, returning blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. Because both aggregations happen over the same Sales table in the same filter context, the ratio is always revenue-weighted, meaning it behaves correctly when rows are filtered but will naturally aggregate differently than a simple average of row-level ratios would. The key watch-out is that if any rows have a null or zero SalesAmount - refunds, adjustments, or data-quality issues - those rows still contribute their FulfillmentCost to the numerator while adding nothing to the denominator, which can silently inflate the ratio beyond what the business expects.

dax: Quantity Share of Total = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ), ALL ( Sales ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates each slice's share of overall quantity sold - for example, what percentage of total units a given product, region, or time period represents. It divides the quantity in the current filter context by the grand total quantity, where ALL(Sales) strips every filter from the entire Sales table to produce that denominator. The result is a ratio between 0 and 1 (multiply by 100 or format as percentage for display). The key watch-out is that ALL(Sales) removes filters on all columns in Sales, so if users apply a slicer that filters a related dimension table (e.g., a Product or Date table), those cross-table filters still flow in through relationships and will affect the denominator - meaning the "total" shifts to match the slicer selection rather than a true unconditional grand total. If a fixed, absolute grand total is required regardless of any slicer, use ALL on each relevant related table or switch to ALLSELECTED deliberately based on the intended business logic.

dax: Orders per Region = DIVIDE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Geography[Region] ) )
