explanation: This measure calculates the average number of distinct orders per distinct region, giving analysts a sense of order volume density across the geographic footprint. It works by independently counting unique Order IDs across all visible Sales rows and unique Region values across all visible Geography rows, then dividing the first by the second - both counts respect whatever filter context is currently active (e.g., a slicer on year or product). The critical watch-out is that the Region count comes from the Geography table, not from regions that actually appear in Sales, so if your filter context leaves Geography unfiltered while Sales is filtered, you may be dividing by more regions than actually have orders - inflating the denominator and understating the average. Ensure your model's relationships propagate filters correctly from Sales to Geography, or consider anchoring the denominator to DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[Region]) if you only want regions with activity.

dax: Gross Sales per Customer = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[GrossSales] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average gross sales revenue generated per unique customer - a per-customer productivity metric useful for segmentation or trend analysis. It works by summing all gross sales values in the current filter context and dividing that total by the count of distinct customer keys within that same context, using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. One important watch-out: DISTINCTCOUNT operates on CustomerKey values present in the Sales table, so if a customer made no purchases in the filtered period they won't appear in the denominator - this is usually the intended behaviour, but it means the measure reflects "average among buying customers only" and will silently exclude inactive customers, which can make the metric look healthier than a true customer-base average would suggest.

dax: Handling Cost % = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[HandlingCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: Handling Cost % expresses handling cost as a proportion of revenue, telling you how many cents of every sales dollar are consumed by handling. It works by summing both columns across whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, report-page filters - then dividing them with DIVIDE, which safely returns blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that this measure is a ratio of sums, not a sum of ratios, so it behaves correctly when aggregated across multiple rows or segments; however, if your Sales table contains negative SalesAmount values (returns, credits, adjustments), the denominator can shrink or flip sign, producing a percentage that looks absurd or misleading without additional context.

dax: List Price to Sale Price Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[ListPrice] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses how much of the theoretical list-price revenue was actually captured in sales, giving you a ratio like 0.85 meaning customers paid 85 cents for every dollar of list price - a proxy for discount depth across any slice of the data. It simply sums the actual sales amount and divides it by the sum of list prices within whatever filter context is active (product, date, region, etc.), using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. One key watch-out: this is a ratio of sums, not an average of per-row ratios, so it weights high-volume rows more heavily - if your goal is a true average discount rate per transaction, you'd need to iterate row by row with AVERAGEX instead.

dax: Revenue per Category = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[Category] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue spread across product categories - essentially, how much sales revenue each distinct category contributes on average. It sums all SalesAmount values in the current filter context, then divides that by the count of unique Category values visible in that same context, using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT operates on the Product table's Category column, so if your model has an inactive or broken relationship between Sales and Product, the category count may reflect all categories in the Product table rather than only those with actual sales - inflating the denominator and understating the per-category figure.

dax: Marketing Cost Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[MarketingCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses marketing spend as a proportion of revenue - essentially, how many cents of every sales dollar went to marketing. It sums both columns directly from the Sales table, so the ratio responds naturally to whatever filter context is active (date slicer, product category, region, etc.), making it safe to use in visuals and as a matrix value. DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator to silently return blank rather than an error when SalesAmount sums to zero. The key watch-out is that both figures come from the same table, so any row-level filter applied to Sales affects numerator and denominator together - if MarketingCost is only populated for certain rows (e.g., specific product lines or campaigns), those blank rows will still contribute to the SalesAmount denominator, potentially understating the true ratio for the segments that do carry marketing cost.

dax: Transactions per Customer = DIVIDE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average number of sales transactions each customer has made - a simple activity or engagement rate metric. It works entirely within whatever filter context is active (report page filters, slicers, visual headers), counting all rows in the Sales table and dividing by the number of unique customers appearing in those same rows, using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of a divide-by-zero error when no data exists. The key watch-out is that both the numerator and denominator draw from the same Sales table, so a customer only gets counted if they have at least one transaction in the current filter context - customers who exist in a separate Customers dimension table but have no sales in scope are silently excluded, which means the denominator can shrink dramatically under heavy filtering and produce a misleadingly high ratio.

dax: Revenue per Transaction = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), COUNTROWS ( Sales ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per individual sales transaction, giving analysts a quick read on ticket size or deal value. It works by summing the total sales amount across all rows visible in the current filter context, then dividing by the count of those same rows using DIVIDE, which safely returns blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that COUNTROWS counts every row in the filtered Sales table, so if your table has multiple line items per order and you actually want revenue per order rather than per line item, this measure will understate the true per-transaction figure - you'd need to count distinct transaction or order IDs instead.

dax: Profit Margin per Unit = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average profit earned per unit sold - in other words, how much gross margin the business generates for every single unit moved. It works by first subtracting total cost from total revenue within whatever filter context is active (a product, region, time period, etc.), then dividing that net profit figure by total quantity using DIVIDE, which safely returns blank instead of an error when quantity is zero. The result is a per-unit margin in currency terms, not a percentage, so it will shift as the product or customer mix changes within your slicer selections. The key watch-out is that this is a simple weighted-average blended rate across all rows in context - if your data contains both high-margin and low-margin products, the measure will obscure that spread, and you should resist interpreting it as representative of any single SKU without filtering down appropriately.

dax: Customer Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Customer ) ) )
explanation: This measure shows each customer's percentage contribution to total revenue - in other words, how much of the overall sales pot belongs to the customer(s) currently in focus. It divides the sales amount filtered by the current context (e.g., a specific customer in a table row) by a denominator calculated with ALL(Customer) stripping away any customer filters, so the denominator always reflects the grand total across all customers. The DIVIDE function handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning blank instead of an error when there are no sales at all. The key watch-out is that ALL(Customer) only removes filters on the Customer table - filters from other tables like Date, Product, or Region still apply to the denominator, meaning the "total" is scoped to whatever else is filtered on the report, which is often the intended behaviour but can surprise users who expect a true all-up company total regardless of any slicer selections.

dax: Product Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Product ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates each product's (or product selection's) share of total revenue - essentially, what percentage of overall sales the current filter context's products represent. It does this by dividing the sales amount under the current filter context by a denominator that uses ALL(Product) to strip out any filters on the Product table, so the denominator always reflects the grand total across all products regardless of how the visual is sliced. The DIVIDE function safely handles division by zero, returning BLANK rather than an error when no sales exist. The key watch-out is that ALL(Product) only removes filters from the Product table - filters on other tables such as Date, Region, or Customer remain intact, so the "total" in the denominator is scoped to whatever other slicers are active, which is usually the intended behaviour but can surprise users who expect a true unconditional grand total.

dax: Geography Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( 'Geography' ) ) )
explanation: This measure answers "what percentage of total revenue comes from the currently selected geography?" by expressing the filtered sales amount as a share of the grand total across all geographies. It works by dividing the naturally filtered SUM of SalesAmount - which respects whatever geography is in context - by a CALCULATE that strips all filters off the Geography table using ALL, giving the company-wide revenue as the denominator. The result is a clean ratio that will display correctly in a matrix or chart sliced by any geography attribute. The key watch-out is that ALL('Geography') only removes filters on that specific table, so if other active filters exist - say a date slicer or a product category filter - those will still constrain both numerator and denominator, meaning the share is relative to the filtered subtotal, not the absolute company grand total; if you need the true all-up denominator regardless of any filter, you'd need ALL applied to every relevant table or use ALLSELECTED deliberately.

dax: Channel Revenue Mix % = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Sales[Channel] ) ) )
explanation: This measure tells you what share of total revenue a given channel contributes - for example, if Online drives $4M out of $10M company-wide, it returns 40%. It works by dividing the sales amount in the current filter context (which includes whatever channel slice is active) by a denominator that strips the channel filter using ALL, while keeping every other filter - date, region, product, etc. - intact, so the percentage is always relative to the visible subtotal, not the grand total. The key watch-out is that the denominator removes filters only on Sales[Channel], meaning if your report has cross-filters or slicers hitting other columns on the Sales table, those will still affect the denominator; if you intended a truly fixed company-wide total regardless of other slicers, you'd need ALL(Sales) or ALLSELECTED(Sales[Channel]) depending on the desired behaviour.

dax: Returns to Sales Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[ReturnQuantity] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses what proportion of sold units were subsequently returned - essentially a return rate - giving the business a signal on product quality, fulfilment accuracy, or customer satisfaction. It works by summing the ReturnQuantity and Quantity columns across whatever filter context is currently active (selected product, date range, region, etc.) and then safely dividing the two totals, returning blank rather than an error when the denominator is zero. The big watch-out is that it assumes both columns live in the same Sales table and that return transactions are recorded as positive values in ReturnQuantity rather than as negative rows in Quantity - if your data model captures returns as negative Quantity entries instead, this measure will double-count the impact and silently overstate the ratio.

dax: Revenue per Customer = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per unique customer - a per-capita revenue metric useful for tracking customer value over time or across segments. It works by summing all SalesAmount values in the current filter context, then dividing by the count of distinct CustomerKeys in that same context, with DIVIDE safely returning BLANK instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that CustomerKey is sourced entirely from the Sales fact table, meaning customers who exist in your Customer dimension but have made no purchases in the current filter context are silently excluded from the denominator - if you want to include all customers (e.g., to account for zero-revenue customers), you'd need to pull the distinct count from the dimension table instead.

dax: Revenue per Product = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[ProductKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per distinct product sold, giving analysts a sense of how much each product contributes to total sales on average. It sums all sales amounts in the current filter context, then divides by the count of unique product keys present in the same filtered Sales table - so both the numerator and denominator respond to whatever slicers, rows, or filters are active. DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator to safely return a blank rather than an error when no rows exist. The key watch-out is that the denominator counts distinct ProductKeys in the Sales table, not in the Products dimension - so any product that exists in your catalog but has zero sales will be silently excluded, potentially inflating the per-product average and misleading comparisons across time periods or segments where product participation varies.

dax: Profit per Order = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average profit earned per sales order - essentially how much margin the business generates on each transaction. It computes gross profit by subtracting total cost from total revenue within the current filter context, then divides that figure by the count of distinct order IDs in the same context, using DIVIDE to return blank rather than an error if the denominator is zero. Watch out for orders that span multiple rows in the Sales table: DISTINCTCOUNT handles that correctly, but if OrderID is ever null or blank, those rows will be silently counted as a single "blank order," inflating profit per order and masking data quality issues.

dax: Freight per Unit = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[FreightCost] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average freight cost incurred for every unit sold, giving you a per-unit logistics cost that can be sliced by any dimension in your model such as product, region, or time period. It works by summing all freight costs and all quantities within the current filter context, then safely dividing the two totals using DIVIDE, which returns BLANK rather than an error if the denominator is zero. The result is a simple ratio of aggregates, not an average of per-row ratios, so it correctly weights high-volume rows rather than treating each order line equally. The key watch-out is that this measure computes a weighted average at whatever granularity the filter context defines, meaning totals and subtotals will reflect the blended rate across all rows in context - not an average of the visible line-level values - which can surprise users who expect row-level figures to simply average up.

dax: Tax per Unit = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[TaxAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average tax charged per unit sold, giving analysts a sense of the effective tax burden on each item across whatever slice of the data is in context. It works by summing total tax amount and total quantity within the current filter context - no iteration or time intelligence involved - and uses DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when quantity sums to zero. The key watch-out is that this is a weighted-average-style ratio computed on aggregated totals, not a true per-row average, so if your data mixes product lines or tax rates with very different quantities, the result can be skewed by high-volume, low-tax items in a way that may not represent the "typical" unit's tax - always validate whether you need a row-level average (AVERAGEX) instead.

dax: Net Revenue per Customer = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[NetRevenue] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average net revenue generated per unique customer - a standard revenue productivity metric. It sums all net revenue in the current filter context and divides it by the count of distinct customer keys present in that same filtered dataset, so slicing by region, time period, or product automatically scopes both the numerator and denominator together. DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator to gracefully return BLANK rather than an error when no rows exist. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT counts distinct CustomerKeys appearing in the Sales table, not all customers in a Customer dimension table - if a customer made no purchases in the filtered period, they are silently excluded from the denominator, which inflates the metric compared to a "per active-and-inactive customer" calculation.

dax: Operating Cost Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[OperatingCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses operating costs as a proportion of revenue, telling you how many cents of operating cost are consumed for every dollar of sales. It simply sums both columns within whatever filter context is active - slicers, row/column headers, report-level filters - and divides them using DIVIDE, which safely returns BLANK instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that both columns live in the same Sales table, so the ratio is computed on the same filtered rows; if your model ever splits costs and revenue across different tables or granularities, the implicit filter context may no longer align them correctly, producing a ratio that silently overstates or understates the true figure.

dax: Promotion Discount Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[PromotionDiscount] ), SUM ( Sales[GrossSales] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses what fraction of gross sales revenue was given away as promotional discounts - essentially a discount rate that tells you how aggressively promotions are eroding revenue. It works by summing the PromotionDiscount column and dividing it by total GrossSales within whatever filter context is active (product, date, region, etc.), using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when GrossSales is zero. The key watch-out is that both columns are aggregated independently before dividing, so in a matrix or when used inside an iterator this will always give you the correct ratio for the current context - but if PromotionDiscount can be non-zero on rows where GrossSales is zero (e.g., credit memos or adjustments), those rows will silently inflate the numerator while contributing nothing to the denominator, causing the ratio to overstate the true discount share.

dax: Online Sales Mix % = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Sales[Channel] = "Online" ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates what share of total sales revenue came through the online channel, expressed as a decimal (multiply by 100 for a percentage). It works by first using CALCULATE to override the current filter context and lock the channel to "Online," summing only those sales, then dividing that figure by the grand total SUM of SalesAmount which respects whatever filters are already applied from slicers, rows, or columns in your report. The key watch-out is that the denominator is not hardcoded to all-channel total - it responds to every other active filter, so if a user slices by region or product, both numerator and denominator shrink accordingly, which is usually the desired behavior but can surprise people who expect the denominator to always represent company-wide revenue.

dax: Repeat Customer Rate = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ), Sales[OrderCount] > 1 ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the proportion of customers who have placed more than one order - essentially your repeat or returning customer rate as a percentage of your total customer base. It counts distinct customers whose order count exceeds one, then divides that by the total distinct customer count in the current filter context, using DIVIDE to handle a zero denominator gracefully. The filter Sales[OrderCount] > 1 is applied via CALCULATE, so it subsets the Sales table to rows where that column's value is greater than one before counting distinct customers.

The key watch-out is that Sales[OrderCount] must be a pre-aggregated column on the Sales table - a value already computed per customer and stamped on every row - rather than a dynamic row count; if multiple rows exist per customer with varying or misleading OrderCount values, the filter will behave unexpectedly and likely overcount or undercount repeat customers. A more robust approach would derive order counts dynamically rather than relying on a stored column.

dax: New Customer Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Customer[IsNewCustomer] = TRUE () ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the percentage of total revenue attributable to new customers - in other words, what share of sales came from customers flagged as first-time buyers. It works by using CALCULATE to filter the Sales table to only rows linked to customers where IsNewCustomer is TRUE, summing their SalesAmount, then dividing that by the unfiltered total SalesAmount in the current filter context; DIVIDE handles any division-by-zero gracefully. The critical watch-out is that IsNewCustomer is a static column on the Customer table, so "new" is determined at data-load time rather than dynamically relative to the report's date selection - if a user filters to a specific month, customers marked new for that month may not match the column's definition, potentially overstating or understating the share depending on how the column was built.

dax: Category Revenue Concentration = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Product[Category] ) ) )
explanation: This measure expresses the current category's share of total revenue - in other words, what percentage of all sales a given product category represents. It works by dividing the sales amount under the existing filter context (typically a single category in a visual) by a denominator that uses ALL to strip the category filter and sum revenue across every category, giving a clean grand total. DIVIDE handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning BLANK rather than an error when no sales exist. The key watch-out is that ALL removes only the Category column filter, so any other active filters - such as date range, region, or sales channel - still apply to the denominator; this is intentional for most use cases but can produce counterintuitive results if users expect the denominator to always reflect absolute company-wide revenue regardless of slicer selections.

dax: Subcategory Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALLEXCEPT ( Product, Product[Category] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates each product subcategory's share of revenue within its parent category - in other words, "what percentage of this category's total sales does this subcategory represent?" The numerator is the plain SUM of sales in the current filter context (subcategory, date, region, etc.), while the denominator uses ALLEXCEPT to strip away every filter on the Product table except Category, so it always sums to the full category total regardless of which subcategory is selected. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters on the Product table - any other filters in context (e.g., a date slicer or region filter) still apply to the denominator, which is usually the right behaviour but can produce unexpected results if users expect the share to be calculated against an unfiltered category total; also, if your model has relationships from other tables filtering Product indirectly, those filters will still flow through and may silently skew the denominator.

dax: Sales per Store = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[StoreKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per store by dividing total sales amount by the number of distinct stores that appear in the Sales table. It evaluates both the SUM and DISTINCTCOUNT within whatever filter context is active - slicers, row context in a matrix, or report-level filters - so the result automatically scopes to the visible subset of data. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT counts only stores that have at least one sales row in the current context, meaning stores with zero sales are silently excluded from the denominator; if you want true average-per-store including non-trading locations, you'd need to drive the store count from a separate Stores dimension table instead.

dax: Units per Customer = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average number of units sold per unique customer - a density metric useful for understanding purchasing intensity across a segment or time period. It works by summing all quantity values in the current filter context, then dividing by the count of distinct customer keys in that same context, using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT operates on the Sales fact table, so it counts customers who appear in at least one transaction - if a customer exists in your Customer dimension but made no purchases in the filtered period, they are silently excluded from the denominator, which inflates the average compared to a true "customers in segment" calculation.

dax: Gross Sales per Rep = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[GrossSales] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[SalesRepKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average gross sales revenue generated per individual sales representative. It sums every gross sales amount in the current filter context, then divides that total by the count of distinct sales rep identifiers present in the same filtered dataset, using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error if the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT operates on the Sales fact table itself, so any rep who exists in a dimension table but has zero transactions in the current context is silently excluded from the denominator - this can inflate the average and produce misleading results if you're expecting headcount-based averaging that includes reps with no sales.

dax: Warranty Cost % = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[WarrantyCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation:
This measure expresses warranty costs as a percentage of revenue by dividing total warranty cost by total sales amount for whatever filter context is active - a slice, a time period, a product category, and so on. Both SUM aggregations run over the same filtered rows of the Sales table simultaneously, so the ratio always reflects the same scope without any extra iterator or time intelligence involved. The result is a simple, well-behaved ratio that works cleanly in most visuals and slicers.

The key watch-out is that DIVIDE silently returns BLANK (its default alternate result) when SalesAmount sums to zero, which can cause rows or periods with no revenue to disappear from visuals rather than showing a visible zero or error - confirm whether BLANK or an explicit fallback value (e.g., DIVIDE(..., ..., 0)) is the right behaviour for your reporting requirements.

dax: Rebate % of Revenue = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[RebateAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the proportion of revenue that is paid out as rebates - essentially, for every dollar of sales, how many cents are given back to customers or partners. It simply sums the RebateAmount column and divides it by the sum of SalesAmount within whatever filter context is active (product, region, time period, etc.), using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when SalesAmount is zero. The key watch-out is that both columns live in the same Sales table, so if your model filters out rows - for example, through row-level security, a slicer, or a relationship - both the numerator and denominator are affected symmetrically, which is usually correct; however, if rebates and sales are ever stored in separate tables or if some sales rows legitimately carry a null RebateAmount (rather than zero), those rows will be silently excluded from the numerator while still contributing to the denominator, understating the rebate rate.

dax: Overhead Absorption Rate = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[OverheadCost] ), SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses what proportion of total costs is made up of overhead, giving finance teams a quick read on how heavily overhead is loaded into the cost base. It sums both the overhead cost and total cost columns directly from the Sales table, then divides them safely using DIVIDE, which returns blank rather than an error when the denominator is zero. The calculation inherits whatever filter context is active - slicers, row context in a matrix, date filters - so it will correctly recalculate the ratio for any slice of the data without additional modification. The key watch-out is that both columns live in the Sales table, meaning if a transaction has a null or zero TotalCost (perhaps due to upstream data quality issues or free/sample orders), those rows silently distort the ratio; you should confirm whether such rows should be excluded or handled explicitly rather than relying on DIVIDE's default blank-for-zero behaviour to mask the problem.

dax: Material Cost Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[MaterialCost] ), SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ) )
explanation: Material Cost Ratio expresses what share of total cost is made up of material cost - for example, a result of 0.72 means 72 cents of every cost dollar is attributable to materials. It works by summing both columns across whatever filter context is active (selected product, region, time period, etc.) and then safely dividing the two totals, returning BLANK instead of an error when TotalCost sums to zero. The key watch-out is that because both columns live in the same Sales table, the ratio is always computed on the same filtered rows, which is correct - but if either column contains NULLs, those rows are silently excluded from the SUM, potentially understating both numerator and denominator and skewing the ratio without any visible warning.

dax: Labor Cost Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[LaborCost] ), SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses labor cost as a proportion of total cost - essentially telling you how much of every cost dollar is attributable to labor. It works by summing both the LaborCost and TotalCost columns from the Sales table within whatever filter context is active (slice by region, product, period, etc.), then safely dividing them using DIVIDE, which returns BLANK instead of an error when TotalCost sums to zero. The key watch-out is that both columns live in the same Sales table, so if rows are filtered out - say by a slicer or a relationship - both numerator and denominator are filtered together, which is usually correct; however, if TotalCost contains nulls or zeros at the row level, those rows still contribute zero to the denominator and could quietly skew the ratio without raising any obvious errors.

dax: Contribution Margin % = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[VariableCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates contribution margin as a percentage of revenue, telling you what share of each sales dollar remains after covering variable costs to contribute toward fixed costs and profit. It works by summing SalesAmount and VariableCost across whatever filter context is active - whether that's a product, region, time period, or the full dataset - then dividing the resulting margin by total revenue using DIVIDE, which safely returns blank instead of an error when revenue is zero. The key watch-out is that the result is only as reliable as the classification of costs in the VariableCost column: if any fixed or semi-variable costs have been loaded into that field, the margin will be overstated or understated, and since there's no explicit relationship handling here, costs and sales must already be joined correctly in the model for the row-level filtering to produce meaningful segment-level results.

dax: Cancelled Order Rate = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ), Sales[OrderStatus] = "Cancelled" ), COUNTROWS ( Sales ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the proportion of sales orders that have a status of "Cancelled" out of all orders in the current filter context, expressed as a decimal ratio. It works by using CALCULATE to override the OrderStatus filter and count only cancelled rows, then divides that by an unfiltered COUNTROWS of the Sales table - both counts still respect any external filters on dates, regions, products, or other slicers applied in the report. DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator, so the measure safely returns BLANK rather than an error when there are no rows in the denominator.

The key watch-out is that the denominator counts every visible row in Sales, including rows with NULL or unexpected OrderStatus values - if your data contains statuses like "Pending", "Draft", or unclassified records that should arguably be excluded from the universe of "countable" orders, the denominator will be inflated and the rate will be understated. Confirm with the business whether the denominator should be all orders or only "completed" orders (e.g., Cancelled + Delivered + Shipped) before deploying this measure.

dax: Fulfilled Order Rate = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ), Sales[OrderStatus] = "Fulfilled" ), COUNTROWS ( Sales ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the percentage of sales orders that have been fulfilled, expressed as a ratio between fulfilled rows and total rows in the Sales table. It works by applying an additional filter for OrderStatus = "Fulfilled" on top of whatever filter context already exists (from slicers, report pages, or visuals), then divides that count by the unfiltered row count of Sales within the same context - DIVIDE handles the zero-denominator case gracefully by returning blank instead of an error. The key watch-out is that COUNTROWS(Sales) in the denominator counts all rows in the current filter context, so if your model or a slicer already filters out non-fulfilled statuses upstream, the denominator will be artificially reduced and the rate will appear inflated - always verify that the Sales table retains all order statuses before this measure is evaluated.

dax: Average Basket Size = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue per order - how much a typical transaction is worth - by dividing total sales amount by the number of unique orders in the current filter context. It uses DIVIDE rather than the division operator so that a zero denominator (no orders in scope) safely returns blank instead of an error. Both the SUM and DISTINCTCOUNT respond to whatever slicers, row context, or report filters are active, so the result automatically recalculates for any product, region, or time period a user selects. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT counts distinct OrderIDs visible in the filtered Sales table, so if your model has a many-to-many order relationship or duplicate OrderID rows across different tables, the denominator can be inflated or misleading - make sure OrderIDs are clean and your relationships are modelled correctly before trusting this figure.

dax: Revenue per Sales Day = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( 'Date'[Date] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue earned on each day that appears in the current filter context - essentially a daily revenue run rate for whatever time period, product, or region the user is looking at. It works by summing all sales amounts and dividing by the count of distinct calendar dates visible in the Date table after filters are applied, using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The critical watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT counts every date present in the filtered Date table, not just dates that actually have sales - so if your Date table contains future dates or non-trading days within the selected period, those will inflate the denominator and deflate the per-day figure, making the measure misleading unless you filter or mark the Date table to include only true selling days.

dax: Profit per Customer = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average profit generated per unique customer by dividing total gross profit (sales minus cost) by the number of distinct customers within the current filter context. Both the profit numerator and the customer count denominator respond to whatever slicers, filters, or visual context are active, so the result naturally adjusts when you filter by region, product, or time period. DIVIDE is used instead of the division operator, which silently returns blank rather than an error when the customer count is zero - a safe default in most reports. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT counts customers who appear in the Sales table, not your full customer dimension, so if a customer made no purchases in the filtered period they are excluded from the denominator, which inflates the metric - this may or may not reflect your intended business definition of "active customer."

dax: Product Mix Index = DIVIDE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[ProductKey] ), CALCULATE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[ProductKey] ), ALL ( Sales ) ) )
explanation:
This measure expresses the share of distinct products sold in the current filter context as a fraction of all distinct products ever sold across the entire Sales table. It works by dividing the filtered distinct product count - shaped by whatever slicers, rows, or columns are active - against a denominator that strips all filters from Sales using ALL, giving a stable total. The result is a value between 0 and 1 representing how much of the full product catalogue appears in the current selection.

The key watch-out is that ALL removes filters only from the Sales table, so if your report also filters on a related dimension table (e.g., a Date or Product table), those filters are not removed from the denominator - meaning the "total" can silently shrink and the index will no longer represent a true share of the whole catalogue. Use ALLSELECTED or expand to ALL(Sales, Sales[ProductKey]) intentionally, or switch to CALCULATE(DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[ProductKey]), REMOVEFILTERS()) if you want a genuinely context-independent denominator.

dax: Customer Penetration Rate = DIVIDE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), ALL ( Customer ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the percentage of your total customer base that actually made a purchase - essentially, what share of known customers are active buyers in the current filter context. The numerator counts distinct customers who appear in the Sales table under whatever slicers or filters are active (e.g., region, product, date), while the denominator uses ALL(Customer) to strip all filters from the Customer dimension and count every customer on record, giving a stable total regardless of context. DIVIDE handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning BLANK rather than an error when there are no customers in the table. The key watch-out is that the denominator reflects the Customer *dimension* table, not Sales, so if your Customer table contains prospects, churned accounts, or records never tied to a transaction, the denominator will be larger than "ever-purchased" customers - make sure you're intentional about whether "total customers" means all records or only those with at least one historical sale.

dax: Sales to Budget Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[BudgetAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses actual sales performance as a proportion of the budgeted sales target - a value above 1 (or 100%) means you beat budget, below 1 means you missed it. It works by summing both the SalesAmount and BudgetAmount columns within whatever filter context is active (e.g., a selected month, region, or product), then safely divides the two totals using DIVIDE, which returns BLANK rather than an error when the denominator is zero. The critical assumption here is that both actual and budget figures live in the same table and at the same grain - if budget is stored at a higher granularity (say, monthly) while sales rows are transactional, the ratio will appear correct in some visuals but silently double-count or misalign budget values when you slice at a lower level, so verify that aggregation behaviour is intentional before publishing.

dax: Return Value % = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[ReturnValue] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses returned sales as a percentage of total sales revenue - essentially a return rate by value, useful for monitoring product quality or return policy impact. It works by summing all values in the ReturnValue column and dividing by the sum of SalesAmount within whatever filter context is active (visual filters, slicers, row context in a matrix), with DIVIDE handling any zero-denominator scenario by returning BLANK rather than an error. One key watch-out: if your data model stores returns as negative values in ReturnValue, the ratio will be negative, which may look correct numerically but can mislead users expecting a positive percentage - confirm the sign convention in your data before publishing.

dax: Net Margin % = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[NetProfit] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses what share of revenue the business keeps as profit - for every dollar sold, how many cents flow through to net profit. It works by summing both the NetProfit and SalesAmount columns across whatever filter context is active (a product, a region, a time period, a report visual), then safely dividing the two totals, returning BLANK rather than an error when SalesAmount is zero. The key watch-out is that both columns live in the same fact table row, so the ratio is computed on aggregated totals, not averaged across individual row-level margins - this means a high-volume, low-margin product can silently dilute the blended percentage in ways that aren't obvious when slicing by category or salesperson.

dax: Upsell Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Sales[IsUpsell] = TRUE () ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure shows what percentage of total sales revenue came from upsell transactions - in other words, how much of the money on the table was driven by selling customers something beyond their original purchase. It works by using CALCULATE to filter the Sales table down to only rows flagged as upsells before summing their SalesAmount, then divides that filtered total by the unfiltered SalesAmount sum within whatever filter context already exists (date range, product, region, etc.), with DIVIDE handling any zero-denominator situations gracefully by returning BLANK rather than an error. The key watch-out is that this measure is entirely dependent on the integrity and population of the IsUpsell boolean column - if that flag is inconsistently applied at source, missing for certain transaction types, or NULL rather than FALSE for non-upsell rows, the numerator will be silently understated and the share percentage will be misleading without any visible error to alert you.

dax: Discount Rate = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[DiscountAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[ListPrice] * Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses discounts as a percentage of full-price value - essentially, 'how much of what we could have charged did we give away?' It divides the total discount dollars by the total list-price value (each line's list price times its quantity, added up), recalculating for whatever you've sliced to, with DIVIDE protecting against a divide-by-zero. Note that SUM(Sales[ListPrice] * Sales[Quantity]) does a little row-by-row multiplication before adding, which only works cleanly because both columns sit on the same Sales table. The key watch-out is exactly that assumption: if ListPrice actually lives on a separate Product table instead of on each sales row, this formula gives wrong answers, and you'd need to rewrite the bottom as SUMX(Sales, RELATED(Product[ListPrice]) * Sales[Quantity]) so it can reach across the relationship.

dax: Subcategory Revenue Concentration = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Product[Subcategory] ) ) )
explanation: This measure shows how concentrated revenue is at the subcategory level - what share of the total a given subcategory makes up. The top of the fraction is the normal, filtered sales; the bottom recalculates sales with ALL(Product[Subcategory]), which removes just the subcategory filter so the denominator represents the total across all subcategories, while DIVIDE handles a zero denominator gracefully. The result is a mix percentage. The key watch-out is that ALL only clears the subcategory filter specifically - if there's still a filter on a related field like Product[Category], that one stays put, so the denominator becomes 'total within the current category' rather than the grand total; often that's what you want, but it surprises people expecting a true company-wide base.

dax: Channel Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Sales[SalesChannel] ) ) )
explanation: This measure shows each sales channel's slice of the revenue pie - its share of the all-channel total. The top is the normal filtered sales; the bottom recalculates sales with ALL(Sales[SalesChannel]), which removes the channel filter so the denominator covers every channel, and DIVIDE handles a zero denominator. Put a channel on the rows of a visual and you'll see what percent each one contributes. The key watch-out is that ALL only clears the SalesChannel column, so any other filters - date, region, product - still bound the denominator, making the share relative to that filtered subtotal rather than the company-wide total; if you want a true global share, use ALL(Sales) or REMOVEFILTERS to clear the rest too.

dax: Return Rate by Value = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[ReturnAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the proportion of sales revenue that was returned - essentially, for every dollar sold, how many cents came back as returns. It works by summing the ReturnAmount column and dividing it by the total SalesAmount column within whatever filter context is active (time period, product, region, etc.), using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when SalesAmount is zero. The key watch-out is that this assumes both ReturnAmount and SalesAmount live in the same Sales table and that returns are recorded as positive values in ReturnAmount - if returns are instead stored as negative rows within SalesAmount itself, the denominator will be understated and the measure will either double-count or produce misleading results.

dax: Cost per Transaction = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), COUNTROWS ( Sales ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average cost incurred per individual sales transaction, giving analysts a unit-economics view of operational efficiency. It sums all cost values in the Sales table and divides by the total number of rows in that same table, with both calculations automatically respecting whatever filter context is active - slicers, visual filters, or report-page filters all apply simultaneously to both the numerator and denominator. DIVIDE is used instead of the native division operator so that a zero-denominator (no rows in context) returns BLANK rather than a division-by-zero error. The key watch-out is that COUNTROWS counts every row in the filtered Sales table, so if your data model has duplicate rows, cancelled transactions, or header/footer records that weren't filtered out during data preparation, those rows will inflate the denominator and silently understate your cost per transaction.

dax: Freight to Revenue Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[FreightCost] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses freight cost as a proportion of revenue, telling you how many cents of every sales dollar is consumed by freight. It sums both FreightCost and SalesAmount directly from the Sales table under whatever filter context is active - slicers, row context in a visual, or report-level filters all shape both sums simultaneously before the division occurs. DIVIDE is used instead of the / operator, so the measure safely returns blank rather than an error when SalesAmount sums to zero. The key watch-out is that both columns live in the same table, so any filter that removes rows (say, a product category slicer) will reduce both the numerator and denominator together - if your freight costs are stored at the order header level while sales amounts are at the line level, or vice versa, row granularity mismatches can silently distort the ratio.

dax: Tax Rate Effective = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[TaxAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the effective tax rate - what percentage of total revenue is actually being collected as tax across the current filter context (e.g., by product, region, or time period). It divides the sum of all tax amounts by the sum of all sales amounts using DIVIDE, which safely returns BLANK instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that this is a weighted-average rate driven by the sales mix, not a simple average of individual line-item tax rates - so if high-value, low-tax items dominate a particular filter context, the effective rate will appear artificially low, and comparing it across segments can be misleading if the underlying mix shifts significantly.

dax: Online Sales Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Sales[SalesChannel] = "Online" ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Sales[SalesChannel] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates what proportion of total sales revenue came through the online channel, expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.35 for 35%). The numerator filters the Sales table to only "Online" rows within whatever filter context is already active (date, region, product, etc.), while the denominator uses ALL on SalesChannel to strip out that column's filter and sum across all channels - preserving every other filter in context so the share remains meaningful within any other slicer selection. DIVIDE handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning BLANK rather than an error. The key watch-out is that ALL(Sales[SalesChannel]) only removes the channel filter; if your model has a separate Channel dimension table driving the filter rather than a column directly on Sales, the denominator won't clear that filter and the measure will incorrectly return 100% for every channel slice - you'd need ALL on the related dimension column instead.

dax: Promotion Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Sales[PromotionKey] <> 1 ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the proportion of total revenue that comes from promoted sales - in other words, what share of the business is driven by any promotion other than the default "no promotion" flag (PromotionKey = 1). The numerator uses CALCULATE to layer an additional filter on top of the existing filter context, restricting the SalesAmount sum to rows where a real promotion was applied, while the denominator runs the same SalesAmount sum unrestricted across the current context. DIVIDE handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning BLANK instead of an error when no sales exist in scope.

The key watch-out is the hardcoded assumption that PromotionKey = 1 represents "no promotion" - if your source system ever reassigns or reuses that key value, or if a new default key is introduced, the measure will silently misclassify rows without any warning, so this magic number should be validated against your Promotion dimension and ideally replaced with a filter on a descriptive column like Promotion[PromotionName] = "No Promotion".

dax: Average Order Value = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per sales order - a standard AOV metric - by dividing total sales amount by the number of unique orders in the current filter context. SUM aggregates all SalesAmount values while DISTINCTCOUNT counts distinct OrderIDs, with DIVIDE handling any zero-denominator scenario gracefully by returning BLANK instead of an error. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT counts distinct OrderIDs within whatever filter context is active, so if an order spans multiple rows (e.g., multiple line items), it is correctly counted once - but if OrderIDs are reused across different customers, dates, or regions and your model doesn't filter appropriately, you may undercount orders and artificially inflate the AOV figure.

dax: Country Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Geography[Country] ) ) )
explanation: This measure tells you what percentage of total revenue comes from whichever country (or countries) are currently selected, expressed as a decimal between 0 and 1. It works by dividing the SalesAmount sum under the current filter context by a denominator that strips all filters off the Country column using ALL, so the denominator always represents the grand total regardless of any country slicer or row context. The DIVIDE function handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning BLANK instead of an error. The key watch-out is that ALL only removes filters on Geography[Country] - if other columns on that table (e.g., Region or Continent) are also filtered, those filters remain active, which can cause the denominator to be smaller than the true grand total and make shares sum to more than 100%.

dax: City Revenue Concentration = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Geography[City] ) ) )
explanation: This measure tells you what share of total revenue comes from whichever city (or cities) are currently in filter context - essentially a city-level revenue concentration ratio expressed as a percentage of the grand total. It works by dividing the filtered city sales (plain SUM, respecting the current filter context) by a denominator that blows away any city filter using ALL, so the denominator always reflects total revenue across all cities regardless of what's selected in a slicer or visual. The one critical watch-out is that ALL(Geography[City]) only removes the city filter - it leaves every other filter (date, product, region, etc.) intact, so if your Geography table has a hierarchy where higher-level filters like Country or State are stored in separate columns, those filters will still constrain the denominator, which is usually correct but can produce unexpected results if city is also filtered indirectly through a related column or a many-to-many relationship path.

dax: Revenue per Sales Rep = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[SalesRepKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per individual sales representative - a per-head productivity metric. It sums all sales amounts in the current filter context, then divides by the count of distinct sales rep keys present in that same filtered slice of the Sales table, using DIVIDE to return blank rather than an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT counts every sales rep who appears in the Sales table for the current context, meaning reps who existed but closed zero deals in the period are silently excluded - if your headcount source lives in a separate dimension table, you'll likely need to count from there instead to avoid overstating productivity.

dax: Gross Margin per Unit = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average gross profit earned on each unit sold - in other words, how much money is left per unit after deducting the cost of goods. It works by summing all sales revenue and subtracting total cost across the current filter context (whatever slicers, rows, or columns are active), then divides that net margin by total units sold using DIVIDE, which safely returns BLANK instead of an error when quantity is zero. The key watch-out is that this is an average across the filtered set, not a per-SKU weighted margin rolled up - if your filter context mixes high-margin and low-margin products, the result will be a blended figure that can mask significant variation, and it will only be meaningful if SalesAmount, TotalCost, and Quantity all live in the same table and share a consistent grain.

dax: Refund Rate = DIVIDE ( COUNTROWS ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[ReturnQuantity] > 0 ) ), COUNTROWS ( Sales ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the proportion of sales rows that contain a refund - in business terms, what fraction of transactions had any returned quantity. It works by filtering the Sales table down to rows where ReturnQuantity is positive, counting those rows, then dividing by the total row count of Sales, both evaluated within whatever filter context is currently active (e.g., a selected date range or product category). DIVIDE is used safely here so that a zero denominator returns blank rather than an error.

The key watch-out is that this measures the row refund rate, not the quantity or value refund rate - if a single transaction can carry multiple units, a row with ReturnQuantity = 1 and one with ReturnQuantity = 1,000 are weighted equally, which can significantly overstate or understate the true business impact of returns. Make sure this row-level definition aligns with how stakeholders actually interpret "refund rate."

dax: Product Line Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Product[ProductLine] ) ) )
explanation: This measure expresses each product line's revenue as a percentage of total revenue across all product lines. It divides the sales amount filtered to the current product line (whatever is in the existing filter context) by a denominator calculated after stripping all filters from the Product Line column, giving you the grand total sales to use as the base. The DIVIDE function handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning blank rather than an error when no sales exist. The key watch-out is that ALL removes filters only on Product Line - other active filters such as date range, region, or sales channel still apply to the denominator, so the percentage reflects "share within the current slice" rather than an absolute share of all-time, company-wide revenue; this is usually the desired behavior but can surprise users who expect the denominator to always equal the unconditional grand total.

dax: Operating Expense Ratio = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[OperatingExpense] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure expresses operating expenses as a proportion of revenue, telling you how many cents of operating cost are incurred for every dollar of sales. It simply sums both columns within whatever filter context is active - slicers, row labels, or report-level filters all shape both figures simultaneously - then safely divides them, returning BLANK rather than an error when sales are zero. The key watch-out is that both columns live in the same Sales table, so any filter that reduces rows (say, a product category or date range) trims the numerator and denominator together, which is usually correct; however, if operating expenses are allocated at a different granularity than transactions (e.g., monthly lump-sum journal entries mixed with daily sales rows), the ratio can be distorted or double-counted depending on how those rows are structured.

dax: Customer Lifetime Value Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALLEXCEPT ( Customer, Customer[CustomerKey] ) ) )
explanation:
This measure expresses each customer's total sales as a share of their own cumulative lifetime sales, which only makes sense as a ratio if there is some outer filter context (such as a product category or date range) slicing the numerator while the denominator expands back to all sales for that same customer regardless of those filters. Mechanically, the numerator is a simple SUM within whatever filter context exists on the visual, while CALCULATE with ALLEXCEPT removes every filter on the Customer table except CustomerKey, effectively restoring all rows for that individual customer across all other dimensions. The result is a percentage showing how much of a given customer's lifetime revenue came from the current slice - for example, what share of Customer A's total spend was on a particular product.

The critical watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters on the Customer table; filters from other tables (like a date slicer driving through a relationship) are not removed by this pattern, so "lifetime" in the denominator is still constrained by any date or product filters applied via related tables - meaning the denominator may not truly represent all-time sales unless your model's filter flow is carefully accounted for.

dax: Avg Discount % per Order = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[DiscountAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) + SUM ( Sales[DiscountAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average discount rate as a share of the original (pre-discount) selling price - in other words, what percentage was knocked off across all orders in the current filter context. It works by summing total discount dollars and dividing by the gross revenue base (net sales plus discounts added back), giving a revenue-weighted blended discount rate rather than a simple average of per-order discount percentages. The result respects whatever slicers, filters, or row context are active, so it will naturally aggregate correctly across products, regions, or time periods without needing explicit iteration. The key watch-out is that this is a weighted average by revenue, not an arithmetic mean of individual order discount percentages - if you need an unweighted average (e.g., "what discount % does a typical order get?"), you'd need AVERAGEX over orders instead, and conflating the two can mislead stakeholders comparing high-value vs. low-value order mix.

dax: Revenue per Store = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[StoreKey] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the average revenue generated per store by dividing total sales amount by the number of distinct stores that appear in the Sales table. It evaluates entirely within the current filter context, so slicing by region, date, or product will narrow both the numerator and denominator simultaneously, giving you the average only for the visible subset of data. The DIVIDE function handles division-by-zero gracefully, returning BLANK rather than an error when no sales exist.

The key watch-out is that the denominator counts only stores that have at least one row in the Sales table under the current filter context - stores that exist in a Store dimension but have made zero sales will be silently excluded, which can inflate the per-store average and mislead capacity or performance analysis.

dax: Orders per Sales Rep = DIVIDE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[SalesRepKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average number of unique orders handled per unique sales rep within whatever filter context is active - a slice, a page filter, or the full dataset. It works by independently counting distinct order IDs and distinct sales rep keys from the Sales table, then safely dividing the first by the second using DIVIDE, which returns blank rather than an error if the denominator is zero. Both DISTINCTCOUNT calls operate on the same table, so the rep count reflects only reps who actually appear in the filtered Sales rows - reps with no orders in scope are silently excluded. The key watch-out is that if a single order spans multiple sales reps (a many-to-many or split-credit scenario), those orders get counted once in the numerator but inflate the denominator, understating the true per-rep workload; always confirm that SalesRepKey in the Sales table represents a clean one-rep-per-order grain before trusting this number.

dax: Aged Inventory Ratio = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ), Sales[DaysToShip] > 30 ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the proportion of total sales quantity that took more than 30 days to ship, expressed as a decimal ratio - essentially a "slow-shipment" rate for whatever slice of the business is currently in view. It works by using CALCULATE to layer an additional row-level filter on top of the existing filter context, summing only those Sales rows where DaysToShip exceeds 30, then dividing that restricted total by the unrestricted total quantity using DIVIDE (which safely returns BLANK instead of an error on a zero denominator). The key watch-out is that DaysToShip must be a column on the Sales table itself - if it is computed via a relationship to a shipment table, the row filter Sales[DaysToShip] > 30 won't behave as expected and you'll need to replace it with a FILTER iterator over the related table instead.

dax: Weekend Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), 'Date'[IsWeekend] = TRUE () ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( 'Date'[IsWeekend] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the proportion of total revenue that comes from weekend days - essentially answering "what percentage of sales happen on weekends?" The numerator filters the Date table to weekend rows only and sums SalesAmount within that context, while the denominator uses ALL to strip the IsWeekend filter entirely, giving unconditional total revenue regardless of any weekend/weekday slice already applied by a report filter or slicer. The result is a simple ratio expressed as a decimal (multiply by 100 or format as percentage in the visual). The key watch-out is that ALL only removes filters on the IsWeekend column specifically - any other active filters on the Date table (such as a year or month slicer) will still apply to both the numerator and denominator, which is usually the desired behaviour but can surprise you if you expect a truly grand-total denominator; if that's the intent, ALL('Date') would be the safer choice.

dax: Q4 Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), 'Date'[QuarterNumber] = 4 ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( 'Date'[QuarterNumber] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates each Q4's revenue as a percentage of total annual revenue, telling you what share of the year's sales fell in the fourth quarter. The numerator hard-filters the Date table to QuarterNumber = 4 using CALCULATE, while the denominator uses ALL to strip any existing quarter filter and sum across all quarters - both expressions still respect whatever other filters are in the report context (year, region, product, etc.). The key watch-out is that if your report already has a slicer or filter that excludes Q4 rows entirely, the numerator returns blank and the result is zero or blank rather than a meaningful share, so always verify the visual's filter context isn't inadvertently hiding Q4 data before trusting the output.

dax: Loyalty Customer Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Customer[LoyaltyTier] = "Gold" ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates what percentage of total revenue comes from Gold-tier loyalty customers - in other words, Gold customers' share of the sales pie within whatever filters are currently active (date range, region, product, etc.). It works by using CALCULATE to inject an additional filter restricting the numerator to rows where the customer's LoyaltyTier equals "Gold," then divides that filtered sum by the unfiltered total revenue in the same filter context; DIVIDE handles any divide-by-zero gracefully by returning BLANK by default. The key watch-out is that the denominator is not protected from the surrounding filter context, so if a report filter already isolates only Gold customers, both numerator and denominator will reflect Gold revenue and the measure will return 100% - use CALCULATE with ALL(Customer[LoyaltyTier]) on the denominator if you always want the true all-customer base as the denominator regardless of slicer selections.

dax: Revenue Contribution vs Parent Category = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALLEXCEPT ( Product, Product[Category] ) ) )
explanation: This measure shows what share of total category revenue each sub-category (or individual product) contributes - for example, how much "Mountain Bikes" makes up of the broader "Bikes" category. It works by dividing the SalesAmount in the current filter context against a recalculated total where all Product table filters are cleared *except* the Category column, meaning the denominator always rolls up to the full category regardless of which sub-category or product is selected. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT operates on the Product table only, so if the visual has slicers or filters coming from *other* tables (like a Date or Geography table), those filters still flow through and affect both numerator and denominator equally - which is usually fine, but the denominator is *not* a true grand total across all categories, so placing this measure in a matrix that shows a Category-level row will return 100% for that row, which can mislead users into thinking the rollup is meaningful when it's actually trivially self-referential.

dax: Net Revenue Margin = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[ReturnAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[DiscountAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates net revenue margin as the percentage of gross sales that remains after stripping out returns and discounts - essentially how much of every revenue dollar the business actually keeps. It sums three columns directly from the Sales table within whatever filter context is active (product, region, period, etc.), subtracts returns and discounts from gross sales to get net revenue, then divides that by gross sales using DIVIDE to handle a zero denominator gracefully. The key watch-out is that all three columns must live in the same Sales table and represent the same grain; if returns or discounts are stored in separate tables without a reliable relationship, the SUMs will either misfire or require explicit CALCULATE wrappers to respect the correct filter context - and the result will silently look plausible while being wrong.

dax: Shipping Cost per Unit = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[FreightCost] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average freight cost incurred for every unit sold, giving a sense of shipping efficiency across whatever slice of data is in context - by product, region, date, etc. It works by summing all freight costs and all quantities within the current filter context, then safely dividing the two totals using DIVIDE, which returns blank rather than an error if the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that this is a ratio of sums, not an average of row-level ratios - it computes one blended rate across all rows in context, so if you have wildly different freight profiles mixed together (say, air freight and ground shipping in the same visual), the result can be a misleading average that obscures those differences; also confirm that FreightCost is stored at the order-line level and not duplicated at the header level, or the numerator will be overstated.

dax: High Value Order Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ), Sales[SalesAmount] > 1000 ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderID] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the proportion of distinct orders where at least one line exceeds $1,000 in sales amount, expressed as a share of all distinct orders in the current filter context. It works by using CALCULATE to layer an additional row-level filter (SalesAmount > 1000) on top of whatever filter context already exists, counting the qualifying distinct OrderIDs, then dividing by the unfiltered distinct OrderID count using DIVIDE to handle zero-denominator cases gracefully. The critical watch-out is that the filter on SalesAmount > 1000 operates at the row level, so a multi-line order where only one line exceeds $1,000 will be counted as a "high value" order even if its total order value is below $1,000 - if you intend to filter on total order value, you need a measure-based approach using SUMX or a related table rather than a simple column filter.

dax: Male Customer Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Customer[Gender] = "M" ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), ALL ( Customer[Gender] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates what percentage of total revenue comes from male customers - for example, if men drive $6M of a $10M total, it returns 60%. The numerator uses CALCULATE to filter SalesAmount to only rows where Gender equals "M" within whatever filter context already exists (date range, product category, region, etc.), while the denominator uses ALL on the Gender column to strip out any gender filter and recover the full revenue across all genders, preserving all other active filters. DIVIDE handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning blank rather than an error. The key watch-out is that ALL(Customer[Gender]) only removes the gender filter - it does not remove other filters on the Customer table, so if your report has a slicer on, say, Customer[Country], both numerator and denominator are already constrained to that country, which is usually correct but can surprise you if you expected a truly global denominator.

dax: Repeat Purchase Rate = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ), FILTER ( Customer, CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ) ) > 1 ) ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the proportion of customers who have made more than one purchase - in other words, the repeat customer rate as a decimal between 0 and 1. The numerator uses FILTER to iterate over the Customer table, and for each customer checks whether their row count in Sales exceeds one within the current filter context; CALCULATE then performs a DISTINCTCOUNT of CustomerKeys passing that test, which is divided by the total distinct customers in the denominator. The critical watch-out is that the FILTER iterates the entire Customer table but evaluates COUNTROWS(Sales) inside a row-by-row context transition, so any slicer or report filter on Sales columns (like date or product) will correctly reduce the sales rows counted per customer - but if your Customer table contains customers with no Sales rows at all, those rows evaluate to 0 and are silently excluded from the numerator, which is the intended behaviour; just ensure your Customer table isn't bloated with inactive or future customers, or the denominator DISTINCTCOUNT (which only counts customers present in Sales) will still be correct while the filtered Customer table in the numerator could behave unexpectedly if relationships aren't properly set.

dax: Revenue per Postal Code = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Geography[PostalCode] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per unique postal code, giving analysts a sense of revenue density or geographic spread. It divides total sales amount - summed across all rows of the Sales table visible in the current filter context - by the count of distinct postal codes present in the Geography table under that same context. Because the two tables are separate, the distinct postal code count reflects whatever Geography rows survive the filter context, not necessarily only those linked to actual sales transactions. The key watch-out is that if a postal code exists in Geography but has no corresponding sales, it still gets counted in the denominator, which will deflate the metric; to count only postal codes that have made at least one sale, you'd want to drive the distinct count from the Sales table or a related bridging column instead.

dax: Clearance Sales Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Product[IsClearance] = TRUE () ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: Clearance Sales Share tells you what fraction of total revenue comes from clearance-tagged products, expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.15 = 15%).
