The numerator uses CALCULATE to layer an additional filter on top of whatever filter context already exists - store, date range, category, etc. - restricting the SUM to rows where the product is flagged as clearance, while the denominator runs the same SUM unrestricted, so both figures naturally respond to any slicers or report filters already in play. The one watch-out is that this measure assumes IsClearance lives in a Product dimension table with a proper relationship to Sales; if that flag is instead a column directly on the Sales fact table, cross-filtering behaviour is identical, but if the relationship is inactive or many-to-many without the right cross-filter direction, the CALCULATE filter on Product won't propagate correctly and the numerator will silently return the same value as the denominator.

dax: Direct Channel Margin Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), Sales[SalesChannel] = "Direct" ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates what proportion of total gross margin (sales minus cost) comes specifically from the Direct sales channel, expressed as a decimal ratio. It works by using CALCULATE to override the channel filter context and isolate Direct sales, computing that channel's margin as the numerator, then dividing by the total margin across all channels using whatever filter context is already active from the report (slicers, rows, pages, etc.). DIVIDE handles division safely, returning BLANK rather than an error when total margin is zero. The key watch-out is that if the report already has a filter on SalesChannel - for example, a slicer or page filter excluding Direct - the numerator will return BLANK or zero while the denominator reflects only the remaining channels, producing a misleading or empty result rather than the expected share figure; consider wrapping the denominator in ALL(Sales[SalesChannel]) if you always want share against the full-channel total.

dax: Category Gross Margin Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), ALL ( Product[Category] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates each product category's gross margin as a percentage of the total gross margin across all categories - essentially, "what slice of overall profit does this category own?" It computes the current context's gross margin (sales minus cost) in the numerator, then uses ALL(Product[Category]) in the denominator to strip away any category filter and produce the grand-total gross margin, with DIVIDE handling any division-by-zero gracefully. The key watch-out is that ALL(Product[Category]) only removes filters on that single column, so other active filters - such as slicers on region, date, or other product attributes - still apply to the denominator; if you want a true share against a completely unfiltered gross margin, you'd need ALL(Product) or ALL(Sales) depending on your model structure.

dax: Revenue per Active Customer = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), CALCULATE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ), FILTER ( Customer, CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ) ) > 0 ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes the average revenue generated per customer who has actually made at least one purchase - a cleaner productivity metric than dividing by your total customer base, which would dilute the figure with inactive accounts. The numerator simply sums all sales amounts in the current filter context. The denominator rebuilds the filter context using CALCULATE inside FILTER to count, for each row in the Customer table, whether that customer has any rows in Sales, then DISTINCTCOUNT tallies only those customers who pass that threshold. The key watch-out is that the FILTER iterates the entire Customer table on every evaluation, which can be painfully slow on large customer dimensions - and more subtly, "active" here means active within whatever the current filter context already is (e.g., a selected date range), so if Sales is already filtered to a period, a customer with purchases outside that period will correctly be excluded, but this context-sensitivity can surprise users who expect a fixed "ever-active" denominator.

dax: Late Shipment Revenue Share = DIVIDE ( CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), Sales[DaysToShip] > Sales[PromisedDeliveryDays] ), SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the proportion of total revenue attributable to orders that shipped later than their promised delivery window - essentially a "lateness rate" expressed in dollar terms rather than order counts. It works by first using CALCULATE to re-sum SalesAmount with a row-level filter that keeps only rows where DaysToShip exceeds PromisedDeliveryDays, then divides that filtered total by the unfiltered SalesAmount sum within whatever filter context exists on the report (date, region, product, etc.). DIVIDE handles the zero-denominator case gracefully, returning BLANK rather than an error when there are no sales in context. The key watch-out is that the row filter Sales[DaysToShip] > Sales[PromisedDeliveryDays] works correctly only when both columns live in the same table - if either value were sourced from a related dimension, you'd need RELATED or a calculated column to bring them together first, otherwise the comparison silently misbehaves.

dax: Avg Order Value = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes the average revenue per distinct sales order - essentially the mean basket value across all orders in the current filter context. It iterates over each unique order number, calculates the total line-item revenue for that order by summing quantity times unit price, then averages those per-order totals. The CALCULATE inside the AVERAGEX is technically redundant here since there's no explicit filter argument, but it does trigger a context transition, converting the row context from VALUES into an equivalent filter context so SUMX correctly sees only the rows belonging to each order. The key watch-out is that if an order number appears across multiple customers, dates, or other dimensions - due to non-unique order keys or a poorly designed schema - the context transition will still aggregate all matching rows together, silently inflating individual order totals and skewing the average.

dax: Min Line Total = MINX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure finds the smallest individual line-item revenue amount across all sales transactions visible in the current filter context. It works by iterating row by row over the Sales table, multiplying Quantity by UnitPrice on each row to derive the line total, then returning the minimum value from those results - importantly, this calculation happens at row level, so it correctly handles cases where neither Quantity nor UnitPrice is pre-stored as a combined column. The key watch-out is that MINX respects the current filter context, meaning slicers, report filters, or relationships can silently reduce which rows are evaluated - if a filter leaves only one row, the "minimum" is just that single line, which can mislead users into thinking they're seeing a true floor across the full dataset.

dax: Total Discount Amount = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * Sales[DiscountPct] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total monetary value of discounts given across all sales transactions - in other words, how much revenue was surrendered through discounting. It iterates row by row over the Sales table, multiplying each row's quantity by its unit price and discount percentage to get a per-row discount amount, then sums those products; because it uses SUMX rather than a pre-computed column, the calculation respects whatever filter context is active (slicers, report filters, row context in a matrix, etc.). The key watch-out is that DiscountPct must be stored as a true decimal fraction (e.g., 0.15 for 15%) - if it's stored as a whole number (15), the measure will silently return results 100× too large with no error to flag the problem.

dax: Total Cost = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total cost of goods sold by multiplying quantity by unit cost at the row level and summing the results across all rows in the Sales table. It uses SUMX to iterate row by row, which correctly handles cases where unit cost varies per line item - unlike a simple SUM of a pre-calculated column, it performs the multiplication dynamically within the current filter context, so slicing by product, region, or date will automatically restrict which rows are evaluated. The key watch-out is that this measure pulls both columns directly from the Sales table, meaning if UnitCost is not stored at the transaction level but instead lives in a related dimension (like a Products table), you'd need a RELATED() call to fetch it - otherwise the measure will return an error or incorrect results if the column reference doesn't resolve cleanly within the row context of the iteration.

dax: Max Units Per Order = MAXX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single largest order by total quantity - in other words, the highest number of units sold across all line items belonging to any one order number within the current filter context. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct order numbers visible in context, then iterating over that table with MAXX; on each row, CALCULATE re-evaluates SUM(Sales[Quantity]) with the current order number injected as a row filter, and MAXX keeps the highest result. The key watch-out is that VALUES respects the existing filter context, so if a slicer or visual filter is already limiting the data, you're getting the max order only within that filtered subset - this is usually the desired behaviour, but if the same order number spans multiple dates or segments that are being filtered independently, partial order totals could be compared against full order totals, silently understating the true order size.

dax: Avg Line Margin = AVERAGEX ( Sales, Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average per-line margin in dollars across all sales rows - in other words, the typical profit contribution per individual transaction line after subtracting unit cost from unit price. It works by iterating row by row over the Sales table (filtered by whatever filter context is active - slicers, report page filters, visual headers), computing the margin on each row, then averaging those results. Because AVERAGEX averages the per-row values rather than dividing total margin by total quantity, a single high-value line with a large margin carries the same weight as a low-value line, which may not align with a volume-weighted view of margin performance. Watch out for rows where UnitCost is NULL or blank - AVERAGEX will treat the subtraction result as blank and silently exclude those rows from both the numerator and denominator, potentially inflating the average if missing cost data is concentrated in low-margin products.

dax: Total Revenue After Discount = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * ( 1 - Sales[DiscountPct] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates net revenue by applying each row's discount percentage to its gross line value, giving you the actual realised revenue after discounts across whatever filter context is active (e.g., a selected product, region, or date range). It iterates row-by-row through the Sales table using SUMX, multiplying Quantity by Unit Price and then scaling down by the discount factor before summing - meaning discounts are applied at the transaction level, not as an aggregate. The key watch-out is that all three columns must live in the same Sales table row; if DiscountPct is resolved via a relationship from a separate table rather than stored directly in Sales, SUMX won't see it as a row-level value without an explicit RELATED call, and the calculation will silently return wrong results.

dax: Avg Quantity Per Line = AVERAGEX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average quantity ordered across individual sales line items - in other words, how many units are typically sold on a single transaction row. It iterates row by row over the Sales table using AVERAGEX, summing nothing first; it simply reads the Quantity value on each row and hands those values to an implicit AVERAGE, all scoped to whatever filter context is active (date slicer, product filter, etc.). The key watch-out is that this averages *rows*, not customers or orders - if your Sales table has multiple lines per order, you're averaging line-level granularity, which will differ meaningfully from an order-level average quantity and may not be what stakeholders expect when they ask "what's our average order size."

dax: Max Sale Per Customer = MAXX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest total revenue generated by any one customer in the current filter context - useful for identifying your top-spending customer within a given period, region, or product slice. It works by first extracting the distinct list of CustomerKeys visible in the current context, then iterating over each one, using CALCULATE to shift the row context into an equivalent filter context so that SUMX correctly aggregates only that customer's sales rows before MAXX picks the largest result. The key watch-out is that VALUES respects the existing filter context, so if your model has any ambiguous or inactive relationships between the Sales and Customer tables - or if CustomerKey contains blanks due to unmatched rows - those customers may be silently excluded or a blank row may inflate/deflate the result unexpectedly.

dax: Total Tax Amount = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * Sales[TaxRate] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total tax revenue collected across all sales transactions by computing the tax amount at the individual row level before aggregating. It iterates row by row over the Sales table using SUMX, multiplying quantity by unit price by the tax rate for each row, then sums those results - meaning the multiplication happens before aggregation, which correctly handles rows with different tax rates. This approach is safe and accurate precisely because it respects per-row variation in tax rates, unlike a simpler SUM-based formula that would require pre-calculated columns. The key watch-out is that all three columns must exist as physical columns in the Sales table; if any of them are themselves measures or come from a related table without being brought in via a relationship, SUMX won't resolve them correctly and will return blanks or errors.

dax: Min Order Total = MINX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single lowest order total across all orders in the current filter context - useful for spotting your smallest transaction by revenue. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct order numbers, then iterating over each one with MINX; inside each iteration, CALCULATE implicitly filters the Sales table to that specific order, and SUMX computes that order's revenue by multiplying quantity by unit price, with MINX ultimately returning the smallest of those per-order totals. The key watch-out is that if your model lacks a dedicated Orders table and OrderNumber is non-unique at the row level due to data quality issues or multi-currency line items, VALUES may surface fewer distinct orders than expected, quietly skewing the minimum - always validate row-level granularity in Sales before trusting this pattern.

dax: Avg Revenue Per Customer = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per individual customer - essentially, what a typical customer is worth in terms of sales within the current filter context. It iterates over each distinct CustomerKey visible in the current context using AVERAGEX, and for each customer computes their total revenue by summing quantity × unit price across all their sales rows via an inner CALCULATE and SUMX, then averages those per-customer totals. The key watch-out is that AVERAGEX averages over customers who actually appear in the VALUES result - meaning customers with zero sales in the current period are excluded from both the numerator and denominator, which will inflate the average compared to a calculation that accounts for your full customer base; if you want to include non-purchasing customers, you'd need to source the customer list from the Customer dimension table directly rather than from VALUES on Sales.

dax: Total Extended Cost = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total cost of goods sold by multiplying quantity by unit cost at the row level and summing the results across all sales transactions - essentially the extended (line-level) cost rolled up to whatever grain the visual or filter context demands. It uses SUMX to iterate row by row through the Sales table, performing the multiplication before aggregating, which ensures the math happens at the correct granularity rather than multiplying pre-aggregated totals. The result respects any active filter context, so it will correctly slice by product, date, region, or any other dimension without additional configuration. The key watch-out is that this measure assumes both Quantity and UnitCost are stored at the transaction row level in the Sales table; if UnitCost is resolved via a relationship to a separate table (e.g., a Products table), you'd need to reference that column explicitly, otherwise you risk blank or incorrect values if the column doesn't physically exist in Sales.

dax: Max Discount Amount Per Line = MAXX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * Sales[DiscountPct] )
explanation: This measure finds the single largest discount dollar amount across all individual sales line items, giving analysts a view of the biggest one-off concession granted in any given filter context. It works by iterating row-by-row over the Sales table, computing the discount amount for each line as Quantity × UnitPrice × DiscountPct, then returning only the maximum of those results - so it's a row-level calculation elevated to a scalar via MAXX. The key watch-out is that this returns the max for one line in isolation, not the total discount on an order or customer, so if DiscountPct is stored as a whole number (e.g., 20 rather than 0.20) the results will be inflated by a factor of 100 and the measure will silently produce nonsense without any error to flag it.

dax: Avg Margin Per Transaction = AVERAGEX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average gross margin dollars earned per individual sales transaction (row) in the Sales table. It does this by iterating over every row in Sales within the current filter context, computing the margin for each row as (UnitPrice minus UnitCost) times Quantity, then averaging those per-row results - not summing them first and dividing. The key watch-out is that "per transaction" here means per row in the Sales table, so if your model stores one row per order line rather than one row per order, you're actually getting average margin per line item, not per customer order - make sure your grain matches the business question being asked.

dax: Total Freight Cost = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[FreightCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total freight cost across all sales transactions by multiplying each line's quantity by its per-unit freight cost and summing the results. It uses SUMX to iterate row by row over the Sales table, performing the multiplication at the row level before aggregating - this is important because simply summing pre-existing totals wouldn't exist here; the product must be computed per row first. The result respects whatever filter context is active, so it will correctly slice by date, region, product, or any other slicer on the report. The key watch-out is that FreightCost is treated as a per-unit rate - if it's actually a flat per-order or per-shipment cost stored on every line, multiplying by Quantity will massively overstate the true freight spend, so confirm the business definition of that column before trusting the output.

dax: Avg Unit Cost = AVERAGEX ( Sales, Sales[UnitCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average unit cost across all rows in the Sales table, representing the mean cost per unit sold within whatever filter context is active (e.g., a selected product, region, or time period). It works by iterating row by row over the Sales table using AVERAGEX, summing the UnitCost values and dividing by the row count - functionally identical to a simple AVERAGE over that column in this case, but expressed as an explicit iteration. The key watch-out is that this is a row-count-weighted average, not a quantity- or revenue-weighted one, so if individual sales rows represent transactions of varying volumes, a high-volume low-cost sale carries the same weight as a single-unit high-cost sale, which can produce a misleading "average" cost from a business perspective.

dax: Max Quantity Per Line = MAXX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest quantity ordered on any individual sales line row in the current filter context - useful for spotting outlier order sizes or capacity peaks. It iterates row by row over the Sales table (as filtered by any slicers, visuals, or report filters in scope), evaluating the Quantity column on each row and returning the maximum value found. Because MAXX respects the current filter context, the result will change as users slice by product, region, date, and so on - which is usually the desired behaviour. The key watch-out is that if Quantity contains blanks or nulls, MAXX silently ignores them, so a result that looks correct may be masking data quality issues in rows where quantity was never recorded.

dax: Total Net Revenue = SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) - ( Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * Sales[DiscountPct] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates net revenue by applying each row's discount to its gross line value and summing the results across all sales - essentially Quantity × UnitPrice × (1 − DiscountPct) aggregated to whatever filter context is active (product, region, date, etc.). It iterates row-by-row over the Sales table using SUMX, which is necessary because the per-row multiplication must happen before aggregation - a plain SUM of pre-aggregated columns would give wrong results if discounts vary by line. The key watch-out is that Sales[DiscountPct] must be stored as a decimal fraction (e.g., 0.15 for 15%), not as a whole number; if the column is formatted as a percentage integer, the subtraction will wildly overstate discounts and produce negative or near-zero revenue figures.

dax: Avg Price Per Product = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Product[ProductKey] ), CALCULATE ( AVERAGEX ( Sales, Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average unit price across products, where each product gets equal weight regardless of how many sales transactions it has - effectively a simple average of per-product average prices rather than a transaction-weighted grand average. It works by first using VALUES to get a distinct list of ProductKeys, then for each product AVERAGEX iterates and the inner CALCULATE fires with that product's filter context active, computing the average UnitPrice across only that product's sales rows. The outer AVERAGEX then averages those per-product figures together. The key watch-out is that this measure deliberately de-weights high-volume products - a product with 1,000 transactions and a $10 price contributes the same $10 to the outer average as a product with a single transaction at $10, so if stakeholders expect a volume-weighted average price, this measure will silently give them the wrong answer.

dax: Min Unit Price = MINX ( Sales, Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure returns the lowest unit price found across all rows of the Sales table that are visible in the current filter context - for example, the cheapest price charged within a selected product category or time period. It uses MINX to iterate row by row over the filtered Sales table and evaluate the UnitPrice column on each row, then returns the minimum value from that iteration (though since UnitPrice is a simple column reference with no expression, this behaves identically to MIN(Sales[UnitPrice])). One key watch-out: this reflects the minimum price on actual transaction rows, not a product catalogue price - so if a product had no sales in the filtered period it is entirely excluded, and outlier rows such as zero-price adjustments or returns can silently drag the result down to an unrepresentative value.

dax: Total Gross Profit = SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure calculates total gross profit in dollars by summing the margin earned on every individual sales transaction - unit selling price minus unit cost, multiplied by quantity sold. It iterates row by row over the Sales table using SUMX, computing the per-unit margin at the row level before aggregating, which correctly handles cases where price and cost vary across transactions (unlike a column-level average approach). The result respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, report page filters, visual axes - so it will automatically scope to the selected period, product, region, or customer. The key watch-out is that this measure assumes UnitPrice, UnitCost, and Quantity all live as physical columns in the same Sales table row; if cost data is stored in a separate table and joined via a relationship, the SUMX iteration won't automatically pull related values without a RELATED() call, silently returning incorrect results.

dax: Avg Lines Per Order = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average number of line items per sales order - in other words, how many distinct product lines a typical order contains. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of unique order numbers within the current filter context, then iterating over each one with AVERAGEX; for each order number CALCULATE implicitly filters the Sales table to that single order and counts its rows, and AVERAGEX divides the sum of those row counts by the number of orders visited. The key watch-out is that VALUES is sensitive to the surrounding filter context, so if a slicer or visual filters to a single product or region, the denominator only includes orders that have at least one row visible after that filter - orders partially hidden by the filter are not counted in full, which can inflate the average compared to what you'd see with no filters applied.

dax: Max Revenue Per Product = MAXX ( VALUES ( Product[ProductKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest total revenue generated by any one product in the current filter context - useful for spotting your top-performing product at whatever slice (region, period, category) the report consumer has selected. It works by first using VALUES to build a one-column table of distinct ProductKeys visible under the current filters, then iterating over that table with MAXX; for each ProductKey, CALCULATE transitions the row context into an equivalent filter context so that SUMX correctly totals Quantity × UnitPrice for that product alone, and MAXX returns the largest of those per-product totals. The key watch-out is that VALUES respects the existing filter context, so if a slicer or visual already filters to a single product, the measure simply returns that product's revenue rather than a meaningful "maximum across products" comparison - consider whether ALLSELECTED or ALL on ProductKey better fits your intended behaviour.

dax: Total Quantity Sold = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total number of units sold across all transactions in the Sales table. It iterates row by row over the Sales table, reads the Quantity value from each row, and sums those values - though for a simple column sum like this, SUMX is functionally identical to SUM(Sales[Quantity]) with no practical difference in result. The measure respects whatever filter context is active, so it will naturally slice by date, product, region, or any other filter applied in a report visual. The watch-out is that using SUMX here adds unnecessary overhead compared to SUM for a straightforward column aggregation; reserve SUMX for when you need row-level expressions or calculated values mid-iteration, otherwise SUM is more efficient and communicates intent more clearly.

dax: Avg Unit Price Sold = AVERAGEX ( Sales, Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average unit price across all sales transactions - essentially the mean selling price per line item. It works by iterating row by row over the Sales table (respecting whatever filter context is active from slicers, visuals, or other measures), reading the UnitPrice on each row and returning their arithmetic mean. The key watch-out is that this is a simple unweighted average of prices, not a revenue-weighted average - if some transactions involve vastly different quantities, a high-volume low-price row carries exactly the same weight as a single-unit high-price row, which can produce a misleading "average price" figure; consider weighting by quantity if true average selling price is the goal.

dax: Total Order Value Incl Tax = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * ( 1 + Sales[TaxRate] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the total gross revenue including tax across all sales transactions by multiplying each line's quantity by its unit price and grossing up by its tax rate. It works by iterating row-by-row over the Sales table with SUMX, evaluating the per-row expression in the current filter context (so slicers, report filters, and relationships all apply naturally), then summing the results. The key watch-out is that tax rate is expected to live as a column on the Sales table itself - if tax rates are stored in a separate dimension and joined via a relationship, SUMX will still resolve them correctly through the row context, but if that relationship is inactive or the column is missing you'll silently get wrong totals rather than an error, since a blank TaxRate would just gross up by 1 (i.e., no tax applied).

dax: Min Margin Per Line = MINX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure finds the single worst-performing (lowest) line-item margin across all sales rows visible in the current filter context - useful for spotting the most damaging transaction in a selection of products, regions, or time periods. It iterates row by row over the Sales table, computing the gross margin contribution for each line as (UnitPrice minus UnitCost) times Quantity, then returns the minimum value found. The result fully respects any slicers or filters applied, so it will surface the worst line within whatever subset of data the user has filtered to. The key watch-out is that this can return a negative number if any line has a unit cost exceeding its unit price, which is valid but can be misread as a data quality issue - confirm whether negative-margin rows are legitimate (deliberate markdowns, returns, corrections) before acting on the result.

dax: Avg Cost Per Order = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average total cost across distinct orders, giving you a per-order cost figure rather than an average of individual line costs. It works by first isolating each unique order number with VALUES, then for each order it uses CALCULATE to sum quantity × unit cost across all rows belonging to that order, and finally AVERAGEX divides the grand total of those per-order costs by the count of orders iterated. The key watch-out is that if OrderNumber has blanks or if the same order number appears across multiple customers or dates due to a data quality issue, those will be silently grouped together as a single order, inflating or deflating the per-order cost and producing misleading averages.

dax: Max Lines Per Order = MAXX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single order that contains the most line items and returns that line count - useful for spotting unusually large or complex orders. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct order numbers, then iterating over each one with MAXX; inside each iteration, CALCULATE inherits the row context-to-filter context transition, so COUNTROWS counts only the Sales rows belonging to that specific order, and MAXX surfaces the highest of those counts. The key watch-out is that VALUES respects the current filter context, so if a slicer or report filter is active, the result reflects the maximum within that filtered subset rather than across all orders - which is usually the desired behaviour, but can surprise analysts who expect a global maximum regardless of page-level filters.

dax: Total Profit After Freight = SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] - Sales[FreightCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates net profit by subtracting both unit cost and freight charges from revenue across all sales rows. It iterates row-by-row through the Sales table using SUMX, computing (UnitPrice − UnitCost) × Quantity − FreightCost for each row and summing the results - meaning freight is deducted per transaction, not as a lump sum. The result respects whatever filter context is active (date, product, region, etc.), so it slices correctly in any visual. The key watch-out is operator precedence: freight is subtracted *after* the margin multiplication, so if a single FreightCost row represents a shipment covering multiple line items, this will over-deduct freight - the model assumes FreightCost is already allocated at the individual row level.

dax: Avg Discount Per Line = AVERAGEX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * Sales[DiscountPct] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average discount dollar amount per sales line, giving analysts a sense of how much revenue is being given away on a typical transaction row. It iterates row-by-row over the Sales table, computes the discount amount for each line (quantity × unit price × discount percentage), then returns the arithmetic mean of those line-level values within whatever filter context is active. The key watch-out is that this is a simple row count-weighted average, not a revenue-weighted one - a single unit sold at 50% off pulls the average just as hard as a 1,000-unit line at 1% off, which can be misleading when line sizes vary significantly; if revenue-weighted average discount is the real business question, the measure needs to be restructured as total discount divided by total revenue.

dax: Min Revenue Per Customer = MINX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single lowest total revenue generated by any individual customer in the current filter context - useful for identifying your least valuable customer segment or setting a revenue floor benchmark. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct CustomerKeys visible in the current context, then iterating over each one with MINX; for each row in that iteration CALCULATE shifts the filter context so that SUMX computes revenue (Quantity × UnitPrice) for that customer alone, and MINX retains only the smallest of those totals. The key watch-out is that VALUES will only surface customers who already appear in the Sales table given the current filters - customers with zero transactions are silently excluded, so the "minimum" reflects the lowest-spending *active* customer, not truly the lowest across your entire customer base.

dax: Total Revenue Per Region = SUMX ( VALUES ( Geography[Region] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes total revenue (quantity × unit price) summed across all regions, which under normal circumstances will equal a simple unfiltered revenue total - making its value primarily apparent when used as a building block or when the filter context already restricts which regions are visible. It works by first extracting the distinct list of Region values visible in the current filter context using VALUES, then iterating over each region with SUMX, and for each one using CALCULATE to inject that region as a filter before summing line-level revenue. The key watch-out is that because CALCULATE inside SUMX re-applies each region as a row filter, the result is effectively the same as a straightforward SUMX over Sales unless there is a disconnected or ambiguous relationship between Geography and Sales - if the Geography table is not properly related to Sales, CALCULATE may not filter Sales rows at all, silently returning inflated or incorrect totals.

dax: Avg Freight Per Order = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[FreightCost] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average freight cost per distinct order number, giving you a meaningful per-order freight metric rather than a simple average of individual row-level freight values. It works by first using VALUES to build a virtual table of unique order numbers, then iterating over each one with AVERAGEX and using CALCULATE to sum the freight costs for that specific order - effectively collapsing multi-line orders into a single total before averaging. The result is correctly weighted by order (not by line count), which is the right business interpretation when orders span multiple rows in the Sales table. The key watch-out is that if freight cost is recorded on every line of a multi-line order rather than just once, the SUM inside CALCULATE will aggregate all those line-level values per order - so make sure your freight data isn't double-counted at the row level, or you'll overstate every order's freight total before the average is even computed.

dax: Max Cost Per Order = MAXX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest total cost across all orders in the current filter context - useful for spotting your most expensive order at a glance. It works by first using VALUES to build a one-column table of distinct order numbers, then iterating over each one with MAXX; inside each iteration, CALCULATE implicitly filters the Sales table to that order number, and SUMX multiplies Quantity by UnitCost across every line for that order before MAXX picks the largest result. The key watch-out is that this assumes OrderNumber is a reliable, non-null unique order identifier in the Sales table - if the same logical order spans multiple fact tables or if OrderNumber contains blanks, those rows will be silently bucketed together into a distorted "blank order" that could either inflate or suppress the maximum.

dax: Total Margin After Tax = SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] * ( 1 - Sales[TaxRate] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the total after-tax gross margin across all sales transactions - essentially the profit remaining on each unit sold once both product cost and applicable tax are stripped out. It iterates row by row over the Sales table, computing the per-unit margin (price minus cost), scaling it by quantity, then applying each row's specific tax rate, before summing all results up; this row-by-row approach means mixed tax rates across transactions are handled correctly rather than applying a blended average. The measure respects whatever filter context surrounds it (date slicers, product filters, etc.), so it will naturally segment correctly in visuals. The key watch-out is that this treats TaxRate as a cost deduction on the margin rather than a liability on revenue, so if your finance team defines "after-tax margin" differently - for example, tax applied only to the price component - this formula will overstate the tax impact and understate the result.

dax: Avg Revenue Per Order = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue per distinct order - in other words, it first totals the line-item revenue for each order and then averages those order-level totals, giving you a true per-order average rather than an average of individual line amounts. It works by iterating over the unique order numbers visible in the current filter context using VALUES, and for each order, CALCULATE narrows the filter context to that single order so SUMX can sum Quantity × UnitPrice across its lines. The result is then averaged across all those per-order totals by AVERAGEX. The key watch-out is that VALUES respects the surrounding filter context, so if your report has slicers or filters that partially exclude lines within an order - for example, filtering to a single product category - the revenue for partially-visible orders will be understated, making the average misleading; if you need complete order revenue regardless of line-level filters, you'd need to handle that with ALL or a different scoping strategy.

dax: Min Cost Per Line = MINX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] )
explanation: This measure finds the single lowest line-level cost amount across all rows in the Sales table that are visible in the current filter context - effectively surfacing the cheapest individual sale by total cost rather than by unit cost alone. It works by iterating row by row through the filtered Sales table, computing Quantity × UnitCost for each row, and returning the minimum of those products. The result respects whatever slicers, visual filters, or other filter context is active, so it will shift as users drill down by product, region, date, and so on. The key watch-out is that both Quantity and UnitCost are read as physical column values from each row, meaning any row-level data quality issues - negative quantities, zero unit costs, or returns - can silently distort the minimum and make it misleading or even negative.

dax: Total Sales Qty By Product = SUMX ( VALUES ( Product[ProductKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the total sales quantity summed across each distinct product in the current filter context - in practice it produces the same numeric result as a plain SUM(Sales[Quantity]) under most circumstances. It works by iterating over the unique ProductKey values visible in the current context, and for each one uses CALCULATE to evaluate SUM(Sales[Quantity]) inside a row context converted to an equivalent filter context, then sums those per-product totals together. The key watch-out is that this pattern only diverges meaningfully from a simple SUM when there are many-to-many or ambiguous relationships involved, or when row-level security or complex filter interactions cause CALCULATE to behave differently per product iteration - in a clean star schema with a standard one-to-many relationship from Product to Sales, this added complexity buys you nothing and just introduces unnecessary overhead.

dax: Max Profit Per Customer = MAXX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest total profit earned from any one customer - useful for spotting your most valuable customer or benchmarking top-tier performance. It works by first isolating the distinct list of CustomerKeys in the current filter context, then iterating over each one: for each customer it drops into a per-customer filter context via CALCULATE and sums up (UnitPrice minus UnitCost) times Quantity across all that customer's sales rows, finally returning the maximum of those totals. The key watch-out is that the result is highly sensitive to whatever filter context is already active - slicing by region or date will correctly narrow the customer pool, but if CustomerKey has a many-to-many or disconnected relationship to the Sales table, CALCULATE may not filter Sales rows as expected, silently overstating profit for every customer and therefore inflating the maximum.

dax: Avg Tax Per Line = AVERAGEX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * Sales[TaxRate] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average tax amount per sales line, giving analysts a sense of the typical tax burden across individual transactions. It iterates row by row over the Sales table, computing the tax amount for each line (quantity × unit price × tax rate), then averages those results - all within whatever filter context is active (product, region, date, etc.). Note that this is an average of per-row tax amounts, not total tax divided by total revenue, so if your lines vary wildly in size, a handful of high-volume rows can skew the average in ways that may not reflect a "typical" transaction.

dax: Total Revenue Per Category = SUMX ( VALUES ( Product[Category] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes total revenue (quantity × unit price) summed across every product category, ultimately returning the same grand total you'd get from a straight SUMX over the Sales table - making it most useful when the outer iteration is combined with additional logic per category (like filters or rankings) rather than used in isolation. It works by first using VALUES to get the distinct list of categories in the current filter context, then iterating over that list; for each category, CALCULATE shifts the filter context so the inner SUMX only sees sales rows belonging to that category, and those subtotals are accumulated into a final sum. The CALCULATE wrapper is the critical mechanic here - without it, the inner expression would ignore the row context established by SUMX and compute the same unfiltered revenue for every category iteration. The key watch-out is that VALUES will include a blank category row if any Sales rows have no matching Product record (a referential integrity gap), silently inflating or distorting the result - enforcing the relationship or wrapping with ALLNOBLANKROW can protect against this.

dax: Min Quantity Per Order = MINX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the smallest total quantity across all individual orders in the current filter context - useful for understanding the lightest order a customer, product, or period has placed. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct order numbers, then iterating over each one with MINX; inside each iteration, CALCULATE implicitly inherits the row context transition, so SUM returns the total quantity for that single order, and MINX keeps the lowest value seen. The critical watch-out is that VALUES respects the existing filter context, so if your model has incomplete relationships or an order number appears in multiple fact rows spanning different filter combinations, the per-order sum may silently aggregate more rows than you expect - always verify that OrderNumber genuinely identifies a single transaction in your data.

dax: Avg Margin Per Line = AVERAGEX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average margin contribution per individual sales line, giving analysts a sense of the typical profit earned on a single transaction row rather than total or overall margin. It iterates row-by-row over the Sales table within the current filter context, computes the absolute margin for each line by multiplying the unit spread by quantity, then returns the arithmetic mean of those per-line values. The result is heavily influenced by how granular the Sales table is - if multiple line items are consolidated into single rows (e.g., one row per order rather than one per product), the "per line" average will be misleadingly large and won't reflect true transaction-level margins. Watch out for quantity outliers or bulk orders skewing the average significantly; in those cases a median or a ratio of total margin to line count may be a more robust metric.

dax: Total Net Revenue = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * ( 1 - Sales[DiscountPct] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total net revenue by applying each transaction's discount to its gross line value - in other words, the actual earned revenue after discounts across all sales rows in scope. It iterates row by row over the Sales table using SUMX, multiplying Quantity by Unit Price and then scaling down by the discount factor (1 - DiscountPct) before summing the results; this row-by-row approach correctly handles varying discount rates per line rather than applying an averaged discount to a pre-aggregated total. The result respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, report filters, relationship filters - so it will slice correctly by product, date, customer, and so on without any special handling. The key watch-out is that DiscountPct must be stored as a decimal proportion (e.g., 0.15 for 15%), not as a whole number percentage (e.g., 15); if the column contains whole numbers, the formula silently produces wildly negative revenue figures that can be easy to miss in aggregated visuals.

dax: Max Revenue Per Customer = MAXX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest total revenue generated by any one customer in the current filter context - useful for benchmarking or identifying your top spender. It works by first using VALUES to get the distinct list of CustomerKeys visible under the current filters, then iterating over that list with MAXX; for each customer, CALCULATE shifts the row context into an equivalent filter context so that SUMX correctly computes that customer's revenue as quantity times unit price, and MAXX retains only the largest result. The key watch-out is that VALUES will include a blank row if there are unmatched rows in the Sales table with no corresponding CustomerKey in the Customer table, which could cause the measure to return a misleadingly high or low value - ensure referential integrity is enforced in your model, or wrap the VALUES with ALLNOBLANKROW or add an explicit blank-row filter to avoid surprises.

dax: Avg Discount Per Order = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * Sales[DiscountPct] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average discount amount per order, where each order's discount is the sum of (Quantity × Unit Price × Discount Pct) across all its line items, and those per-order totals are then averaged. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct order numbers in the current filter context, then iterating over each with AVERAGEX; inside each iteration, CALCULATE shifts the row context into a filter context so that SUMX correctly aggregates only the lines belonging to that order. The key watch-out is that if an order number appears in multiple fact rows with varying discount percentages, those are correctly summed before averaging - but if your model has a separate Orders table and Sales[OrderNumber] contains duplicates due to data quality issues, VALUES will still deduplicate them, potentially masking missing or orphaned lines that could skew the average.

dax: Max Profit Per Order = MAXX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single most profitable order in the current filter context - useful for spotting your best-performing transaction at any slice (region, product, period, etc.). It works by first using VALUES to get the distinct list of order numbers visible in the current context, then iterating over that list with MAXX; for each order number, CALCULATE narrows the filter to that one order so SUMX can accumulate (UnitPrice − UnitCost) × Quantity across all its line rows, and MAXX returns the highest resulting profit figure. The key watch-out is that if OrderNumber is not a reliable unique key per order - for example, if the same order number appears across multiple customers or dates due to data quality issues - CALCULATE will pull all matching rows into each iteration, silently inflating the profit figure for those orders without any error or warning.

dax: Total Tax Collected = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * Sales[TaxRate] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total tax revenue collected across all sales transactions by deriving the tax amount per line item - something not stored as a pre-calculated column in the table. It iterates row by row through the Sales table, multiplying quantity, unit price, and tax rate together for each row, then sums those products; the result automatically respects whatever filter context is active (date, region, product, etc.). The key watch-out is that this calculation is sensitive to data granularity - if the Sales table contains partially aggregated rows rather than true transaction-level rows (e.g., daily summaries per product), the row-level multiplication will still execute correctly mathematically, but you must be certain the three fields are consistently stored at the same grain, otherwise you risk multiplying mismatched aggregates and silently overstating or understating tax.

dax: Avg Quantity Per Customer = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes the average total quantity purchased per customer - in other words, how many units a typical customer buys within whatever filter context is active (e.g., a selected time period, region, or product category). It works by first materialising the distinct list of CustomerKeys visible in the current filter context, then iterating over each one, calculating that customer's total quantity using CALCULATE (which transitions the row context into a filter context), and finally averaging those per-customer totals. The CALCULATE wrapper is doing real work here - without it, the SUM would not correctly respect each customer's row context during iteration. The key watch-out is that only customers already present in the filter context are included in the denominator; if a customer made no purchases in the selected period they won't appear in VALUES, so this is an average over *buying* customers only, not your full customer base - which can make the metric look more favourable than a true average across all customers would.

dax: Min Order Value = MINX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the lowest total order value across all orders in the current filter context - in other words, what was the smallest order, by revenue, in the selected period or segment. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct order numbers, then iterating over each one with MINX; for every order number CALCULATE implicitly filters the Sales table to that single order, and SUMX multiplies Quantity by UnitPrice across its rows to get the order total, with MINX finally returning the smallest of those totals. The key watch-out is that this pattern assumes OrderNumber is a reliable grain identifier that groups all line items belonging to a single order - if order numbers are reused across, say, different sales channels or fiscal years, CALCULATE's implicit filter will roll up lines from multiple distinct orders into one inflated total, silently understating the true minimum.

dax: Total Net Profit = SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] * ( 1 - Sales[DiscountPct] ) - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total net profit across all sales transactions by taking each line's discounted selling price, subtracting the unit cost, and multiplying by quantity sold - giving you contribution margin in absolute dollar terms. It iterates row by row over the Sales table using SUMX, evaluating the profit formula at the grain of each individual sales record before summing the results, which means it correctly handles varying discount percentages and costs at the line level rather than relying on pre-aggregated averages. The measure respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, report page filters, visual dimensions - so it will naturally slice by product, region, date, or any other attribute in your model. The key watch-out is that UnitCost must represent the cost *per unit* (not a pre-extended line cost), since the formula multiplies it by Quantity; if UnitCost is already extended (i.e., total cost for the row), you'll double-count quantity and dramatically understate profit.

dax: Avg Revenue Per Product = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Product[ProductKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue generated per distinct product - essentially, what a typical product earns in the current filter context. It iterates over each unique ProductKey visible in the current context using AVERAGEX, and for each one, CALCULATE evaluates total revenue (quantity × unit price) scoped to that individual product, then AVERAGEX divides the sum of those per-product revenues by the count of products iterated. The key watch-out is that VALUES returns only the ProductKeys present in the current filter context, so if a product exists in the Product table but has no sales in the filtered period, it won't appear in the iteration - meaning the denominator excludes zero-revenue products, which can silently inflate the average compared to a true all-products average; if you want to include products with no sales, you'd need to drive the iteration from the Product dimension table directly using ALL or a relationship-aware table expression.

dax: Max Units Per Customer = MAXX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest total unit sales recorded by any one customer in the current filter context - useful for spotting your top buyer or benchmarking volume concentration. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct CustomerKeys visible in context, then iterating over each row with MAXX; on every iteration CALCULATE shifts the filter context so SUM only aggregates that specific customer's sales, and MAXX retains the largest result across all iterations. The key watch-out is that VALUES respects the existing filter context, so if a slicer or visual already filters to a subset of customers, the measure returns the max *within that subset* - it will never surface a customer outside the current context, which can surprise users who expect an absolute all-time maximum.

dax: Total Freight Weighted By Qty = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[FreightCost] * Sales[Quantity] / CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ), ALL ( Sales ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes a quantity-weighted average freight cost, expressing each sale's freight as a share of total freight proportional to how much of the overall quantity that row represents - essentially a weighted contribution of freight across all sales rows. It iterates row by row through the Sales table using SUMX, multiplying each row's FreightCost by its Quantity, then dividing by the grand total quantity retrieved via CALCULATE/ALL, which strips all filters to return the absolute sum of Quantity across the entire Sales table. The result is mathematically equivalent to a weighted mean freight cost only if FreightCost is already a per-unit figure; if FreightCost is a line-total, you're weighting a total by a quantity share, which produces a dimensionally inconsistent number - so confirm the grain and meaning of FreightCost before using this in reporting.

dax: Orders With Discount = COUNTROWS ( FILTER ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[DiscountPct] ) ) > 0 ) )
explanation: This measure counts the number of distinct order numbers where at least one line item carries a non-zero discount. It works by first extracting the unique order numbers visible in the current filter context, then iterating over each one and using CALCULATE to narrow the Sales table to just that order's rows, summing the discount percentages across those rows - any order with a total greater than zero is kept, and COUNTROWS tallies the survivors. The key watch-out is that it sums *percentages*, not amounts, so an order with multiple lines where discounts happen to cancel out (e.g., a positive and a negative DiscountPct) could net to zero and be incorrectly excluded; you'd likely want SUMX over the actual discount amount, or use MAX/COUNTROWS of filtered rows, depending on your data model's conventions around negative discount values.

dax: Avg Unit Price By Category = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Product[Category] ), CALCULATE ( AVERAGE ( Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes the average unit price per product category, then averages those category-level averages together - giving each category equal weight regardless of how many sales transactions it contains. It iterates over the distinct categories visible in the current filter context using AVERAGEX, and for each category CALCULATE implicitly restricts the Sales table to that category before computing the average unit price, then AVERAGEX averages those results. The critical watch-out is that this is an average of averages, not a transaction-weighted mean - a category with two sales at $100 and $100 counts the same as one with ten thousand sales at $5, so the result will diverge meaningfully from a simple AVERAGE(Sales[UnitPrice]) whenever category sizes differ substantially.

dax: Total Revenue By City = SUMX ( VALUES ( Geography[City] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue (quantity × unit price) summed across every city in the current filter context, effectively producing the same grand total you'd get from a straightforward SUMX over Sales - but by explicitly iterating over each city first. It works by looping through the distinct cities returned by VALUES, and for each city CALCULATE imposes that city as an additional filter before summing the revenue rows within it. The results are then aggregated back up to a single total. The key watch-out is that this pattern only behaves differently from a simple SUMX(Sales, ...) when there are rows in Sales with no matching city in Geography - those orphaned rows would be excluded here because VALUES only surfaces cities that exist in the Geography table, meaning unmatched sales can silently disappear from your total.

dax: Max Freight Per Order = MAXX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[FreightCost] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest total freight cost across all orders in the current filter context - useful for spotting outlier shipping charges or validating freight caps. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct order numbers, then iterating over each one with MAXX; for every row in that iteration, CALCULATE implicitly filters the Sales table to that specific order and sums its freight rows, and MAXX keeps only the largest result. The key watch-out is that if a single logical order spans multiple rows in the Sales table (e.g., multiple line items each carrying a partial freight allocation), the SUM inside CALCULATE will aggregate all those rows together before the MAX comparison - so what you're really maximising is total freight per order, not freight per line item, which is the intended behaviour but can surprise analysts who expect row-level values.

dax: Avg Gross Profit Per Customer = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the average gross profit earned per individual customer - in other words, it answers "how much gross profit does a typical customer generate?" It iterates over each distinct CustomerKey visible in the current filter context, computes that customer's total gross profit by summing (Unit Price minus Unit Cost) times Quantity across all their sales rows, then averages those per-customer totals. The CALCULATE inside the AVERAGEX ensures each customer's filter is applied correctly during the row-by-row iteration.

The key watch-out is that the denominator is the count of *visible* customers - those with at least one row in the Sales table after filtering - not your full customer base; customers who made no purchases in the selected period are silently excluded, which can make average gross profit look healthier than it truly is if you're trying to account for inactive or churned customers.

dax: Total Sales Tax = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[UnitPrice] * Sales[Quantity] * Sales[TaxRate] * ( 1 - Sales[DiscountPct] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the total sales tax collected across all transactions, accounting for any discounts applied before the tax is assessed - essentially the tax on net revenue rather than gross revenue. It iterates row-by-row through the Sales table using SUMX, computing each row's net taxable amount (unit price × quantity × discount adjustment) multiplied by that row's tax rate, then summing the results; this row-level approach correctly handles varying tax rates and discount percentages per line item. The result respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, page filters, visual dimensions - so it will naturally slice by product, region, date, and so on without any extra CALCULATE needed. The key watch-out is that this assumes TaxRate is already stored as a decimal (e.g., 0.08 for 8%); if it's stored as a whole number (8), the measure will return values 100× too large, and that's an easy mistake to miss until someone compares the output to an accounting report.

dax: Min Units Per Order = MINX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the smallest total quantity across all individual orders in the current filter context - effectively answering "what is the lightest order, by units, visible right now?" It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct order numbers, then iterating over each row with MINX; for each order number, CALCULATE implicitly adds that order number as a filter context override, so SUM returns only that order's quantity, and MINX keeps track of the running minimum. The watch-out is that if an order spans multiple rows with different products or dates, those rows are correctly aggregated by the inner SUM - but if your filter context already slices by, say, product, you may be seeing the minimum units *for that product within each order*, not the true order-level minimum, which can mislead users who assume they're getting a whole-order figure.

dax: Avg Net Revenue Per Order = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * ( 1 - Sales[DiscountPct] ) ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average net revenue across distinct orders, where net revenue accounts for quantity, unit price, and any applied discount. It works by first isolating each unique order number using VALUES, then for each order iterating through its rows to sum the discounted line-level revenue via CALCULATE and SUMX, and finally averaging those per-order totals. The CALCULATE inside the iterator ensures the row context from AVERAGEX is converted into a filter context, scoping the inner SUMX correctly to only the rows belonging to each order. The key watch-out is that if the same OrderNumber appears across multiple customers or dates - meaning it isn't a truly unique transaction identifier - the inner SUMX will aggregate across all matching rows for that order number, potentially inflating individual order totals and skewing the average.

dax: Products With Sales = COUNTROWS ( FILTER ( VALUES ( Product[ProductKey] ), CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ) ) > 0 ) )
explanation:
This measure counts how many distinct products have at least one sales transaction in the current filter context - essentially a "products sold" count rather than a total product count. It works by first extracting the list of unique product keys visible under the current filter context, then iterating over each one and temporarily setting that product key as the filter to check whether any rows exist in the Sales table, keeping only those where the row count exceeds zero. The outer COUNTROWS then tallies how many products passed that test.

The key watch-out is that VALUES(Product[ProductKey]) respects the existing filter context, so if your report has no slicer or filter active, it evaluates all products in the model - but more importantly, if there are products in the Product table with no matching rows in Sales due to a broken or incomplete relationship, those will correctly return zero and be excluded, which is the desired behaviour. However, if your model has bidirectional cross-filtering enabled on the Product-Sales relationship, the Sales table may already be pre-filtered before the measure even runs, potentially causing the CALCULATE inside to behave differently than expected and undercount in certain cross-filter scenarios.

dax: Total Margin After Tax = SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] - Sales[UnitPrice] * Sales[Quantity] * Sales[TaxRate] )
explanation: This measure computes the total post-tax profit margin across all sales transactions - essentially gross margin minus the tax liability on revenue, giving a net profitability figure. It iterates row by row over the Sales table, calculating each line's gross profit (UnitPrice - UnitCost) * Quantity and then subtracting the tax amount UnitPrice * Quantity * TaxRate, accumulating the results with SUMX. The measure respects whatever filter context is active (report slicers, row/column filters), so it will correctly aggregate for any product, region, or time slice you drop it into. The key watch-out is that tax is applied only to revenue, not to cost - if your business applies tax differently (e.g., as a fixed per-unit amount or on margin rather than price), the formula will silently produce wrong numbers, so validate the tax logic against your finance team's definition before publishing.

dax: Avg Lines Per Customer = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average number of sales transaction lines per customer - essentially, how many rows in the Sales table each customer is responsible for on average. It works by iterating over each distinct CustomerKey visible in the current filter context, using CALCULATE to evaluate COUNTROWS(Sales) scoped to that individual customer, then averaging those per-customer counts across all customers in scope. The key watch-out is that VALUES(Customer[CustomerKey]) only returns customers who exist in the current filter context on the Customer table, so if a customer has no matching rows in Sales, they will still appear in the denominator - but only if they are currently filtered in; customers entirely absent from the model's relationship path or filtered out upstream will silently skew the average, potentially inflating it if you expected to include zero-transaction customers in the base.

dax: Total Discounted Line Value = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[DiscountPct] > 0 ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * ( 1 - Sales[DiscountPct] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the total after-discount revenue for only those sales lines where a discount was actually applied, excluding full-price transactions entirely. It works by first filtering the Sales table down to rows where DiscountPct is greater than zero, then iterating row by row over that filtered set to multiply Quantity by UnitPrice by the complement of the discount rate, finally summing those line-level results. The measure respects whatever filter context is active - slicers, row context in a matrix, etc. - so the FILTER operates on an already-reduced table, not the full Sales table. The key watch-out is that lines with a DiscountPct of exactly zero are excluded, so if your data uses NULL or BLANK instead of zero to represent no discount, those rows will also be dropped by the > 0 condition, potentially understating revenue if blanks and zeros carry different business meanings in your model.

dax: Avg Freight Per Line = AVERAGEX ( Sales, Sales[FreightCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average freight cost across individual sales line items, giving you a per-row mean rather than an average of aggregated totals. It iterates row by row over the Sales table as filtered by the current filter context - slicing by date, region, product, or any other active filters - and divides the sum of FreightCost values by the count of rows evaluated. Because AVERAGEX counts every row in the iteration, blank or zero FreightCost values are treated differently: blanks are excluded from the denominator while zeros are included, which can silently inflate or deflate your average depending on how missing freight data is stored in your model.

dax: Total Revenue High Margin Lines = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] > 50 ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue contributed only by sales lines where the per-unit margin exceeds $50, giving you a focused view of your highest-margin transactions rather than the full revenue picture. It works by first filtering the Sales table row-by-row to keep only rows where the unit price minus unit cost is greater than 50, then iterating over that reduced set to multiply quantity by unit price and sum the results - all of this happens within whatever filter context (date range, region, product, etc.) is already applied to the visual. Because the margin threshold is hardcoded at 50, any change to that boundary requires editing the measure directly rather than using a slicer or parameter. The key watch-out is that this measure uses gross unit margin as the filter criterion but revenue as the output, so a line can have a margin of $51 on a low-priced item and still qualify - make sure the $50 threshold is intentional and consistently understood as a dollar margin, not a percentage.

dax: Min Profit Per Customer = MINX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single worst-performing customer in the current filter context by returning the lowest total profit earned across any individual customer. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct CustomerKeys visible in the current context, then iterating over each row with MINX; for every CustomerKey CALCULATE shifts the row context into an equivalent filter context so that SUMX can sum that customer's line-level profit ((UnitPrice − UnitCost) × Quantity), and finally MINX returns the smallest of those per-customer totals. The key watch-out is that if any customer has no matching rows in the Sales table their profit evaluates to BLANK, and MINX silently ignores BLANKs - so a customer with zero transactions is excluded from the competition entirely, meaning the result reflects the worst *active* customer rather than the worst customer overall.

dax: Avg Revenue Per Geography = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Geography[CountryRegion] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue across distinct countries/regions - in other words, it answers "what is a typical country's revenue contribution?" rather than simply dividing total revenue by total rows. It works by first extracting the unique list of CountryRegion values visible in the current filter context, then for each country iterates over the Sales table to compute that country's total revenue (Quantity × UnitPrice), and finally averages those per-country totals. The key watch-out is that the result is a simple mean of country totals, so a handful of high-revenue markets will be weighted equally to tiny ones - if your geography table has countries with no sales at all but which pass the current filter, they'll contribute zeros to the average and drag it down, potentially in surprising ways depending on how your Geography and Sales tables are related.

dax: Total Cost High Volume Lines = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] > 10 ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total cost-quantity multiplied by unit cost-but only for individual sales line items where the quantity sold exceeds 10 units, effectively focusing the cost roll-up on high-volume transactions. It works by first using FILTER to scan the Sales table row by row and retain only rows where Quantity is greater than 10, then SUMX iterates over that filtered subset to multiply and accumulate the cost for each qualifying row-all within whatever external filter context exists (slicers, report filters, etc.). One key watch-out: FILTER here operates as a row-by-row iterator over the full Sales table before any aggregation, so on large tables this can be slow; additionally, the threshold of 10 is hardcoded, meaning if the business definition of "high volume" changes, the measure must be manually updated rather than driven by a parameter or reference table.

dax: Max Lines Per Customer = MAXX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the highest number of sales line items that any single customer has, giving you the peak transaction volume for your busiest customer. It iterates over each distinct CustomerKey using MAXX, and for each one CALCULATE re-evaluates COUNTROWS(Sales) inside that customer's filter context, then MAXX returns the largest count found across all customers. The implicit CALCULATE inside the iteration means any existing external filter context (e.g., a slicer on region or date) is respected, so the result reflects the busiest customer *within* whatever filters are active on the report.
