If you want the measure to respect an external category slicer, you'd need to add KEEPFILTERS.

dax: Weekend Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], 'Date'[IsWeekend] = TRUE () )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue (or whatever metric [Total Sales] aggregates) restricted to only Saturday and Sunday transactions. It works by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE with a filter argument that overrides the current filter context on the Date table, keeping only rows where the IsWeekend column is TRUE - so it behaves correctly whether you're already filtered to a specific month, region, or product. The one critical watch-out is that this depends entirely on IsWeekend being a calculated column (or imported field) correctly defined on your Date table; if your Date table isn't marked as a Date Table or has gaps in date coverage, some weekend dates could be silently excluded, giving you understated results with no visible error.

dax: Weekday Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], 'Date'[IsWeekend] = FALSE () )
explanation:
This measure calculates total sales for weekdays only, excluding any transactions that fall on Saturdays or Sundays. It works by wrapping the base [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE with an explicit filter that overrides the current filter context, keeping only rows in the Date table where IsWeekend is FALSE. This means whatever slicers or report filters are already in play, they remain active - the measure simply adds the weekday restriction on top.

The key watch-out is that this depends entirely on the IsWeekend column being correctly defined and present in your Date table - if that column is missing, mislabelled, or incorrectly populated (e.g., treating a public holiday as a weekday), the measure will silently return wrong results without any error, so validating that column's logic upfront is essential.

dax: Q1 Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], 'Date'[QuarterNumber] = 1 )
explanation: This measure returns total sales for Quarter 1 only, regardless of whatever quarter filter the user has applied in the report - giving a fixed Q1 benchmark you can place next to any other period for comparison. It works by calling CALCULATE, which replaces the existing filter on the Date table's QuarterNumber column with a hard filter for value 1, then evaluates Total Sales inside that modified context. Any other active filters - such as year, region, or product - remain in place, so the result is correctly scoped to, say, Q1 of a specific year if a year slicer is active. The key watch-out is that QuarterNumber must contain the integer 1 for every date in Q1 across all years; if the column holds formatted strings like "Q1" or is derived inconsistently, the filter will silently return blank, and you'll get no warning that the measure is broken.

dax: Q4 Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], 'Date'[QuarterNumber] = 4 )
explanation:
This measure returns total sales for Q4 only, regardless of whatever quarter filter is currently active in the report. It works by using CALCULATE to override the existing filter context on the Date table, replacing any quarter filter with a hard-coded condition that restricts rows to QuarterNumber = 4. Because it completely replaces the quarter filter rather than intersecting with it, it will return the same Q4 figure in every row of a matrix or chart, even when another quarter is selected or sliced.

The key watch-out is that this measure does not respect year context automatically - if your Date table contains multiple years, it will sum Q4 across all years simultaneously unless a year filter is applied elsewhere in the report or the measure. If your use case requires year-specific Q4 figures, you need to ensure a year slicer or row context is in place, or explicitly add a year filter inside the CALCULATE.

dax: Premium Product Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Product[Tier] = "Premium" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue restricted to products classified as "Premium" in the Product table. It works by injecting a filter context override into CALCULATE, which replaces or intersects any existing filter on the Product[Tier] column with the hard-coded value "Premium" before evaluating the [Total Sales] base measure. One key watch-out is that if a report-level slicer or page filter is already filtering Product[Tier] to a different value - such as "Standard" - CALCULATE's filter will override that context, potentially surprising users who expect the slicer to interact with this measure; however, if the slicer selects "Premium" alongside other tiers, only "Premium" records will contribute, which can look correct but may mask the override behaviour.

dax: Budget Product Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Product[Tier] = "Budget" )
explanation: This measure computes total sales revenue restricted to products classified in the "Budget" tier, giving analysts a quick slice of performance for that product segment. It works by wrapping the existing [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which overrides the current filter context on the Product[Tier] column and forces it to show only rows where Tier equals "Budget" - any other tier filters already in the report context are replaced, not stacked, for that column. The key watch-out is that CALCULATE replaces existing filters on Product[Tier] rather than intersecting with them, so if a report page already has a slicer filtering to a different tier, this measure will ignore that slicer and always return Budget figures, which can mislead users who expect their tier selection to interact with it.

dax: Sales Excl Internal = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[OrderType] <> "Internal" )
explanation:
This measure calculates revenue from external-facing sales only, stripping out any transactions flagged as internal orders - useful for reporting customer-facing performance without intercompany noise. It works by injecting an additional filter into the current filter context using CALCULATE, which overwrites or intersects with any existing filters on the OrderType column to keep only rows where OrderType is not "Internal." The underlying [Total Sales] measure then evaluates against that filtered subset of the Sales table.

The key watch-out is that this filter is additive to, not a replacement of, the surrounding context - but if a report-level or slicer filter has already isolated rows to "Internal" only, CALCULATE's filter on the same column will replace that context, not intersect it, so the measure will unexpectedly return non-internal sales even when a user has filtered to Internal. Also confirm that "Internal" is the exact, case-sensitive string used in the data; a mismatch silently returns all sales rather than throwing an error.

dax: Sales Keep Current Category = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( Product[Category] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates Total Sales while preserving any existing category filter on the Product table, rather than replacing it. Normally, a CALCULATE call can override or ignore an existing filter context; KEEPFILTERS changes that behavior by intersecting the filter passed (here, the current category filter already in context) with whatever filters are already active, so only rows satisfying both constraints are included. In practice this means the measure respects the category slicer or row context a user has selected, rather than blowing it away with a new filter argument.

The key watch-out is that when no category filter exists in the current context - for example, in a grand total row or an unfiltered report page - KEEPFILTERS has no additional filter to intersect with, so the measure simply returns the same result as plain Total Sales; don't mistake that grand total behavior for a bug or assume the measure is always more restrictive than its base measure.

dax: Total Quantity All Products = CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ), ALL ( Product ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the total quantity sold across all products, regardless of whatever product filters are active in the report - making it useful as a denominator for product-level share-of-total calculations. It works by wrapping SUM(Sales[Quantity]) inside CALCULATE with ALL(Product) as a filter modifier, which strips any filters coming from the Product table before evaluating the sum, while still respecting filters on other tables such as Date or Customer. One key watch-out: ALL(Product) removes filters on the entire Product table, so if your model has related dimension tables (e.g., a separate Category or Brand table) that aren't physically part of the Product table, filters on those tables will not be cleared - meaning the "all products" total can still be sliced by those dimensions unexpectedly.

dax: Total Quantity All Customers = CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ), ALL ( Customer ) )
explanation: This measure computes the total units sold across all customers, regardless of any customer-level filters applied in the report - making it useful as a denominator for market-share or contribution-percentage calculations. It works by wrapping SUM(Sales[Quantity]) inside CALCULATE with ALL(Customer), which strips every filter on the Customer table before evaluating the sum, while leaving all other filters (date, product, region, etc.) intact. One critical watch-out: ALL(Customer) only removes filters that flow through the Customer table - if your model has a slicer or filter applied directly to a column in the Sales table (e.g., Sales[CustomerID]), that filter will not be cleared and the measure will silently return a lower-than-expected total.

dax: Subcategory Share = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales], CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Product, Product[Subcategory] ) ) )
explanation: This measure shows each cell's share of total sales within its subcategory - in other words, what fraction of subcategory revenue a particular product (or other dimension slice) represents. It works by keeping the subcategory filter intact while wiping out every other filter on the Product table via ALLEXCEPT, so the denominator always rolls up to the full subcategory total regardless of which product, colour, or other attribute is in the current filter context. DIVIDE then safely handles any zero-denominator cases. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters applied directly to the Product table - if subcategory is filtered from a related table or through a slicer on a separate dimension, that filter won't be cleared and the denominator will silently under-count, producing shares that sum to more than 100%.

dax: Region Share = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales], CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Geography, Geography[Region] ) ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates each cell's share of total sales relative to its parent region - in other words, what percentage of the region's sales a particular sub-region, city, or store contributes. It works by dividing the current context's sales by a modified total that strips away all filters on the Geography table *except* the Region column, so the denominator always represents the full region aggregate regardless of whatever lower-level geography filters are active. The result is a ratio between 0 and 1 (multiply by 100 or format as percentage as needed).

The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters originating from the Geography table itself - if Region is being filtered by a slicer or cross-filter coming from a *related* table outside Geography, that filter won't be cleared, and the denominator will be narrower than you expect, silently inflating the share percentage. Make sure all region-level filtering flows through Geography[Region] directly.

dax: Country Share = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales], CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Geography, Geography[Country] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates each row's share of total sales relative to its parent country - in other words, how much a city, region, or sub-geography contributes as a percentage of its country's total. It does this by dividing the current context's sales by a modified version of total sales where all filters on the Geography table are removed *except* the Country column, effectively rolling sales up to the country level while preserving whichever country is in context. The result is a clean within-country share that works correctly when you slice by any Geography attribute below country level. The key watch-out is that this measure assumes all sub-geography columns - city, state, region, etc. - live in a single Geography table; if any geographic attributes come from a different table or are used as a slicer outside the Geography table, ALLEXCEPT won't strip those filters and the denominator will be wrong.

dax: Sales Excl Wholesale = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Channel] <> "Wholesale" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue excluding any transactions that came through the Wholesale channel, giving you a view of retail or direct sales only. It works by taking your existing [Total Sales] measure and applying a filter override on the Sales[Channel] column - the <> condition adds (or replaces) a filter that keeps only rows where Channel is not "Wholesale," regardless of what the current filter context says about that column. The key watch-out is that CALCULATE replaces any existing filter on Sales[Channel] rather than intersecting with it, so if a report slicer or page filter is already filtering to a specific channel, this measure will override that and show all non-Wholesale channels - which may surprise users who expect slicer selections to further narrow the result.

dax: Male Customer Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[Gender] = "Male" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue attributed exclusively to male customers. It does so by taking the existing [Total Sales] measure and layering an additional filter on the Customer table that restricts the Gender column to "Male," overriding any conflicting gender filter already in the current filter context. The watch-out is that this filter applies only to the Customer table's Gender column, so if your model has any ambiguity in the relationship between the Customer table and the fact table - such as inactive relationships, many-to-many joins, or customers with null/blank Gender values - those records will be silently excluded from the result, potentially understating true sales figures.

dax: Female Customer Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[Gender] = "Female" )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue (or whatever [Total Sales] represents) generated exclusively from female customers. It works by injecting a hard filter on the Gender column of the Customer table into the existing filter context, overriding any conflicting gender filters already in place before evaluating [Total Sales]. One watch-out: if your data contains inconsistent gender values - such as "female," "F," or trailing spaces alongside "Female" - those records will be silently excluded, so a data quality check on that column is essential before trusting the result.

dax: Sales Keep Customer Filter = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( Customer[CustomerType] = "Premium" ) )
explanation: This measure calculates Total Sales restricted to Premium customers only, and is designed to work *alongside* any existing customer-type filter already in the filter context rather than replacing it. Mechanically, KEEPFILTERS adds the "CustomerType = Premium" condition as an *additional* filter instead of overwriting the current one, meaning the result is the intersection of whatever CustomerType filters are already active and the Premium condition - if both are satisfied, Premium sales show; if they conflict, the result is blank or zero. The critical watch-out is that this intersecting behavior is precisely what distinguishes it from a plain CALCULATE with a direct filter: if a report slicer already filters to, say, "Standard" customers, this measure will return blank rather than ignoring that slicer and forcing Premium - which can surprise users who expect the measure to always show Premium sales regardless of external filters.

dax: Sales Keep Date Filter = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( 'Date'[CalendarYear] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates Total Sales while preserving any existing filter on the Calendar Year column that the user has already applied in their report, rather than replacing it. Normally, CALCULATE would override the current filter context on a column when you pass a new filter argument; KEEPFILTERS changes that behavior so the two filters are intersected instead - meaning only year values that satisfy both the outer context and any argument you'd add here are kept. In this particular form, since no new year filter is actually being specified as an argument, the practical effect is that the measure behaves identically to Total Sales in most scenarios, making it most useful as a pattern foundation when you intend to add an explicit filter argument alongside KEEPFILTERS.

The key watch-out is that KEEPFILTERS can silently return blank when filters don't overlap: if your report applies "2023" as an outer filter but your KEEPFILTERS argument restricts to "2022," the intersection is empty and the measure returns nothing - no error, just blank - which can be difficult to diagnose and is easy to mistake for a data quality issue rather than a filter conflict.

dax: Senior Customer Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[AgeGroup] = "Senior" )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue (or whatever [Total Sales] represents) restricted to customers classified as "Senior" in the AgeGroup column, giving you a clean segment-level sales figure. It works by injecting a hard filter on Customer[AgeGroup] = "Senior" into the filter context before evaluating [Total Sales], overriding any conflicting AgeGroup filters already in the context while leaving all other filters - dates, regions, products - intact. The main watch-out is that this filter replaces rather than intersects any existing AgeGroup filter in the report, so if a slicer or other visual is already filtering to a different age group, this measure will silently ignore it and always return the Senior figure - which can mislead users who expect the slicer to interact with it.

dax: Sales Excluding Refunds = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[TransactionType] <> "Refund" )
explanation: This measure calculates net revenue by summing only transactions that are not tagged as refunds, effectively giving you gross sales with refund rows stripped out. It works by injecting a filter into the current filter context that restricts the Sales table to rows where TransactionType is anything other than "Refund," then evaluates your existing Total Sales measure against that narrowed dataset. The watch-out is that this filter *replaces* any existing filter on TransactionType rather than intersecting with it - so if a report slicer or another CALCULATE upstream has already filtered TransactionType to, say, "Refund" only, this measure will override that and still return non-refund sales, which can produce surprising results and make the measure behave inconsistently across different report pages or filter combinations.

dax: In Stock Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Product[InStock] = TRUE () )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue (or quantity, depending on how [Total Sales] is defined) restricted to only those products currently marked as in-stock. It works by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE with an explicit filter that overrides the existing filter context on the Product table, keeping only rows where the InStock column is TRUE. The one critical watch-out is that InStock is a snapshot attribute - it reflects the product's *current* status, not its status at the time of each historical transaction, meaning products that are now out of stock will be silently excluded from all past sales history, which can cause this figure to change over time even for closed periods.

dax: Sales North Region = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Geography[Region] = "North" )
explanation: This measure reports total sales figures scoped exclusively to the North region, giving analysts a hard-coded regional slice of revenue without needing to manually filter a report visual. It works by wrapping the existing [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which overrides or layers onto the existing filter context by injecting a fixed filter on the Region column-so regardless of what region filters are already active on the report canvas, "North" is always enforced. One key watch-out: because CALCULATE uses filter replacement rather than intersection when a conflicting explicit filter exists, if a report slicer or another CALCULATE upstream already filters Region to, say, "South," this measure's hardcoded filter will replace that conflicting filter and still return North data-which can surprise users who expect the slicer to zero it out.

dax: Sales South Region = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Geography[Region] = "South" )
explanation: This measure computes total sales figures filtered exclusively to the South region, giving analysts a hard-coded regional slice of revenue without needing a slicer or report-level filter. It works by wrapping the [Total Sales] base measure inside CALCULATE, which overrides or adds to the existing filter context by applying an explicit filter on the Region column in the Geography table - any other active filters on the model (date, product, etc.) remain in play. The key watch-out is that if the Geography table isn't properly related to the fact table, or if the Region value "South" doesn't exactly match the data (case-sensitive in some environments, or trailing spaces), the measure will silently return BLANK rather than throwing an error, making bad results easy to miss.

dax: Loyalty Member Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[LoyaltyMember] = TRUE () )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue (or whatever [Total Sales] represents) restricted to customers who are flagged as loyalty members, giving you a quick read on how much of your business comes from that segment. It works by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE, which overwrites the existing filter context on the Customer[LoyaltyMember] column and keeps only rows where that field equals TRUE before evaluating the base measure. The key watch-out is that this assumes LoyaltyMember is a Boolean (or a value that cleanly equates to TRUE()) stored in the Customer dimension - if it's actually stored as a string ("True", "Yes", "1") or an integer, the filter will silently return blank, making it look like you have zero loyalty sales when the data is simply mismatched in type.

dax: Retail Channel Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Channel] = "Retail" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue restricted to transactions that occurred through the Retail channel only. It works by injecting a hard-coded filter on the Channel column into the current filter context, overriding any existing Channel filters from slicers or report visuals before evaluating the base [Total Sales] measure. The result is the same regardless of what channel a user selects in a slicer, because CALCULATE replaces conflicting filters on that column with this explicit "Retail" condition. The key watch-out is that if "Retail" is ever renamed or recoded in the source data - say to "retail" or "Retail Channel" - the measure will silently return blank rather than throwing an error, so a data quality check or a relationship to a stable channel dimension table is strongly recommended.

dax: Active Customer Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[Status] = "Active" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue restricted to customers whose Status field is marked "Active," effectively giving you a filtered view of sales from your active customer base only. It works by injecting an additional filter into the current evaluation context using CALCULATE, overriding or intersecting with any existing filters on the Customer Status column so that only "Active" rows are visible when [Total Sales] is evaluated. The key watch-out is that if your data model has an existing filter on Customer[Status] already applied - say from a slicer - CALCULATE will replace that filter on the Status column entirely with "Active," which is the expected behavior here, but it means users cannot slice this measure by a different status value and get a meaningful result.

dax: Premium Tier Revenue = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Product[Tier] = "Premium" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue restricted to products classified as "Premium" in the product tier, giving analysts a clean view of high-end product performance without needing to filter manually in every visual. It works by wrapping the base [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which overrides the existing filter context on the Product[Tier] column and applies a hard filter for "Premium" only - any other tier filters already in context are replaced, not layered on top. The key watch-out is that CALCULATE replaces, not intersects, existing filters on the same column, so if a report page or slicer is already filtering Product[Tier] to something else (say "Standard"), this measure will still return Premium results, which can confuse users expecting the slicer to further restrict it - consider using KEEPFILTERS if you want the slicer and the measure filter to coexist.

dax: Completed Orders Count = CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ), Sales[OrderStatus] = "Completed" )
explanation: This measure counts the number of sales rows where the order status is "Completed," giving a simple volume of fulfilled orders that can be sliced by any dimension in your model. It works by injecting an additional filter into the current filter context via CALCULATE, restricting the Sales table to only "Completed" rows before COUNTROWS tallies them - any existing filters from slicers, rows, or columns remain in effect and stack with this condition. The key watch-out is that this filter on OrderStatus will override any conflicting external filter on that same column (e.g., if a slicer is set to "Pending"), since CALCULATE replaces existing filters on the filtered column rather than intersecting them - so users selecting a different status in a slicer will still see the "Completed" count, which can be confusing or misleading in a report.

dax: Sales Excluding Discounted = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Discount] = 0 )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue considering only transactions where no discount was applied, effectively isolating full-price sales performance. It works by wrapping the existing [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which adds a filter to the Sales table that keeps only rows where the Discount column equals zero - this filter merges with (and can override) any existing filter context from slicers or report visuals. The key watch-out is that this assumes discounts are stored as exact zeros for undiscounted transactions, not as blanks or nulls; if your data source populates undiscounted rows with NULL instead of 0, those rows will be silently excluded and the measure will underreport full-price sales.

dax: Electronics Category Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Product[Category] = "Electronics" )
explanation: This measure computes total revenue restricted exclusively to products in the "Electronics" category, regardless of what category filters may already exist in the report. It works by taking the existing filter context and overriding - or adding to - it with a hard-coded filter on Product[Category], then evaluating [Total Sales] within that modified context. The key watch-out is that CALCULATE replaces any existing category filter rather than intersecting with it, so if a user slices a visual by a different category, this measure will still return Electronics figures, which can be confusing and may make cross-filtering from category slicers feel broken to end users.

dax: Sales Keep Region Filter = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( Geography[Region] = "North" ) )
explanation: This measure calculates Total Sales restricted to the North region, while preserving any region filters already active in the report rather than overriding them. Mechanically, KEEPFILTERS adds the "North" condition as an intersecting filter on top of the existing filter context, meaning the result is only non-blank when the visual's own filter context is either unfiltered on Region or already includes "North" - if the context has "South" selected, the two filters produce an empty set and the measure returns blank. The key watch-out is that this behaviour is the opposite of a plain CALCULATE with a filter argument, which would silently replace the existing Region filter; swapping KEEPFILTERS for a standard filter here would make the measure always show North sales regardless of what a slicer or row context has selected, so understanding this distinction is critical before deploying it in a cross-filtered report.

dax: Wholesale Channel Orders = CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ), Sales[Channel] = "Wholesale" )
explanation: This measure counts the number of rows in the Sales table that belong to the Wholesale channel, effectively giving you a transaction or order count for that specific channel. It works by injecting a hard-coded filter on Sales[Channel] into the current filter context using CALCULATE, overriding or intersecting with any slicers or report filters already in play, then counting the resulting rows. One watch-out: if your model has a separate Channel dimension table with a relationship to Sales, filtering via Sales[Channel] directly bypasses that relationship and can produce unexpected results when cross-filters or bidirectional relationships are involved - it's generally safer to filter through the related dimension column instead.

dax: High Value Orders = CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ), Sales[OrderAmount] > 1000 )
explanation: This measure counts the number of sales rows where the order amount exceeds $1,000, giving analysts a quick read on premium transaction volume within any slice of the report. It works by injecting an additional filter - Sales[OrderAmount] > 1000 - into the current filter context using CALCULATE, so whatever slicers, rows, or columns are already active get combined with that threshold before COUNTROWS runs. The watch-out is that this filter operates on individual rows, not aggregated order totals, so if a single business order spans multiple rows in the Sales table (e.g., one row per line item), the measure counts qualifying *rows*, not qualifying *orders* - you may be overcounting or undercounting depending on your data granularity.

dax: Sales Except Country = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Geography, Geography[Country] ) )
explanation: This measure computes total sales while preserving only the Country-level filter from the Geography table, stripping away any more granular filters (such as City, State, or Region) that might be active in the report context. It does this by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE with ALLEXCEPT(Geography, Geography[Country]) as a filter modifier, which clears all filters on the Geography table except the one on the Country column. The practical effect is that no matter how deeply a visual drills into geography, the measure always aggregates sales at the full country level. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters on the columns you *don't* list - it doesn't actively apply a country filter if none exists, so if Country itself has no filter in context the measure simply returns grand-total sales, which can produce unexpectedly large numbers in visuals that aren't sliced by Country.

dax: West Region Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Geography[Region] = "West" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales restricted exclusively to the West region, regardless of any region filters already applied in the report. It works by wrapping the [Total Sales] base measure inside CALCULATE, which overwrites the existing filter context on the Geography[Region] column with a hard-coded filter for "West" - so even if a slicer or visual is filtering a different region, this measure will always return West figures. One key watch-out is that because CALCULATE replaces (not adds to) the filter on that specific column, placing this measure in a visual already filtered to, say, "East" will still show West sales, which can confuse users who expect the slicer to interact with it - if cross-filter sensitivity is desired, you'd need to add KEEPFILTERS to preserve the existing context.

dax: Clothing Category Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Product[Category] = "Clothing" )
explanation: This measure returns the total sales revenue for products in the Clothing category only, regardless of any category filters already in place on the report. It works by taking the existing filter context and overriding - or adding - a filter on the Category column so that only "Clothing" rows are visible before evaluating Total Sales. The watch-out is that this hard-coded filter *replaces* any user-selected category filter from a slicer or visual, meaning if a user filters to "Accessories," this measure will still show Clothing sales, which can be confusing or misleading in cross-filtered visuals.

dax: Sales Keep Product Filter = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( Product[Category] = "Electronics" ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales but scoped exclusively to the Electronics product category, even when the surrounding report context tries to filter on other categories. Mechanically, CALCULATE evaluates [Total Sales] after applying a Boolean filter on Product[Category]; KEEPFILTERS then intersects that new filter with any existing category filter in the visual context rather than overriding it, meaning if a slicer or row already filters to "Clothing," the result will be blank because no row can simultaneously satisfy both filters. The key watch-out is the behavioral difference from a plain CALCULATE filter: without KEEPFILTERS, the "Electronics" condition would silently overwrite any user-selected category filter, potentially misleading users who expect their slicer choice to matter - with KEEPFILTERS, Electronics acts as a hard ceiling that no external filter can expand beyond, so you'll see blanks instead of unexpected cross-category totals.

dax: Corporate Customer Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[CustomerType] = "Corporate" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue restricted to customers classified as "Corporate," giving you a clean segment-level sales figure you can drop into any report visual without manually filtering. It works by injecting a hard filter on the CustomerType column into the current filter context before evaluating Total Sales - any existing filters on the Customer table remain in effect and are intersected with this new one, not replaced. Watch out if your data model has an inactive or ambiguous relationship between the Sales table and the Customer table: CALCULATE won't automatically resolve that, and the filter will silently fail to propagate, returning the same result as unfiltered Total Sales.

dax: Sales Except Customer Segment = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Customer, Customer[Segment] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales while ignoring all filter context on the Customer table except for whatever segment filter is currently active, effectively letting you see sales aggregated across all customers within a segment regardless of any other customer-level filters (such as individual customer selections or other customer attributes). It works by wrapping Total Sales in CALCULATE with ALLEXCEPT on the Customer table, which strips every filter on that table and then immediately restores only the Segment column's filter - meaning the result will reflect the full segment's sales even when a user drills into or selects a specific customer. This is useful for segment-level benchmarks or ratios where you want to compare an individual customer's sales against their entire segment total. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters originating from the Customer table; if Segment is also filtered from a separate Segment dimension table or a slicer connected through a different path, that filter won't be affected, so your model's relationship design can silently break the intended behaviour.

dax: Refunded Orders = CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ), Sales[OrderStatus] = "Refunded" )
explanation: This measure counts the number of rows in the Sales table where the order status is "Refunded," giving you a simple tally of refunded transactions. It works by injecting an additional filter into the current filter context via CALCULATE, restricting the Sales table to only rows flagged as "Refunded" before counting them - any slicers, page filters, or row context already in play stack on top of this filter. One key watch-out: if your model stores one row per order line rather than one row per order, this count reflects refunded *line items*, not distinct orders, which can inflate the number significantly compared to what stakeholders expect when they hear "orders."

dax: Sales Keep Channel Filter = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( Sales[Channel] = "Online" ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales restricted to the Online channel, while preserving any existing filters on the Channel field that the report's filter context may already have in place. Mechanically, CALCULATE evaluates [Total Sales] after applying a filter on Sales[Channel], but the KEEPFILTERS modifier prevents that filter from overwriting the current filter context - instead it intersects with it, meaning both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously. The key watch-out is the behavioral difference from a plain CALCULATE without KEEPFILTERS: if a user slices by "In-Store" on a report visual, a standard filter argument would silently override that selection and still return Online sales, whereas KEEPFILTERS will return blank because "In-Store" and "Online" cannot coexist - which is more honest but can surprise stakeholders who expect a number.

dax: Standard Tier Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Product[Tier] = "Standard" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue restricted to only "Standard" tier products, giving you a permanent, context-independent slice of sales regardless of any tier filters already on the report. It works by wrapping the existing [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which replaces any existing filter on the Product[Tier] column with a hard filter for "Standard" - all other active filter context (dates, regions, customers, etc.) remains in effect. Because CALCULATE uses filter replacement rather than intersection on that column, if a report page or slicer has already filtered to a different tier (say "Premium"), this measure will override it and still return Standard tier sales, which is usually the intended behavior but can surprise users expecting cross-filter interaction. Make sure the "Standard" string exactly matches the casing and spacing in your data, since DAX string comparisons are case-insensitive by default but whitespace differences will silently return blank.

dax: North Region Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Geography[Region] = "North" )
explanation: This measure returns the total sales figure scoped exclusively to the North region, regardless of any region filter already applied in the report. It works by calling CALCULATE, which modifies the current filter context by replacing whatever region filters exist with a single hard-coded filter keeping only rows where Geography[Region] equals "North," then evaluates [Total Sales] within that restricted context. The watch-out is that because CALCULATE overwrites the existing region filter rather than intersecting with it, this measure will always return North sales even if a user selects a different region in a slicer - meaning it ignores end-user region selections entirely, which may be intentional or a silent bug depending on the use case.

dax: Sales All Channels = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALL ( Sales[Channel] ) )
explanation: This measure computes total sales revenue across every sales channel combined, regardless of which channel the user has selected or filtered in the report. It achieves this by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE with ALL(Sales[Channel]), which strips any filter context applied to the Channel column before evaluating the base measure - leaving all other filters (date, region, product, etc.) fully intact. This makes it ideal for computing channel-mix percentages, where you divide [Total Sales] by [Sales All Channels] to see each channel's share of the total. The key watch-out is that ALL only removes filters on Sales[Channel] specifically, so if Channel is also filtered indirectly through a related dimension table (e.g., a separate Channel dimension), those filters will not be cleared - you'd need ALL(ChannelDim) or REMOVEFILTERS targeting the right table to handle that correctly.

dax: Direct Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Channel] = "Direct" )
explanation: This measure isolates revenue (or whatever [Total Sales] represents) coming exclusively through the Direct sales channel, giving analysts a clean slice of performance without interference from other channels. It works by injecting a hard filter on the Channel column into the current filter context, so any existing slicers or report filters still apply on top of it - meaning if a user filters to a specific region, this measure respects that and returns Direct sales for that region only. The one watch-out is that this filter replaces any existing filter on Sales[Channel] rather than intersecting with it, so if another measure or role-level security has already filtered Channel to a different value, CALCULATE will override it and you'll always get Direct - which can produce surprising or misleading results in security-sensitive models.

dax: Repeat Customer Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[IsRepeat] = TRUE () )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales generated exclusively by customers who have been flagged as repeat buyers, giving you a quick read on revenue driven by returning customer relationships. It works by taking the existing [Total Sales] measure and layering on a hard filter that restricts the Customer table to rows where IsRepeat is TRUE, overriding or intersecting with any filter context already in play on that column. The key watch-out is that the result is entirely dependent on how and when IsRepeat is stamped on the Customer table - if it's a static boolean calculated at load time, it reflects repeat status as of the last refresh, meaning a customer who became a repeat buyer mid-period will have *all* their historical sales counted here, not just those made after they crossed the repeat threshold.

dax: Sales Except Product Category = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Product, Product[Category] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates total sales while ignoring all filter context on the Product table except filters on the Category column - effectively letting Category slicers and filters still constrain results while stripping away any more granular product filters (e.g., subcategory, product name, or color). Mechanically, ALLEXCEPT replaces the entire Product table filter in the current context with one that only retains the Category filter, then CALCULATE evaluates [Total Sales] under that modified context. The practical effect is that, say, on a report filtered to "Bikes > Mountain Bikes > Red," the measure returns sales for all Bikes, not just red mountain bikes.

The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only preserves filters on the specified columns of that table - it does not protect filters coming from related tables (e.g., a Date or Geography slicer), so those external filters remain in full effect; also, if Category is not the column driving your slicer hierarchy, you may inadvertently surface more data than expected when users filter at a sub-category level that lives on a different column.

dax: In Store Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Channel] = "In-Store" )
explanation: This measure isolates the portion of total sales that came through the physical in-store channel. It works by taking whatever filter context is already active - say, a specific year, region, or product - and layering on an additional hard filter that restricts the Sales table to rows where Channel equals "In-Store" before evaluating Total Sales. The mechanics are straightforward: CALCULATE replaces or adds to the existing Channel filter, so if a slicer or report filter already has Channel set, this explicit filter will override it. The key watch-out is that the string "In-Store" must match your data exactly - a trailing space, different casing, or a variant like "In Store" (no hyphen) will silently return blank rather than an error, so validate your Channel values during data prep.

dax: Q2 Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], 'Date'[QuarterNumber] = 2 )
explanation: This measure returns total sales for Q2 regardless of whatever quarter filter is currently active in the report. It works by using CALCULATE to override the filter context on the Date table, replacing any existing quarter filter with a hard-coded filter that keeps only rows where QuarterNumber equals 2, then evaluating Total Sales against that restricted context. The result is that the measure always shows Q2 figures even when a user is viewing a Q3 or Q4 page or slicer selection - which may be intentional for a fixed benchmark but can confuse users who expect the visual to respect their filter choices. The key watch-out is that this filter only overrides the QuarterNumber column; filters on other Date columns such as Year remain active, so if the model has multi-year data the measure will sum Q2 across all years unless year context is also controlled.

dax: Shipped Orders = CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ), Sales[OrderStatus] = "Shipped" )
explanation: This measure counts the number of sales rows where the order status is "Shipped," giving you a simple volume of fulfilled orders at whatever grain the visual is filtered to. It works by injecting an additional filter on the OrderStatus column into the current filter context using CALCULATE, then counting the surviving rows - any existing filters on the report (date, region, product, etc.) stack on top of that status filter. The key watch-out is that this counts rows, not distinct orders, so if your Sales table is at the order-line level rather than the order-header level, the number will reflect line items shipped, not unique orders shipped - confirm your table grain before presenting this as "number of orders."

dax: Sales Except Region = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Geography, Geography[Region] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales while keeping only the Region column from the Geography table as an active filter, effectively summarizing sales at the Region level regardless of any lower-level geographic slicers or filters (such as country, city, or postal code) that may be active in the report. It works by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE and using ALLEXCEPT to strip all filters from the Geography table except those on Geography[Region], so the result always rolls up to whatever Region value(s) are in the current context. In practice this means the measure returns the same Region-level total for every row within a region, making it useful for ratio-to-region calculations or benchmarking individual locations against their parent region. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters on the Geography table - filters from other tables (e.g., Date, Product) remain fully active - so if your model has cross-filtering relationships that push filters onto Geography from another table, those will also be cleared, potentially producing unexpected results.

dax: Food Category Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Product[Category] = "Food" )
explanation: This measure returns total revenue restricted to products in the "Food" category, giving analysts a slice of sales for that segment regardless of any category filters already on the report. It works by injecting a hard filter on Product[Category] into the current filter context before evaluating [Total Sales], overriding any existing category selection from slicers or visuals. The key watch-out is that because CALCULATE replaces conflicting filters on the same column, if a user has already filtered to a different category (e.g., "Beverages"), this measure will silently override that selection and always show Food sales - which can produce misleading results if the measure is used in a context where dynamic category filtering is expected.

dax: Sales Keep Loyalty Filter = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( Customer[LoyaltyMember] = TRUE () ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales restricted to loyalty members only, even when the surrounding report context might otherwise override or expand that filter. It works by wrapping the loyalty filter in KEEPFILTERS, which means the new filter is intersected with - rather than replacing - any existing filter on Customer[LoyaltyMember] already in the filter context, so both filters must be satisfied simultaneously. The practical watch-out is that if a slicer or other visual has already filtered Customer[LoyaltyMember] to FALSE (non-members), the intersection of that context with TRUE will return blank, which can be confusing and is easy to misread as missing data rather than an intentional filter conflict.

dax: East Region Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Geography[Region] = "East" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales figures scoped exclusively to the East region, regardless of whatever region filters may already exist in the report. It works by wrapping the [Total Sales] base measure inside CALCULATE and injecting a hard-coded filter on the Geography Region column, which overrides any existing region filter in the current filter context - so even if a slicer or visual is set to "West," this measure will always return East numbers. The key watch-out is that this filter override behavior can surprise users when the measure appears in a cross-filtered visual or matrix: since CALCULATE replaces rather than intersects the region filter, the East value will appear identically across every region row, which may look like a data error but is actually working as designed.

dax: Partner Channel Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Channel] = "Partner" )
explanation: This measure isolates revenue generated exclusively through the Partner channel, giving analysts a clean slice of total sales attributed to that route to market. It works by injecting a hard filter on the Channel column of the Sales table, overriding any conflicting filter context from slicers or visuals before evaluating the underlying Total Sales measure. The result is that regardless of what a report user selects for Channel in the UI, this measure will always and only show Partner sales. The key watch-out is that if Channel values in the data contain inconsistent casing or trailing spaces - such as "partner" or "Partner " - the string match will silently return blank, making it look like there are no Partner sales rather than flagging a data quality issue.

dax: Q3 Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], 'Date'[QuarterNumber] = 3 )
explanation: This measure returns total sales for the third calendar quarter (July-September), regardless of whatever quarter filter is currently active in the report. It works by invoking CALCULATE, which replaces the existing filter on the QuarterNumber column with a hard-coded filter for value 3, then evaluates the Total Sales measure inside that modified filter context. All other active filters - such as year, product, or region - remain in place, so the result is still scoped to whatever else the user has selected. The key watch-out is that this assumes QuarterNumber is a plain integer column (or equivalent) where 3 always maps to Q3 of the calendar year; if your date table uses fiscal quarters or a different numbering convention, Q3 here may not mean what your business stakeholders expect.

dax: Sales Except Date Year = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( 'Date', 'Date'[Year] ) )
explanation: This measure computes total sales while preserving only the Year filter from the Date table, stripping away all other filters on Date columns such as month, quarter, or day. It works by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE and using ALLEXCEPT on the Date table, which clears every filter on that table except for the Year column - meaning the result accumulates across all months, weeks, and days within whatever year is currently in context. A typical use case is showing a year-to-date-style total or a full-year denominator alongside a more granular breakdown, without explicitly writing time-intelligence functions. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters on the Date table itself; if other tables in the model also filter dates through relationships, those filters remain active and can produce unexpected results - so this measure behaves differently than a true "ignore everything except year" if your model has multiple date-related tables or role-playing dimensions.

dax: Inactive Customer Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[Status] = "Inactive" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue attributed exclusively to customers whose status is marked as "Inactive" in the Customer table. It works by taking the existing [Total Sales] measure and wrapping it in CALCULATE with an explicit filter that overrides or appends to the current filter context, restricting the Customer[Status] column to only the "Inactive" value before evaluating the sum. The key watch-out is that if your report already has a slicer or filter on Customer[Status] selecting, say, "Active," CALCULATE will replace that filter on the Status column with "Inactive" - meaning the measure will always return inactive customer sales regardless of what the user selects for Status, which can surprise end users and mislead them into thinking their slicer is working when it isn't.

dax: Sales Excl Cancelled = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[OrderStatus] <> "Cancelled" )
explanation: This measure totals sales while leaving out any orders marked 'Cancelled,' giving a cleaner picture of real, active revenue. CALCULATE takes the current filters and adds one more condition - keep every row where OrderStatus is anything other than 'Cancelled' - then works out Total Sales over what's left. It's a common, readable way to carve out unwanted statuses. The key watch-out is that this kind of condition replaces any existing filter on OrderStatus rather than adding to it, so if a user has a slicer set to, say, 'Pending,' this measure overrides it and still shows all non-cancelled sales; it also won't drop rows where OrderStatus is blank, so messy status data can sneak through.

dax: New Customer Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[CustomerSegment] = "New" )
explanation: This measure totals the sales that come from customers tagged 'New' in the CustomerSegment field. CALCULATE adds the condition 'only rows where CustomerSegment is New' to the current filters and then evaluates Total Sales over that subset. It's a quick way to isolate one customer group. The key watch-out is that this condition replaces, rather than combines with, any existing filter on CustomerSegment - so if a user has a slicer set to 'Returning,' this measure overrides it and still returns New-customer sales rather than blank, which can mislead people reading the report; wrapping the condition in KEEPFILTERS makes it respect the user's selection instead.

dax: Sales Keep Date Filter = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( 'Date'[CalendarYear] = 2023 ) )
explanation: This measure calculates Total Sales restricted to the calendar year 2023, but unlike a plain CALCULATE filter argument, it intersects that 2023 condition with whatever year filter already exists in the report context rather than replacing it. Under the hood, KEEPFILTERS wraps the Boolean filter as an additional constraint layered on top of the existing filter context, meaning both the hardcoded 2023 predicate and any slicer or visual filter on CalendarYear must be satisfied simultaneously for rows to contribute to the result. The critical watch-out is that if a user selects any year other than 2023 in a slicer, the two filters produce an empty intersection and the measure returns blank - so the measure effectively goes silent outside 2023, which can look like missing data or a broken visual if consumers don't understand that behaviour.

dax: Sales All Products = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALL ( Product ) )
explanation: This measure returns total sales revenue across every product, regardless of which products are currently selected or filtered in the report. It works by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE with ALL(Product) as a filter modifier, which strips any existing filter context on the entire Product table before evaluating the base measure - so slicers, visual filters, and cross-filters targeting Product are all ignored. A common use case is a market-share denominator, where you divide a product's sales by this all-products total. The key watch-out is that ALL(Product) only removes filters on the Product table; filters from other tables - such as Date, Geography, or Customer - remain fully active, so the measure is not a true "grand total" if those dimensions are also filtered.

dax: High Margin Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Product[GrossMarginPct] > 0.4 )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue restricted to only those products whose gross margin percentage exceeds 40%, giving you a quick view of how much revenue comes from your most profitable product lines. It works by wrapping the existing [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE with a filter argument that overrides the current filter context on the Product table, keeping only rows where GrossMarginPct is above 0.4 before evaluating the sales figure. One important watch-out: GrossMarginPct must be a stored column on the Product table, not a measure - CALCULATE's filter arguments require a boolean column expression, so if GrossMarginPct is itself a DAX measure, this filter will silently fail or error; in that case you'd need CALCULATE([Total Sales], FILTER(ALL(Product), [GrossMarginPct] > 0.4)) instead. Also note that if margin percentages fluctuate over time and aren't static on the product dimension, the filter will reflect whatever value is in that column today rather than the margin at time of sale.

dax: Sales Excl Promotions = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[IsPromotion] = FALSE () )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue generated exclusively from non-promotional transactions, giving analysts a clean view of baseline demand stripped of any discounted or campaign-driven activity. It works by injecting a filter into the current filter context via CALCULATE, restricting the Sales table to only rows where IsPromotion is FALSE before evaluating Total Sales - so any slicers, page filters, or row context already in play stack on top of this additional constraint. One key watch-out: this assumes IsPromotion is a Boolean column (or equivalent 0/1 integer) on the Sales fact table itself; if promotional flagging lives in a separate dimension table instead, the filter won't propagate correctly and you'll need a relationship-aware approach or a CROSSFILTER adjustment to get accurate results.

dax: Domestic Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Geography[Region] = "Domestic" )
explanation:
This measure calculates total sales for the domestic region only, isolating that single segment from whatever broader context the report or visual provides. It works by calling CALCULATE, which overrides the existing filter on the Geography Region column and hard-codes it to "Domestic," then evaluates the Total Sales base measure within that restricted filter context. Any other active filters on the data model - date, product, customer - remain in effect alongside this hard-coded region filter.

The key watch-out is that "Domestic" must match the Region column values exactly, including case sensitivity on some data sources and any leading/trailing whitespace introduced during data loading; a mismatch will silently return blank rather than an error, making it easy to miss if you don't validate against a known total.

dax: Sales Except Date Year = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( 'Date', 'Date'[CalendarYear] ) )
explanation: This measure returns Total Sales while stripping away every filter on the Date table *except* for the CalendarYear filter, effectively locking the result to the selected year but ignoring any finer-grained date filters (month, quarter, day, etc.). It works by wrapping CALCULATE with ALLEXCEPT, which clears all column-level filters on Date and then adds CalendarYear back as the sole remaining filter context from that table. In practice this is commonly used to produce a full-year total regardless of which months or weeks a user has sliced on, making it useful as a denominator in year-to-date share or "year total" reference calculations. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters originating from the Date table itself - filters flowing in from related tables (e.g., a slicer on a Product category or a cross-filter from another fact table) are unaffected, so the result is not a true "ignore all context except year" measure across the whole model.

dax: VIP Customer Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[LoyaltyTier] = "VIP" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue generated exclusively by customers classified in the "VIP" loyalty tier. It works by taking your existing [Total Sales] measure and wrapping it in CALCULATE, which overrides the current filter context on the Customer[LoyaltyTier] column to keep only "VIP" rows - all other active filters from slicers, rows, or columns remain in effect. The result is whatever [Total Sales] returns once that hard filter is applied, so if your report is already filtered to a region or date range, those filters still apply. The key watch-out is that this filter replaces any existing filter on Customer[LoyaltyTier] rather than intersecting with it - so if a report-level filter or slicer is set to a different tier, this measure will silently override it and still return VIP sales, which can confuse users who expect their tier selection to affect this number.

dax: Sales Excl Samples = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[OrderType] <> "Sample" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue excluding any transactions classified as sample orders, giving a clean view of genuine commercial revenue. It works by injecting an additional filter into the current filter context that keeps only rows where OrderType is not "Sample," then evaluates Total Sales against that filtered subset. One watch-out: if Total Sales itself contains CALCULATE with conflicting filters or REMOVEFILTERS on the Sales table, those inner context transitions could override or partially neutralize this exclusion filter, so always verify the measure behaves correctly when Total Sales is more complex than a simple SUM.

dax: Large Order Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Quantity] >= 50 )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue but restricted to only those sales transactions where the quantity ordered is 50 units or more, effectively giving you a "large order" revenue figure. It works by using CALCULATE to inject a row-level filter on the Quantity column, which overwrites or intersects with any existing filter context on that column before summing sales through the [Total Sales] base measure. The filter Sales[Quantity] >= 50 is a table filter applied directly to the Sales table, so it retains all other active filters from slicers, rows, or columns in your report. The key watch-out is that this filter operates on individual row values, not aggregated totals - so a customer with ten orders of 10 units each (100 total) would be excluded entirely, because no single row clears the 50-unit threshold.

dax: Sales Except Customer Region = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Customer, Customer[Region] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates total sales while preserving only the Region filter from the Customer table, stripping away every other filter that may be active on Customer (such as individual customer name, segment, or tier). It works by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE with ALLEXCEPT, which clears all column filters on the Customer table except Customer[Region], meaning the result rolls up to the region level regardless of how granularly the visual or other slicers are filtering customers. A common use case is computing a region subtotal alongside a customer-level breakdown, so each customer row shows its region's total for share-of-region calculations.

The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters on the Customer table - filters coming from other tables (dates, products, geography if stored separately) remain fully active, which is usually what you want but can surprise you if Region is actually stored in a separate dimension rather than directly on the Customer table; in that case, the region filter would not be preserved at all and the measure would behave unexpectedly.

dax: Online B2C Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Channel] = "Online", Customer[CustomerType] = "Consumer" )
explanation:
This measure isolates revenue from direct-to-consumer online transactions, giving you a clean view of the B2C digital channel without any wholesale, in-store, or business-customer noise. It works by taking whatever filter context already exists on the report - say, a selected year or region - and layering two additional hard filters on top: only rows where the Channel column equals "Online" and the CustomerType column equals "Consumer," then evaluating Total Sales within that narrowed context. Both filter arguments are applied simultaneously as an AND condition, so a sale must satisfy both criteria to be counted.

The key watch-out is that this measure assumes a direct relationship exists between the Sales table and the Customer table, or that the model's relationship chain can propagate those filters correctly - if the Customer table isn't related to Sales (or the relationship is inactive), the CustomerType filter will silently fail, causing the measure to return all online sales regardless of customer type without any error or warning.

dax: Sales With Full Price = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[DiscountPct] = 0 )
explanation: This measure calculates the total sales revenue generated from transactions where no discount was applied - essentially your full-price selling activity. It works by taking whatever filter context already exists (selected date range, product, region, etc.) and adding a hard filter on the Sales table that keeps only rows where DiscountPct equals zero before evaluating Total Sales. The mechanics are straightforward: CALCULATE modifies the filter context, the column filter overrides any existing DiscountPct filters, and then Total Sales runs against that reduced row set. The key watch-out is that this assumes a zero DiscountPct genuinely means "no discount" - if your data uses NULLs, blanks, or negative values to represent undiscounted sales, those rows will be silently excluded and the measure will undercount full-price revenue.

dax: Sales Except Product Subcategory = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Product, Product[Subcategory] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales while preserving only the Subcategory filter on the Product table, effectively showing the sales total for the entire subcategory regardless of any other product-level filters that may be active (such as individual product name, color, or size). It works by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE and using ALLEXCEPT to clear all filters on the Product table except the one on Product[Subcategory], so the result aggregates across every product within the currently selected subcategory. This makes it useful for subcategory-level share calculations or as a denominator in a "% of subcategory" metric. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters on the Product table itself - filters arriving from related tables (such as a Date or Sales Territory table) remain fully active, which is the intended behaviour but can surprise analysts who expect a broader "ignore all filters except subcategory" result.

dax: Exported Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Geography[SalesTerritory] = "Export" )
explanation: This measure isolates revenue generated specifically through the Export sales territory, giving analysts a clean slice of total sales attributable to international or export-designated channels. It works by injecting a hard-coded filter override into the current filter context - regardless of what territory filters are already active on a report, CALCULATE replaces (or adds) a filter on Geography[SalesTerritory] so only rows tagged "Export" contribute to [Total Sales]. The mechanics are straightforward single-column filter replacement with no iteration or time intelligence involved. The key watch-out is that this filter targets the Geography dimension table, so the model must have a correctly active relationship between Geography and the sales fact table - if that relationship is inactive or the "Export" label is inconsistently cased or spelled in the data, the measure will silently return zero rather than throwing an error.

dax: Sales Keep Customer Type Filter = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( Customer[CustomerType] = "Corporate" ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales restricted to Corporate customers, while preserving any existing filter on the CustomerType column that the report context has already applied. Normally CALCULATE would overwrite an existing filter on CustomerType, but KEEPFILTERS intersects the new condition with whatever filter is already active - so the result is non-blank only when the current filter context already includes "Corporate" (or has no filter on that column at all). The key watch-out is that if a slicer or visual has already filtered CustomerType to, say, "Retail," this measure will return blank rather than overriding it with "Corporate" - which is intentional behavior but can surprise users who expect it to always show Corporate sales regardless of selections.

dax: Sales Excl Draft Orders = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[OrderStatus] <> "Draft" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue excluding any orders that are still in a "Draft" status, giving analysts a view of only committed or processed order value. It works by wrapping the base [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which adds a filter to the Sales table that removes all rows where OrderStatus equals "Draft" before the measure evaluates - this filter merges with (and can override) any existing filter context from slicers or report visuals. The key watch-out is that because CALCULATE uses an explicit column filter rather than KEEPFILTERS, this filter will override any existing slicer or filter on OrderStatus, meaning if a user filters the report to show only "Draft" orders, this measure will still return non-Draft sales rather than blank or zero - which can produce confusing, misleading results on filtered report pages.

dax: Silver Tier Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Customer[LoyaltyTier] = "Silver" )
explanation: This measure computes total revenue (or whatever [Total Sales] represents) restricted exclusively to customers classified as Silver-tier loyalty members. It works by injecting a hard filter on the LoyaltyTier column that overrides any conflicting filter already in the evaluation context, so report-level slicers or page filters on that column will be replaced, not stacked, with this "Silver" condition. The one watch-out is that if a user applies a different LoyaltyTier filter on the report canvas, CALCULATE's argument will silently overwrite it - meaning the measure will always return Silver numbers regardless of what the slicer shows, which can surprise end users who expect their selection to further narrow the result.

dax: Sales All Geographies = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALL ( Geography[Country], Geography[State], Geography[City] ) )
explanation: This measure returns total sales stripped of any geographic filters, so regardless of how the report is sliced by country, state, or city, it always shows the company-wide sales figure. It works by wrapping [Total Sales] in CALCULATE with an ALL call that removes filter context from exactly three columns - Country, State, and City - while leaving every other filter (date, product, customer, etc.) intact. The key watch-out is that ALL is scoped to only those three named columns, so if your Geography table has additional columns - say, Region or Continent - filters on those columns will still apply and can produce unexpectedly lower numbers than a true "all geographies" total; to remove filters on the entire table unconditionally, you'd use ALL(Geography) instead.

dax: Clearance Product Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Product[IsClearance] = TRUE () )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue restricted to products flagged as clearance items, giving analysts a quick view of how much revenue is coming from end-of-life or discounted inventory. It works by wrapping the base [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which overrides the existing filter context on the Product table to keep only rows where IsClearance is TRUE - any other filters from slicers, rows, or columns in the report still apply on top of this. One watch-out: this assumes IsClearance is a boolean column on the Product table with clean TRUE/FALSE values - if the column stores integers (1/0) or strings ("Yes"/"No"), the filter will silently return blank rather than throwing an error, so always validate the column's data type and values before trusting the output.

dax: Sales Excl Low Value = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[OrderAmount] >= 100 )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue considering only orders with an order amount of $100 or more, effectively excluding low-value transactions from the result. It works by using CALCULATE to layer a row-level filter on the Sales table, overriding or intersecting with any existing filter context so that only rows where OrderAmount is at least 100 contribute to the [Total Sales] base measure. The filter applies as a table filter on the Sales table, meaning it interacts with - and can be overridden by - any conflicting external filter context from slicers or report visuals. The key watch-out is that this filter operates on the raw OrderAmount column value, not on any aggregated or net amount, so if your data contains line-item rows that individually fall below $100 but belong to a larger order, those lines will be silently dropped rather than evaluated at the order level.

dax: Sales Except Geography State = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Geography, Geography[State] ) )
explanation: This measure computes total sales while preserving only the State filter from the Geography table, effectively stripping away every other filter that may be active on Geography (such as City, Zip Code, or Country) but keeping whichever State or States are currently selected. Mechanically, ALLEXCEPT replaces the entire Geography filter context with a new one that retains only the Geography[State] column's active filter, then CALCULATE evaluates [Total Sales] inside that modified context. The practical effect is that slicing by City or Zip will be ignored, but slicing by State still narrows the result - useful for computing state-level subtotals regardless of lower-level geography filters. The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT only removes filters on the Geography table itself; if State is also filtered from a disconnected slicer table or through a relationship on another table, those filters will remain active and can produce unexpected results.

dax: Direct Sales Only = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Channel] = "Direct" )
explanation: This measure isolates revenue generated exclusively through the Direct sales channel, giving analysts a clean view of sales that bypassed intermediaries or partners. It works by injecting a hard-coded filter on the Channel column into the current filter context, overriding any existing Channel selections before evaluating Total Sales - so it will always show Direct regardless of what a slicer or visual filter says about Channel. The key watch-out is that this override only replaces filters on Sales[Channel] specifically; all other filters (date, region, product, etc.) remain active, which is usually what you want but can surprise you if Total Sales itself references measures that internally manipulate context in unexpected ways. Also ensure "Direct" exactly matches the case and spacing in your data - a mismatch will silently return blank rather than an error.

dax: Sales Excl Intercompany = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[OrderType] <> "Intercompany" )
explanation: This measure computes net external revenue by stripping out any intercompany transactions from the Total Sales figure, giving you only sales made to third-party customers. It works by injecting a filter into the current filter context that keeps only rows in the Sales table where OrderType is not "Intercompany," then evaluating Total Sales over that reduced dataset. The watch-out is that this filter replaces any existing filter on the OrderType column rather than intersecting with it - so if a report page or slicer has already filtered OrderType to a specific value, CALCULATE's new filter will override it, potentially returning unexpected results or surfacing order types the user thought they had excluded.

dax: Recurring Order Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[IsRecurring] = TRUE () )
explanation: This measure calculates the total sales amount attributable only to orders flagged as recurring, giving the business a view of predictable, repeat-purchase revenue. It works by injecting an additional filter into the existing filter context - keeping only rows in the Sales table where IsRecurring is TRUE - and then evaluating the Total Sales measure against that filtered set. Because it wraps an existing measure rather than summing a column directly, any filters already on the report (date slicers, region, product, etc.) stack on top of this filter as normal. The key watch-out is that this measure depends entirely on the accuracy and completeness of the IsRecurring flag; if that column is populated by upstream ETL logic with nulls or inconsistent Boolean handling, rows with a NULL value will be silently excluded, understating recurring revenue without any visible error.

dax: Sales Except Product Brand = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT ( Product, Product[Brand] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates total sales while ignoring all filter context on the Product table except for any filter on the Brand column - meaning slicers, cross-filters, or report-page filters on attributes like Product Category, Color, or SKU are stripped away, but a Brand filter is preserved and still applied.
