Under the hood, CALCULATE modifies the filter context by replacing the entire Product table filter with one that retains only the Brand column's current filter, using ALLEXCEPT as a table filter argument. The practical effect is that the measure rolls sales up to the Brand level regardless of any more granular product selections the user has made.

The key watch-out is that ALLEXCEPT operates on the entire Product table, so it also removes filters arriving from related tables via cross-filtering - for example, a slicer on a separate Date or Store table is not affected, but any filter propagated *through* Product from another table will be cleared unless Brand is the conduit. If your model has bidirectional relationships touching Product, you may get surprising results where filters you expected to survive are silently dropped.

dax: Sales Keep Subcategory Filter = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( Product[Subcategory] = "Laptops" ) )
explanation: This measure returns Total Sales restricted to the "Laptops" product subcategory, making it useful for isolating laptop revenue within any broader report context. It works by injecting a new filter on Product[Subcategory] inside CALCULATE, but unlike a plain filter argument, KEEPFILTERS intersects that condition with whatever subcategory filters already exist in the evaluation context rather than overriding them. The practical effect is that if a report visual or slicer has already filtered to, say, "Monitors," the measure returns blank instead of silently switching the context to Laptops - which is the intended safety net behaviour. The watch-out is that this intersection logic can surprise users who expect the measure to always show laptop numbers regardless of slicer selections; if the surrounding filter context excludes Laptops entirely, the result will be blank or zero, so visuals should be designed and documented with that conditional behaviour in mind.

dax: Sales Excl Test Orders = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[IsTestOrder] = FALSE () )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue excluding any transactions flagged as test orders, giving analysts a clean, production-only sales figure for reporting. It works by layering an additional filter on top of the existing filter context - CALCULATE rewrites the filter context on the Sales table so only rows where IsTestOrder is FALSE are considered before handing off to the [Total Sales] measure. One key watch-out: if IsTestOrder contains blanks rather than explicit FALSE values, those rows will also be excluded, since = FALSE() does not match BLANK - so make sure the column has no nulls, or add a separate OR condition to handle them, otherwise you'll silently undercount sales.

dax: Sales All Product Categories = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], ALL ( Product[Category] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue across all product categories, regardless of which category is currently selected or filtered in the report - essentially giving you the grand total of sales from a category perspective. It works by wrapping [Total Sales] inside CALCULATE with ALL(Product[Category]), which strips any existing filter context on the Category column before evaluating the base measure, while leaving all other filters (date, region, customer, etc.) intact. This makes it useful as a denominator in a "% of total" calculation or as a category-agnostic benchmark. The key watch-out is that ALL removes filters only on the Category column, not the entire Product table, so if your report has slicers or filters on other Product columns (like Subcategory or ProductName), those filters will still apply - which may produce unexpectedly low numbers if analysts assume this is a true unconditional grand total.

dax: High Quantity Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Quantity] > 100 )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue restricted to only those transactions where the quantity sold exceeds 100 units, effectively isolating high-volume line items from the broader sales figure. It works by wrapping the base [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which adds a row-level filter on Sales[Quantity] > 100 - this filter is applied directly to the Sales table and stacks with (or can be overridden by) any existing filter context from slicers, rows, or columns in the report. The result is the sum of sales only for rows meeting that quantity threshold, with all other report filters still in play. The key watch-out is that this filter targets individual rows in the Sales table, so if [Total Sales] is built on a different granularity or references a related table, the filter may not propagate as expected - and if your data rarely or never has quantities above 100, the measure will silently return blank rather than flagging a data or threshold issue.

dax: Sales Keep Geography Filter = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], KEEPFILTERS ( Geography[Country] = "United States" ) )
explanation: This measure returns Total Sales restricted to the United States, but unlike a plain CALCULATE filter argument, it intersects the "United States" condition with whatever Country filters already exist in the report context rather than replacing them. Mechanically, KEEPFILTERS wraps the filter argument so that CALCULATE adds it as an additional constraint on top of the existing filter context instead of overriding it. The practical result is that if a user has already filtered to, say, "Canada," the measure returns blank - because no row can simultaneously be Canada and United States - whereas without KEEPFILTERS it would silently override the slicer and always show US sales. The key watch-out is that this behaviour makes the measure highly sensitive to whatever is selected upstream; analysts who expect a guaranteed US-only figure regardless of slicer state should use a plain filter argument instead, and those who use KEEPFILTERS should document clearly that the measure can go blank when conflicting geography filters are active.

dax: Sales Excl Back Orders = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[OrderStatus] <> "Backorder" )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue excluding any transactions flagged as backordered, giving analysts a clean view of fulfilled demand. It works by wrapping the base [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which overrides the existing filter context on the OrderStatus column to keep only rows where the status is not "Backorder" - all other filters from slicers, rows, and columns remain intact. One important watch-out: this filter uses a not-equal comparison, which means any NULL or blank values in OrderStatus will also be excluded, since DAX inequality comparisons do not match blanks - you may want to explicitly verify whether unset statuses should be included or excluded in your business logic.

dax: Weighted Avg Selling Price = DIVIDE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ), SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure finds the average selling price the proper way - weighting by how many units sold at each price instead of just averaging the price tags, so big-volume sales count more. It divides total revenue by total units: the first SUMX goes row by row multiplying Quantity by UnitPrice to get revenue, the second SUMX adds up the quantities, and DIVIDE guards the divide-by-zero. Weighting like this gives you the price customers actually paid on average, not a misleading flat average. The key watch-out is how blanks behave: a row with a missing UnitPrice still adds its quantity to the bottom of the fraction but contributes nothing to the top, which quietly drags the average down - so it's better to clean or exclude price-less rows than to trust SUMX's habit of silently treating blanks as zero.

dax: Weighted Avg Unit Cost = DIVIDE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] ), SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure works out the average cost per unit sold, weighted by volume - so a product that ships in big quantities influences the average more than a rarely-sold one, which a plain average of unit costs would miss. It divides total cost by total units: the first SUMX multiplies Quantity by UnitCost row by row to get total cost, the second SUMX sums the quantities, and DIVIDE protects against dividing by zero. The weighting is what makes it reflect reality rather than a naive mean. The key watch-out is that it assumes one row per transaction line; if the Sales table holds pre-aggregated or duplicated rows instead, the quantities get double-counted and the weighting is thrown off, so it's worth confirming the grain (how detailed each row is) before trusting the result.

dax: Avg Order Revenue = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes the average revenue per order by first totalling the line-item revenue for each distinct order number, then averaging those order-level totals - giving you a true per-order average rather than an average of individual line values. It works by iterating over each unique OrderNumber in the current filter context using AVERAGEX, and for each one, CALCULATE implicitly filters the Sales table to that order so SUMX can sum Quantity × UnitPrice across all its rows. The result respects whatever slicers or report filters are active, so the average will shift correctly when you filter by region, product, or date. The key watch-out is that VALUES will include a blank member if OrderNumber contains nulls or has referential integrity gaps, which can silently inflate the denominator and deflate the average - make sure your data has no missing order numbers before trusting this metric.

dax: Avg Quantity Per Order = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average total quantity shipped per distinct sales order - in other words, how many units a typical order contains. It works by first using VALUES to build a one-column table of unique order numbers within the current filter context, then iterating over each one with AVERAGEX; for each order, CALCULATE transitions the row context into a filter context so that SUM returns only the quantity belonging to that specific order, and AVERAGEX then averages those per-order totals. The result is a true order-level average, not a simple average of row-level quantities, so it correctly handles orders that span multiple line items. The key watch-out is that VALUES will include a blank member if Sales[OrderNumber] contains nulls or has referential integrity gaps, which can silently inflate the order count and deflate the average - ensure your data has clean, non-null order numbers before trusting this figure.

dax: Avg Gross Profit Per Line = AVERAGEX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure calculates the average gross profit contributed by a single sales line item - useful for understanding deal-level profitability rather than totals. It iterates row by row over the Sales table, computes the gross profit for each row as (UnitPrice - UnitCost) × Quantity, then returns the arithmetic mean of those row-level values within whatever filter context is active (e.g., a selected product, region, or time period). The key watch-out is that this is an average of line-level profits, not total profit divided by line count - if your Sales table contains multiple rows per order (one per product line), the granularity of that table directly drives what "one line" means, so any fan-out from an incorrect relationship or a table at the wrong grain will silently distort the result.

dax: Max Order Revenue = MAXX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest revenue-generating order in the current filter context - in other words, which order produced the most revenue. It works by first using VALUES to get a distinct list of order numbers visible in the current context, then iterating over that list with MAXX; for each order number, CALCULATE implicitly filters the Sales table to that order so SUMX can multiply Quantity by UnitPrice across all line items belonging to it, and MAXX retains only the largest of those totals. The key watch-out is that this measure is sensitive to whatever filter context surrounds it - if used in a visual sliced by, say, product category, it returns the max order revenue *within that category*, not necessarily the globally highest order, which can surprise users who expect an absolute maximum across all orders.

dax: Max Gross Profit Per Order = MAXX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest gross profit earned across all individual orders in the current filter context. It works by first using VALUES to generate a one-column table of distinct order numbers, then iterating over each one with MAXX; inside each iteration, CALCULATE inherits the row context-to-filter context transition, so SUMX correctly aggregates (UnitPrice − UnitCost) × Quantity only for the rows belonging to that order, and MAXX returns the largest of those per-order totals. The key watch-out is that if an order spans multiple rows with mixed positive and negative quantities - such as returns or credit lines - those will net down within that order's SUMX, which is usually correct behaviour but can mask a high-volume order that also had significant returns, making a different order appear as the "max."

dax: Min Revenue Per Order = MINX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single lowest revenue-generating order in the current filter context - useful for spotting your weakest transaction or setting a floor benchmark. It works by first using VALUES to get a distinct list of order numbers visible in context, then iterating over each one with MINX; for each order, CALCULATE implicitly filters the Sales table to that order number, SUMX multiplies Quantity by Unit Price across all rows for that order to produce its revenue, and MINX retains only the smallest of those totals. One key watch-out: if your Sales table has multiple rows per order (e.g., one row per line item), this works correctly because CALCULATE scopes to each OrderNumber during iteration - but if OrderNumber has nulls or blanks, those rows will be grouped into a single "blank order" bucket that may silently inflate or deflate the minimum, so ensure OrderNumber is always populated.

dax: Min Gross Profit Per Customer = MINX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single lowest gross profit total across all customers in the current filter context - useful for spotting your worst-performing customer in any given slice (region, period, product, etc.). It works by iterating over each distinct CustomerKey visible in the current context, using CALCULATE to shift the row context into an equivalent filter context so that SUMX correctly aggregates only that customer's sales, then MINX returns the smallest of those per-customer totals. The key watch-out is that this measure reflects the minimum among customers *who have sales in the current context* - customers with zero transactions are simply absent from VALUES, so they're ignored rather than contributing a zero, which means the result can be misleadingly positive if you're trying to identify customers with no activity at all.

dax: Min Order Cost = MINX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single lowest total cost across all distinct orders in the current filter context - useful for spotting your cheapest order within any slice (product, region, date, etc.). It works by first extracting the unique order numbers visible in context via VALUES, then iterating over each one; for every order, CALCULATE shifts the row context into a filter context so SUMX can correctly sum the line-level quantity-times-unit-cost values for that order alone, and MINX returns the smallest of those per-order totals. The key watch-out is that if an OrderNumber spans multiple rows with different products, the inner SUMX correctly aggregates all lines - but if your model has a separate Orders table and you run this measure against Sales directly, a single OrderNumber appearing in multiple fact partitions (due to a poorly defined relationship or duplicated keys) could inflate individual order totals and make MINX return a misleadingly high "minimum."

dax: Total Revenue Loss Lines = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[UnitPrice] < Sales[UnitCost] ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total revenue generated specifically from loss-making lines - transactions where the unit price charged was below the unit cost, representing sales where the business is guaranteed to lose money on every unit sold. It works by first filtering the Sales table down to only those rows where UnitPrice is less than UnitCost, then iterating over that filtered set to multiply Quantity by UnitPrice row by row, summing the results. Note that this returns the revenue from those lines, not the loss amount itself - if your intent is to quantify the financial damage, you'd want to compute (UnitCost − UnitPrice) * Quantity instead. Also be aware that any external filter context (e.g., a slicer on date or product) will be applied before FILTER runs, so the result is correctly scoped but will silently return blank if no loss-making lines exist in the current context.

dax: Total Cost Loss Lines = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[UnitPrice] < Sales[UnitCost] ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total cost exposure - in dollar terms - for every sales line where the business is selling below cost, essentially quantifying how much revenue *should* have been collected just to break even on those loss-making transactions. It works by first filtering the Sales table down to only rows where the unit price is less than the unit cost, then iterating over that subset with SUMX to multiply quantity by unit cost on each row, summing the results. The figure represents the full cost burden of those lines, not the loss itself - to get the actual margin loss you'd subtract total revenue on those same lines. The key watch-out is that this measure runs in whatever filter context is active (e.g., a slicer on product or date), but because FILTER operates row by row on the physical Sales table, performance can degrade significantly on large datasets - and if UnitPrice or UnitCost are sourced from related dimension tables rather than stored directly on Sales, the comparison may silently return blanks or incorrect results without explicit RELATED() calls.

dax: Total Revenue Discounted Lines = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[DiscountPct] > 0 ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total revenue generated exclusively from sales lines where a discount was applied, giving you a focused view of discounted business volume rather than overall revenue. It works by first using FILTER to reduce the Sales table to only rows where DiscountPct is greater than zero, then iterating over that filtered set with SUMX to multiply Quantity by UnitPrice row by row and sum the results - note that the discount itself is not deducted, so this is gross revenue on discounted lines, not the discounted revenue amount. Any external filter context from slicers or report visuals still applies on top of the FILTER, so the measure will respect selections on date, product, or region as expected. The key watch-out is that lines with a DiscountPct of exactly zero are excluded, so if your data uses NULL or BLANK instead of zero to represent "no discount," those rows will also be silently excluded, potentially understating the result.

dax: Total Units Discounted Lines = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[DiscountPct] > 0 ), Sales[Quantity] )
explanation: This measure totals the quantity of units sold exclusively on lines where a discount was applied, giving you a sense of how much volume is tied to discounted activity. It works by first using FILTER to reduce the Sales table to only rows where DiscountPct is greater than zero, then iterating over that filtered set with SUMX to accumulate the Quantity values - all within whatever filter context is active from slicers, visuals, or other measures. Because FILTER operates on the physical Sales table row by row, this will respect any external filter context already applied (e.g., a date slicer), so the result correctly scopes to discounted lines within the current selection. The key watch-out is that a DiscountPct of exactly zero and a blank/null DiscountPct are treated differently - blank does *not* satisfy > 0, so lines with a missing discount value are correctly excluded, but if your data uses zero as a placeholder for "no discount entered" versus a genuine zero-percent discount, you may be understating or overstating discounted volume depending on data quality.

dax: Total Revenue Large Orders = SUMX ( FILTER ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) > 100 ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes total revenue, but only from orders where the total quantity sold exceeds 100 units - effectively isolating "large orders" as a revenue segment. It works by first using VALUES to get the distinct order numbers visible in the current filter context, then FILTER iterates over those orders and uses CALCULATE to evaluate each order's total quantity in isolation, keeping only those above 100; for each qualifying order, the outer CALCULATE then computes line-level revenue by multiplying Quantity by UnitPrice and summing across all rows belonging to that order. The key watch-out is that the inner CALCULATE inside FILTER inherits the row context transition from the iterator, so each order number acts as an implicit filter - this relies on Sales[OrderNumber] being a proper key that correctly partitions rows, and if your model has a separate Orders table with a relationship, you'd likely get cleaner, more predictable results by filtering there rather than iterating directly over a column in the fact table.

dax: Count Orders Above Avg Revenue = COUNTX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), IF ( CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) > [Avg Order Revenue], 1, BLANK () ) )
explanation:
This measure counts how many distinct order numbers have a total revenue greater than the average order revenue. It iterates over each unique order number using COUNTX with VALUES, and for each one, CALCULATE shifts the filter context so that SUMX computes revenue only for that specific order - returning 1 if it exceeds the [Avg Order Revenue] measure, or BLANK to effectively exclude it from the count. The result tells you how many orders are "above average," which is useful for distribution analysis or identifying high-value transactions.

The key watch-out is that [Avg Order Revenue] is re-evaluated in the outer filter context on every iteration, not locked to the per-order row context - so if that measure is sensitive to external slicers or filters, you may unintentionally be comparing each order against a moving average rather than the overall average, producing results that shift unexpectedly when the report is filtered. If a stable, context-independent baseline is needed, you should wrap [Avg Order Revenue] in ALL() or compute it explicitly with ALLSELECTED() depending on your intent.

dax: Avg Margin Pct Per Line = AVERAGEX ( Sales, DIVIDE ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost], Sales[UnitPrice] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average margin percentage at the individual sales line level, giving each transaction equal weight regardless of its revenue size. It iterates row by row over the Sales table, computes the margin percentage for each line as (UnitPrice − UnitCost) / UnitPrice, then returns the simple arithmetic mean of those per-line percentages within the current filter context. The key watch-out is that this is a unit-weighted average, not a revenue-weighted one - a high-volume, low-margin product and a single low-volume, high-margin line contribute equally to the result, which can produce a very different answer than dividing total margin dollars by total revenue; make sure stakeholders understand which averaging method the business actually wants.

dax: Total Revenue Premium Products = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, RELATED ( Product[UnitPrice] ) > 500 ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue generated exclusively from "premium" products, defined as those with a unit price above $500. It works by first using FILTER to walk every row in the Sales table and calling RELATED to pull each row's corresponding unit price from the Product dimension, keeping only rows where that lookup exceeds 500; SUMX then iterates over that filtered set and multiplies each sale's quantity by its own unit price to accumulate the total. The critical watch-out is that the price threshold is applied against Product[UnitPrice] (the dimension attribute) but revenue is calculated using Sales[UnitPrice] (the fact column) - if these two columns ever diverge due to discounting, overrides, or data quality issues, a product could pass the premium filter yet contribute non-premium pricing to the result, silently distorting the figure.

dax: Total Revenue Category A = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, RELATED ( Product[Category] ) = "A" ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue - quantity multiplied by unit price - but restricted to products belonging to Category "A" only. It works by iterating row-by-row over a filtered subset of the Sales table, where FILTER walks every row in the current filter context and RELATED performs a lookup across the relationship to the Product table to check the category, then SUMX multiplies and accumulates the revenue for matching rows. The key watch-out is that FILTER here evaluates the entire Sales table within the existing filter context, which can be expensive on large fact tables - a more performant alternative would be to use CALCULATE with a category filter, letting the engine leverage the data model's relationship and columnstore indexes rather than row-by-row evaluation; also, if the Sales-to-Product relationship is inactive or missing, RELATED will return blank and silently exclude all rows, making the measure return zero with no error.

dax: Avg Discount Pct Per Order = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( AVERAGEX ( Sales, Sales[DiscountPct] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average discount percentage per order, treating each order as a single unit rather than letting high-volume orders dominate the result - effectively an average of averages at the order level. It works in two passes: the outer AVERAGEX iterates over each distinct OrderNumber, and for each one CALCULATE shifts the filter context so the inner AVERAGEX computes the mean DiscountPct across only the rows belonging to that order; those per-order averages are then averaged together. The key watch-out is that this gives equal weight to every order regardless of how many line items it contains, so a one-line order and a hundred-line order contribute equally to the final result - if your business logic requires line-item-weighted discounts, this measure will silently give you the wrong answer.

dax: Total Net Revenue By Customer = SUMX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * ( 1 - Sales[DiscountPct] ) ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total net revenue - quantity times unit price after discount - aggregated across all customers in the current filter context. It works by first using VALUES to get the distinct list of CustomerKeys visible in context, then iterating over each one with SUMX; for each customer, CALCULATE re-establishes a row context-to-filter context transition so that the inner SUMX correctly computes that customer's net revenue before summing everything up. In practice, the outer SUMX/VALUES pattern produces the same numeric result as a single flat SUMX over Sales in most scenarios, so its value is really in scenarios where you need per-customer granularity locked in before aggregating - such as when a measure branch or security filter could otherwise cause double-counting or incorrect context. The key watch-out is that if CustomerKey has a many-to-many or ambiguous relationship to the Sales table, the CALCULATE inside the loop may not filter Sales rows as expected, so confirm that a clean one-to-many relationship exists from Customer to Sales before relying on this measure.

dax: Max Discount Pct Per Order = MAXX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( MAX ( Sales[DiscountPct] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest discount percentage applied to any individual order in the current filter context - useful for spotting outlier pricing or policy breaches. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct order numbers visible in context, then iterating over each one with MAXX; inside each iteration CALCULATE implicitly filters to that order, and MAX picks the largest discount on that order's rows, with MAXX then returning the greatest of those per-order maximums. The critical watch-out is that because VALUES respects the existing filter context, any slicer or visual filter on, say, a product or region will reduce which orders appear - so the result is always "max discount among orders that contain at least one row matching the current filters," not necessarily the true maximum across the whole order if some of its rows are filtered out.

dax: Total Revenue Single Line Orders = SUMX ( FILTER ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( COUNTROWS ( Sales ) ) = 1 ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue exclusively from orders that contain exactly one line item, giving the business a way to analyse single-SKU or simple transactions separately from multi-line orders. It works by first using VALUES to get the distinct list of order numbers in the current filter context, then FILTER iterates that list and uses CALCULATE(COUNTROWS(Sales)) to check - one order at a time - whether that order has only one row in the Sales table; for qualifying orders, the inner CALCULATE(SUMX(...)) computes Quantity × UnitPrice and SUMX accumulates the total. The key watch-out is that this measure assumes one row per line item in the Sales table - if your model ever has pre-aggregated data where a single row represents multiple lines, or if Sales contains header-level rows alongside detail rows, the COUNTROWS = 1 check will produce misleading results.

dax: Avg Cost Per Customer = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes the average total cost spent per customer - in other words, it first figures out what each individual customer cost in aggregate, then averages those per-customer totals across however many customers exist in the current filter context. It does this by iterating over each distinct CustomerKey using AVERAGEX, and for each customer, CALCULATE shifts the row context into a filter context so that SUMX can correctly sum that customer's quantity-times-unit-cost line items. The result is a true per-customer average of total cost, not a simple average of line-level costs - so a customer with ten transactions contributes one aggregated value to the average, not ten. The key watch-out is that VALUES returns only the customers visible in the current filter context, so if your report filters to a specific region or date range, customers with zero sales in that context are excluded from the denominator entirely, which can make the average look higher or lower than expected depending on whether partial-period or inactive customers should logically be included.

dax: Total Margin High Volume Customers = SUMX ( FILTER ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) > 500 ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates total margin contributed exclusively by high-volume customers - those whose total sold quantity across the current filter context exceeds 500 units. It works by first using VALUES to get the distinct customer keys visible in context, then FILTER iterates over them and uses CALCULATE to evaluate each customer's total quantity in isolation, keeping only those above 500; for each qualifying customer, a nested CALCULATE with SUMX computes margin as (UnitPrice minus UnitCost) times Quantity, and SUMX at the outer level sums those margins together. The key watch-out is that the 500-unit threshold is evaluated within the existing filter context - meaning a customer might qualify on a filtered page (e.g., one region or one year) but not globally, so the "high volume" designation is relative and dynamic rather than a stable customer segment, which can cause confusion or inconsistency if stakeholders expect a fixed classification.

dax: Max Margin Per Product = MAXX ( VALUES ( Product[ProductKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, ( Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[UnitCost] ) * Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest total margin earned by any one product in the current filter context - useful for spotting your most profitable SKU at a glance. It works by first using VALUES to build a one-column table of distinct ProductKeys visible in the current context, then iterating over each one with MAXX; for each row CALCULATE shifts the filter context to isolate that product, and SUMX computes its total margin by multiplying (UnitPrice minus UnitCost) by Quantity across every matching sales row before MAXX picks the largest result. The key watch-out is that VALUES respects the existing filter context, so if a slicer or visual already filters to a subset of products the measure returns the max within that subset - this is usually desirable, but if ProductKey has referential integrity gaps (sales rows with no matching product record), those orphan rows are silently excluded from the iteration and could cause the measure to understate true margin.

dax: Total Revenue Top Customers = SUMX ( TOPN ( 10, VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ), DESC ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure returns the total revenue contributed by the top 10 customers, ranked by their individual revenue within whatever filter context is active (e.g., a selected year, region, or product). It works by first using TOPN to identify the 10 customers with the highest revenue - evaluating each customer's revenue via an inner CALCULATE that transitions the row context from VALUES into a filter - then SUMX iterates over that 10-customer table and re-evaluates each customer's revenue a second time to produce the grand total. The double calculation of revenue (once to rank, once to sum) is intentional but adds overhead, so on large datasets consider materialising the customer revenue as a base measure to avoid redundant computation. The key watch-out is that TOPN breaks ties arbitrarily: if the 10th and 11th customers have identical revenue, the result can change non-deterministically across refreshes or query engines, so always verify tie-handling behaviour in your specific data before relying on this for contractual or incentive reporting.

dax: Total Extended Price With Tax = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] * ( 1 + Sales[TaxRate] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the total tax-inclusive revenue across all sales transactions by multiplying each line's quantity by its unit price and grossing it up by that line's own tax rate. It works by iterating row-by-row over the Sales table using SUMX, performing the arithmetic at the individual transaction level before aggregating, which correctly handles situations where tax rates differ across rows. Any filters active in the report - slicers, visual filters, or relationships - will automatically narrow the Sales table before the iteration begins, so the measure respects all filter context naturally. The key watch-out is that this approach assumes TaxRate is stored as a decimal (e.g., 0.10 for 10%); if it's stored as a whole number (e.g., 10), the formula will massively overstate revenue, so validate that column's formatting and values before trusting the output.

dax: Avg Extended Cost Per Product = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Product[ProductKey] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average total extended cost across all products in the current filter context - in other words, it first computes each product's total cost (quantity × unit cost summed across all its sales rows), then averages those per-product totals. It does this by iterating over the distinct product keys visible in the current filter context using AVERAGEX, and for each product, CALCULATE scopes the inner SUMX to that individual product's sales rows, producing one cost total per product before averaging them. The key watch-out is that this is an average of per-product totals, not an average of individual transaction costs - so a product with one $1,000 sale counts equally to a product with 10,000 small sales, which can produce a misleading result if your products vary wildly in sales volume. Also ensure Sales[UnitCost] is stored at the transaction level; if it's a dimension attribute resolved via a relationship, you may need to reference it differently to avoid incorrect row context.

dax: Total Revenue Negative Discount = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[DiscountPct] < 0 ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue exclusively from sales transactions where a negative discount percentage was recorded - effectively rows where a pricing anomaly, data entry error, or premium surcharge resulted in a sub-zero discount. It works by first using FILTER to scan the Sales table within the current filter context and isolate only rows where DiscountPct is negative, then iterating over that reduced table with SUMX to multiply Quantity by UnitPrice and sum the results. Note that the revenue calculated here ignores the DiscountPct value itself - it reflects gross line revenue on those anomalous rows, not a net or adjusted figure - so if your intent is to understand the financial impact of the negative discounts, you'd need to incorporate DiscountPct into the expression. Also be aware that if negative discounts are legitimately rare, this measure will return BLANK in most filter contexts, which can cause unexpected behaviour in visuals or downstream calculations.

dax: Count Customers With Multiple Orders = COUNTX ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), IF ( CALCULATE ( DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[OrderNumber] ) ) > 1, 1, BLANK () ) )
explanation: This measure counts the number of customers who have placed more than one distinct order. It iterates over each unique CustomerKey in the current filter context, and for each one uses CALCULATE to transition that row context into a filter context, counting the distinct orders belonging to that customer - returning 1 if the count exceeds one, BLANK otherwise, with COUNTX tallying only the non-blank results. The key watch-out is that COUNTX ignores BLANKs naturally, so the IF/BLANK pattern is doing the heavy lifting for filtering, but if your Sales table has duplicate OrderNumbers within a single customer (e.g., due to order line granularity), DISTINCTCOUNT is essential here - swapping it for COUNT would inflate the order tally and falsely qualify customers as multi-order buyers.

dax: Total Revenue New Customers = SUMX ( FILTER ( VALUES ( Customer[CustomerKey] ), CALCULATE ( MIN ( 'Date'[Year] ) ) = MAX ( 'Date'[Year] ) ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates total revenue generated exclusively by customers who made their first-ever purchase within the current filter context's year - in other words, revenue from new customers only. It works by iterating over each customer key visible in the current context, and for each one it checks whether that customer's earliest purchase year (MIN of Year across all their sales) equals the report's current year (MAX of Year in context); only customers who pass that test contribute their sales revenue to the sum. The inner CALCULATE for MIN intentionally removes the outer row context's filter on the date table, so it looks back across all years to find the customer's true first purchase year.

The key watch-out is that MIN('Date'[Year]) inside CALCULATE transitions the row context to filter context but does not automatically remove the visual/report-level year filter on the Date table - meaning if your Date table is already filtered to a single year by a slicer or page filter, MIN and MAX will both resolve to that same year, and virtually every customer will appear "new," severely inflating the result. To be safe, the MIN leg should use ALL or ALLSELECTED on the Date table to guarantee it's scanning the full date history regardless of external filters.

dax: Avg Order Size By Region = AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Geography[Region] ), CALCULATE ( AVERAGEX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) ) ) )
explanation: This measure computes the average order size (in quantity) across regions, where each region's contribution is itself the average total quantity per distinct order - in other words, it gives you a simple average of regional averages, not a quantity-weighted one. The outer AVERAGEX iterates over each region in the current filter context, and for each region the inner CALCULATE + AVERAGEX iterates over every distinct order number within that region, summing the quantity lines per order and then averaging those order totals; the result is then averaged again across all regions. Because this is an unweighted average of averages, a region with two orders will carry exactly the same weight as a region with two thousand orders, which can produce a misleading overall figure if regional order volumes differ significantly. Also be aware that if your model has a many-to-many or ambiguous relationship between Geography and Sales, the CALCULATE inside the outer AVERAGEX may not correctly isolate each region's rows, so validate that the Geography-to-Sales relationship filters as expected.

dax: Total Revenue Weekend Orders = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, RELATED ( 'Date'[IsWeekend] ) = TRUE () ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue generated exclusively from orders that fell on weekend dates. It iterates row by row over the Sales table, first using FILTER to retain only rows where the related Date table's IsWeekend flag is TRUE, then multiplying Quantity by UnitPrice for each qualifying row and summing the results. The outer report filter context still applies, so slicers or page filters on date, region, or product will further narrow the rows before FILTER does its work. The critical watch-out is that RELATED requires a valid many-to-one relationship from Sales to the Date table on the date key - if that relationship is inactive, missing, or the IsWeekend column is unpopulated for certain dates, those rows will silently drop out, understating revenue with no error to alert you.

dax: Max Revenue Per Region = MAXX ( VALUES ( Geography[Region] ), CALCULATE ( SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the single highest total revenue figure across all regions - in other words, it answers "which region sold the most, and what was that amount?" It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct region names, then iterating over each row with MAXX; on every iteration CALCULATE shifts the filter context so that SUMX multiplies quantity by unit price only for that region's sales, and MAXX retains whichever result is largest. The key watch-out is that this measure is sensitive to whatever external filter context already exists (e.g., a slicer on year or product), so the "max region" it surfaces can change across report visuals - if you need an absolute all-time max regardless of other slicers, you'll need to wrap the inner CALCULATE with additional REMOVEFILTERS or ALL calls on those dimensions.

dax: Total Cost Single Unit Lines = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] = 1 ), Sales[UnitCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total unit cost for sales transactions where exactly one unit was sold, giving you a view of single-unit order line spend - useful for identifying low-volume, potentially high-touch transactions. It works by first filtering the Sales table down to rows where Quantity equals 1, then iterating over those rows and summing the UnitCost column, all within whatever filter context is already applied (date slicers, region filters, etc.). Note that this measure reads UnitCost directly from the Sales table rather than a related Products table, so if UnitCost varies row-by-row (e.g., due to discounts or data quality issues), those actual row-level values are used - and if UnitCost is blank on any matching row, those rows silently contribute nothing to the sum.

dax: Total Revenue Excluding Discounted = SUMX ( FILTER ( Sales, Sales[DiscountPct] = 0 ), Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] )
explanation: This measure calculates total revenue from full-price transactions only, deliberately excluding any sales row where a discount was applied. It works by first filtering the Sales table down to rows where the discount percentage is exactly zero, then iterating over that reduced set to multiply Quantity by Unit Price on each row and sum the results - all within whatever filter context is active on the report (slicers, row/column filters, etc.). The key watch-out is that the zero-equality check is strict: rows with a NULL or blank DiscountPct will not match = 0 in DAX (blanks and zero are treated differently), so if your data has missing discount values that should logically mean "no discount," those rows will be silently excluded and your revenue figure will be understated - you'd need to wrap the condition with || ISBLANK(Sales[DiscountPct]) to capture them.
